We need to compare the equation {tex}{\text{1}} \cdot x - \frac{y}{5} - 10 = 0{/tex} with the general equation ax + by + c = 0, to get the values of a, b and c.

Therefore, we can conclude that {tex}a = 1,b = - \frac{1}{5}{\text{ and }}c = - 10{/tex}

Q 4

Express the linear equation -2x + 3y = 6 in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the value of a, b and c in case.

Solution

We need to express the linear equation {tex} - 2x + 3y = 6{/tex} in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the values of a, b and c.
{tex} - 2x + 3y = 6{\text{ can also be written as }} - 2x + 3y - 6 = 0.{/tex}
We need to compare the equation {tex} - 2x + 3y - 6 = 0{/tex} with the general equation ax + by + c = 0, to get the values of a, b and c.
Therefore, we can conclude that {tex}a = - 2,b = 3{\text{ and }}c = - 6{/tex}

Q 5

Express the linear equation x = 3y in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the value of a, b and c in case.

Solution


We need to express the linear equation {tex}x = 3y{/tex}  in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the values of a, b and c

{tex}x = 3y{\text{ can also be written as }}x - 3y + 0 = 0.{/tex}
We need to compare the equation {tex}x - 3y + 0 = 0{/tex} with the general equation ax + by + c = 0, to get the values of a, b and c.
Therefore, we can conclude that {tex}a = 1,b = - 3{\text{ and }}c = 0{/tex}

Q 6

Express the linear equation 2x = -5y in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the value of a, b and c in case.

Solution

We need to express the linear equation {tex}2x=-5y{/tex} in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the values of a, b and c.
{tex}2x = - 5y{\text{ can also be written as }}2x + 5y + 0 = 0.{/tex}
We need to compare the equation {tex}2x + 5y + 0 = 0{/tex} with the general equation ax + by + c = 0, to get the values of a, b and c.
Therefore, we can conclude that {tex}a = 2,b = 5{\text{ and }}c = 0{/tex}

Q 7

Express the linear equation 3x + 2 = 0 in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the value of a, b and c in case.

Solution

We need to express the linear equation 3x + 2 = 0 in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the values of a, b and c.
{tex}3x + 2 = 0{/tex} can also be written as 3x + 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}y + 2 = 0.
We need to compare the equation 3x + 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}y + 2 = 0 with the general equation ax + by + c = 0, to get the values of a, b and c.
Therefore, we can conclude that a = 3, b = 0 and c = 2

Q 8

Express the linear equation y - 2 = 0 in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the value of a, b and c in case.

Solution

We need to express the linear equation y - 2 = 0 in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the values of a, b and c.
y - 2 = 0 can also be written as 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}x + 1{tex}\cdot{/tex}y - 2 = 0.
We need to compare the equation 0{tex}\cdot{/tex}x + 1{tex}\cdot{/tex}y - 2 = 0 with the general equation ax + by + c = 0, to get the values of a, b and c.
Therefore, we can conclude that a = 0, b = 1 and c = -2

Q 9

Express the linear equation 5 = 2x in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the value of a, b and c in case.

Solution

We need to express the linear equation 5 = 2x in the form ax + by + c = 0 and indicate the values of a, b and c.
5 = 2x can also be written as -2x + 0{tex} \cdot {/tex}y + 5 = 0.
We need to compare the equation {tex} - 2x + 0 \cdot y + 5 = 0{/tex} with the general equation ax + by + c = 0, to get the values of a, b and c.
Therefore, we can conclude that a = -2, b = 0 and c = 5

sections.label: Exercise 4.2
sections.html:
Q 1

Which one of the options is true, and why? y = 3x + 5 has

Q 2

Write four solutions of the equation: 2x + y = 7

Solution

2x + y = 7
⇒ y = 7 – 2x
Put x = 0, we get y = 7 – 2(0) = 7 – 0 = 7
Put x = 1, we get y = 7 – 2(1) = 7 – 2 = 5
Put x = 2, we get y = 7 – 2(2) = 7 – 4 = 3
Put x = 3, we get y = 7 – 2(3) = 7 – 6 = 1
∴ Four solutions are (0, 7), (1, 5), (2, 3) and (3, 1).

Q 3

Write four solutions of the equation: {tex}\pi{/tex}x + y = 9

Solution

{tex}\pi{/tex}x + y = 9
⇒ y = 9 – {tex}\pi{/tex}x
Put x = 0, we get y = 9 – {tex}\pi{/tex}(0) = 9 – 0 = 9
put x = 1, we get y = 9 – {tex}\pi{/tex}(1) = 9 – {tex}\pi{/tex}
Put x = –1, we get y = 9 – {tex}\pi{/tex}( –1 ) = 9 + {tex}\pi{/tex}
Put x = {tex}\frac{9}{\pi }{/tex}, we get {tex}y = 9 - \pi \left( {\frac{9}{\pi }} \right) = 9 - 9 = 0{/tex}
∴ Four solutions are (0, 9), (1, 9 – {tex}\pi{/tex}), (–1, 9 + {tex}\pi{/tex}) and {tex}\left( {\frac{9}{\pi },0} \right){/tex}.

Q 4

Write four solutions of the equation: x = 4y.

Solution

x = 4y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{x}{4}{/tex}
put x = 0, we get y = {tex}\frac{0}{4}{/tex} = 0
Put x = 4, we get y = {tex}\frac{4}{4}{/tex} = 1
Put x = –4, we get y = {tex}\frac{-4}{4}{/tex} = -1
Put x = 2, we get y = {tex}\frac{2}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Four solutions are (0, 0), (4, 1), (–4, –1) and (2, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}).

Q 5

Find whether (0, 2) is the solution of the equation x – 2y = 4 or not?

Solution

The given equation is x – 2y = 4
Put x = 0 and y = 2 in given equation, we get
x – 2y = 4
0 – 2(2) = –4, which is not 4.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (0, 2) is not a solution of given equation.

Q 6

Find whether (2, 0) is the solution of the equation x – 2y = 4 or not?

Solution

x-2y=4

Put x = 2 and y = 0 in given equation, we get

x – 2y = 2 – 2(0) = 2 – 0 = 2, which is not 4.
∴ (2, 0) is not a solution of given equation.

Q 7

Find whether(4, 0) is the solution of the equation x – 2y = 4 or not?

Solution

x-2y=4

Put x = 4 and y = 0 in given equation, we get
x – 2y = 4 – 2(0) = 4
∴ (4, 0) is a solution of given equation.

Q 8

Find whether {tex}(\sqrt{2},4\sqrt{2}){/tex} is the solution of the equation x – 2y = 4 or not?

Solution

x-2y=4

Put x = {tex} \sqrt{2}{/tex} , y = {tex}4 \sqrt{2}{/tex} in given equation, we get
 {tex}\sqrt{2}-2(4\sqrt{2})=\sqrt{2}-8\sqrt{2}=-7\sqrt{2}{/tex}

which is not 4.
∴ {tex}(\sqrt2,4\sqrt2){/tex} is not a solution of given equation.

Q 9

Find whether (1, 1) is the solution of the equation x – 2y = 4 or not?

Solution

Put x = 1 and y = 1 in given equation, we get
x – 2y = 1 – 2(1) = 1 – 2 = –1, which is not 4.
∴ (1, 1) is not a solution of given equation.

Q 10

Find the value of k, if x = 2, y = 1 is a solution of the equation 2x + 3y =k.

Solution

Given linear equation is
2x+3y=k
take x=2 & y=1 then,
 2(2)+3(1)
=4+3
=7
so, k=7

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1

In the given figure, if A, B and C are three points on a line and B lies between A and C, then prove that AB + BC = AC.

Solution


In the given figure, AC coincides with AB + BC.
Also, Euclid’s axiom 4 says that things which coincide with one another, are equal to one another. So, it can be deduced that
AB + BC = AC

Q 2

Prove that an equilateral triangle can be constructed on any given line segment.

Solution

In the statement above, a line segment of any length is given, say AB [see Fig. (i)].

Here, we need to do some construction. Using Euclid’s Postulate 3, we can draw a circle with point A as the centre and AB as the radius [see Fig. (ii)]. Similarly, draw another circle with point B as the centre and BA as the radius. The two circles meet at a point, say C. Then, draw the line segments AC and BC to form {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC [see Fig. (iii)].
Therefore, we have to prove that this triangle is equilateral, i.e., AB = AC = BC.
Now, AB = AC, because they are the radii of the same circle ...(1)
Similarly, AB = BC (Radii of the same circle) ...(2)
From these two facts, and Euclid’s axiom that things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another, we can conclude that AB = BC = AC. Therefore, {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC is an equilateral triangle.

Hence,proved.

sections.label: Exercise 5.1
sections.html:
Q 1

Only one line can pass through a single point.

Q 2

There are an infinite number of lines which pass through two distinct points.

Q 3

A terminated line can be produced indefinitely on both the sides.

Q 4

If two circles are equal, then their radii are equal.

Q 5

In Fig., if AB = PQ and PQ = XY, then AB = XY.

Q 6

Define : Parallel lines

Solution

Lines which do not intersect anywhere are called parallel lines.

Q 7

Define : Perpendicular lines

Solution

Two lines which are at a right angles to each other are called perpendicular lines.

Q 8

Define : line segment

Solution

A line segment is a part of a line with two end points and it cannot be extended further. It has a definite length or breadth.

Q 9

Define : radius of a circle

Solution

The length of the line-segment joining the centre of a circle to any point on its circumference is called the raduis of the circle.

Q 10

Define : Square

Solution

A quadrilateral with all the four sides equal and all the four angles of measure 90o each is called a square.

Q 11

Consider two postulates given below:

  1. Given any two distinct points A and B, there exists a third point C which is in between A and B.
  2. There exist at least three points that are not on the same line.

Do these postulates contain any undefined terms? Are these postulates consistent? Do they follow from Euclid’s postulates? Explain.

Solution
  1. Point C is lying in between and on the line segment joining A and B as shown in below fig
  2. Yes, this postulate contains undefined terms, which are point and angle.
    Point C does not lie on the line segment joining A and B.

    No, they don't follow from Euclid's postulates. This follow an axiom, stated as given two distinct points; there is a unique line that passes through them.
Q 12

If a point C lies between two points A and B such that AC = BC, then prove that AC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AB. Explain by drawing the figure.

Solution


Given, AC = BC
AC + AC = BC + AC . . . . [AC are added to both the side]
2AC = AB . . . . [BC + AC coincides with AB]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AB

Q 13

Point C is called a mid point of line segment AB, prove that every line segment has one and only one mid-point.

Solution


Let a line AB have two mid-points, say, C and D. Then
AB = AC + CB = 2AC . . . . (i) . . . [As C is the mid-point of AB]
and AB = AD + DB = 2AD . . . . (ii) [As D is the mid-point of AB]
From equation (i) and (ii)
AC = AD and CB = DB
But this will possible only when D lies on point C. So every line segment has one and only one mid-point.

Q 14

In fig., if AC = BD, then prove that AB = CD

Solution

AC = BD . . . . [Given] . . . (1)
AC = AB + BC . . . . [Point B lies between A and C] . . . . (2)
BD = BC + CD . . . . [Point C lies between B and D] . . . . (3)
Substituting (2) and (3) in (1), we get
AB + BC = BC + CD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = CD . . . . [Subtracting equals from equals]

Q 15

Why is Axiom 5, in the list of Euclid's axioms, considered a universal truth?

Solution

Euclid's Axiom 5 states that "The whole is greater than the part. Since this is true for anything in any part of the world. So, this is a universal truth.

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1

In figure lines, PQ and RS intersect each other at point O. If {tex} \angle POR:\angle ROQ{/tex} = 5 : 7, find all the angles.

Solution

{tex}\angle POR + \angle ROQ = {180^0}{/tex} [linear pair]
But, {tex} \angle {\text{POR : }}\angle ROQ{/tex} = 5 : 7 [Given]
{tex}\therefore {\text{ }}\angle {\text{ POR = }}\frac{5}{{12}} \times {\text{18}}{{\text{0}}^0}{\text{ = 7}}{{\text{5}}^0}{\text{ }}{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}\angle {\text{ROQ = }}\frac{7}{{12}} \times {\text{18}}{{\text{0}}^0}{\text{ = 10}}{{\text{5}}^0}{/tex}
Now {tex}\angle {\text{POS = }}\angle {\text{ROQ = 10}}{{\text{5}}^0}{/tex} [vertically opposite angle]
And {tex}\angle {\text{SOQ = }}\angle {\text{POR = 7}}{{\text{5}}^0}{/tex} [vertically opposite angle]

Q 2

In figure, ray OS stands on a line POQ. Ray OR and ray OT are angle bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}POS and {tex}\angle{/tex}SOQ, respectively. If {tex}\angle{/tex}POS = x, find {tex}\angle{/tex}ROT.

Solution

Ray OS stands on the line POQ
{tex} \therefore {\text{ }}\angle {\text{POS + }}\angle {\text{SOQ = 18}}{{\text{0}}^0}{/tex}
But {tex}\angle{/tex}PQS = X
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + {tex}\angle{/tex}SOQ = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}SOQ = 180° - x
Now ray OR bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}POS,
Therefore {tex} \angle {\text{ROS = }}\frac{1}{2} \times \angle POS{/tex} {tex} {\text{ = }}\frac{1}{2} \times x = \frac{x}{2}{/tex}
Similarly, {tex} \angle {\text{ SOT = }}\frac{1}{2} \times \angle SOQ{/tex} {tex} {\text{ = }}\frac{1}{2} \times ({180^0} - x) = {90^ \circ } - \frac{x}{2}{/tex}
{tex} \angle ROT = \angle ROS + \angle SOT{/tex} {tex} {\text{ = }}\frac{x}{2} + {90^ \circ } - \frac{x}{2} = {90^0}{/tex}

Q 3

In the given figure, OP, OQ, OR and OS are four rays. Prove that
{tex}\angle{/tex}POQ + {tex}\angle{/tex}ROQ + {tex}\angle{/tex}SOR + {tex}\angle{/tex}POS = 360°.

Solution

Let us produce a ray OQ backwards to a point M, then MOQ is a straight line.
Now, OP is a ray on the line MOQ. Then, by linear pair axiom, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}MOP + {tex}\angle{/tex}POQ = 180o ......(i)

Similarly, OS is a ray on the line MOQ. Then, by linear pair axiom, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}MOS + {tex}\angle{/tex}SOQ = 180o ....(ii)
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}SOR and {tex}\angle{/tex}ROQ are adjacent angles.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}SOQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}SOR + {tex}\angle{/tex}ROQ ...(iii)
On putting the value of {tex}\angle{/tex}SOQ from Eq.(iii) in Eq.(ii), we get
{tex}\angle{/tex}MOS + {tex}\angle{/tex}SOR + {tex}\angle{/tex}ROQ = 180o ....(iv)
Now, on adding  Eqs.(i) and (iv), we get
{tex}\angle{/tex}MOP + {tex}\angle{/tex}POQ + {tex}\angle{/tex}MOS + {tex}\angle{/tex}SOR + {tex}\angle{/tex}ROQ = 180o + 180o 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}MOP + {tex}\angle{/tex}MOS + {tex}\angle{/tex}POQ + {tex}\angle{/tex}SOR + {tex}\angle{/tex}ROQ = 360o ....(iv)
But {tex}\angle{/tex}MOP + {tex}\angle{/tex}MOS = {tex}\angle{/tex}POS
Then, from Eq.(v), we get
{tex}\angle{/tex}POS + {tex}\angle{/tex}POQ + {tex}\angle{/tex}SOR + {tex}\angle{/tex}ROQ = 360o 
Hence proved.

Q 4

In Fig., if {tex}PQ \parallel RS, \angle MXQ=135^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\angle MYR=40^{\circ}{/tex}, find {tex}\angle XMY{/tex}.

Solution

Through point M draw a line AB parallel to the line PQ as shown in Fig. Thus, we have

AB || PQ and PQ || RS
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB || RS
Now, AB || PQ and {tex}\angle{/tex}QXM and {tex}\angle{/tex}XMB are interior angles on the same side of the transversal XM.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QXM + {tex}\angle{/tex}XMB = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 135o + {tex}\angle{/tex}XMB = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}XMB = 180o - 135o = 45o
Now, AB || RS and {tex}\angle{/tex}BMY and {tex}\angle{/tex}MYR are alternate angles.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BMY = {tex}\angle{/tex}MYR
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BMY = 40o
Hence, {tex}\angle{/tex}XMY = {tex}\angle{/tex}XMB + {tex}\angle{/tex}BMY = 45o + 40o = 85o

Q 5

If a transversal intersects two lines such that the bisectors of a pair of corresponding angles are parallel, then prove that the two lines are parallel.

Solution

Given AD is transversal intersect two lines PQ and RS
To prove PQ {tex}\parallel{/tex} RS
Proof: BE bisects ABQ
{tex}\angle 1=\angle A B E=\angle E B Q=\frac{1}{2} \angle A B Q{/tex} ...(i)
Similarity CG bisects {tex}\angle{/tex} BCS
{tex}\therefore \angle 2=\frac{1}{2} \angle B C S{/tex} ...(ii)
But BE {tex}\parallel{/tex} CG and AD is the transversal
{tex}\therefore {\text{ }}\angle {\text{1 = }}\angle {\text{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {\text{ }}\frac{1}{2}\angle ABQ = \frac{1}{2}\angle BCS{/tex} [by (i) and (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow {\text{ }}\angle {\text{ABQ = }}\angle {\text{BCS}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} corresponding angles are equal]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PQ {tex}\parallel{/tex} RS

Q 6

Fig., AB || CD and CD || EF. Also, EA {tex}\perp{/tex} AB. If {tex}\angle{/tex}BEF = 55°, find the values of x, y and z.

Solution

Since corresponding angles are equal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = y ... (i)
We know that the interior angles on the same side of the transversal are supplementary.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y + 55o = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 180o - 55o = 125o
So, x = y = 125o
Since AB || CD and CD || EF.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB || EF
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}FEA = 180o [{tex}\because{/tex} Interior angles on the same side of the transversal EA are supplementary]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 90o + z + 55o = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 35o

sections.label: Exercise 6.1
sections.html:
Q 1

In Figure lines AB and CD intersect at O. If {tex}\angle AOC + \angle BOE = {70^ \circ }{/tex} and {tex}\angle BOD = {40^ \circ }{/tex}, find {tex}\angle BOE{/tex} and reflex {tex}\angle COE.{/tex}

Solution

We are given that {tex}\angle{/tex}AOC + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE = 70° and {tex}\angle{/tex}BOD = 40°
We need to find {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE and reflex {tex}\angle{/tex}COE
From the given figure, we can conclude that {tex}\angle{/tex}AOE and {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE form a linear pair.
We know that sum of the angles of a linear pair is 180°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOE + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE = 180°
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOE = {tex}\angle{/tex}AOC + {tex}\angle{/tex} COE
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOC + {tex}\angle{/tex}COE + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE = 180°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOC + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE + {tex}\angle{/tex}COE = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 70° + {tex}\angle{/tex}COE = 180°
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}COE = 180° - 70°
= 110°
Reflex {tex}\angle{/tex}COE = 360° - {tex}\angle{/tex}COE
= 360° - 110°
= 250°
{tex}\angle{/tex}AOC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BOD (Vertically opposite angles), or
{tex}\angle{/tex}BOD + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE = 70
But, we are given that {tex}\angle{/tex}BOD = 40°.
 40° + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE = 70°
{tex}\angle{/tex}BOE = 70° - 40°
= 30°.
Therefore, we can conclude that  Reflex {tex}\angle{/tex}COE = 250° and {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE = 30°

Q 2

In fig lines XY and MN intersect at O. If {tex}\angle {/tex}POY = 90° and a : b = 2 : 3 find {tex}\angle {/tex}c.

Solution

In the given figure,
{tex}\angle{/tex}POY = 90o
{tex}\angle{/tex}POX + {tex}\angle{/tex}POY = 180o (Linear pair)
{tex}\angle{/tex}POX + 90o = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}POX = 90o
a : b = 2 : 3
Let a = 2xo
and b = 3xo
{tex}\angle{/tex}POX = a + b = 5x
90o = 5x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 18o
{tex}\angle{/tex}MOX = a = 2x = 36o
{tex}\angle{/tex}MOX + {tex}\angle{/tex}NOX = 180o (Linear pair)
a + c = 180o
36o + c = 180o
c = 180o - 36o = 144o

Q 3

In the given figure, {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}PRQ, then prove that {tex}\angle{/tex}PQS = {tex}\angle{/tex}PRT

Solution

We need to prove that {tex}\angle{/tex}PQS = {tex}\angle{/tex}PRT
We are given that {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}PRQ
From the given figure, we can conclude that {tex}\angle{/tex}PQS and {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR, and {tex}\angle{/tex}PRQ and {tex}\angle{/tex}PRT form a linear pair.
We know that sum of the angles of a linear pair is {tex}{180^\circ }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \angle PQS + \angle PQR = {180^\circ },{/tex} and ...(i)
{tex}\angle PRQ + \angle PRT = {180^\circ }.{/tex} ...(ii)
From equation (i) and (ii), we can conclude that
{tex}\angle PQS + \angle PQR = \angle PRQ + \angle PRT.{/tex}
But, {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = {tex}\angle{/tex}PRQ
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PQS = {tex}\angle{/tex}PRT
Hence, proved.

Q 4

In figure, if x + y = w + z, then prove that AOB is a line.

Solution

Given that, x + y = w + z  ... (1)
As the sum of all angles round a point is equal to 360o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + y + w + z = 360o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + y + x + y = 360o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2(x + y) = 360o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x+y={360^0\over2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + y = 180o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AOB is a line.

Q 5

In the figure, POQ is a line. Ray OR is perpendicular to line PQ. OS is another ray lying between rays OP and OR. Prove that {tex}\angle ROS={1\over2}(\angle QOS-\angle POS){/tex}

Solution


Ray OR is perpendicular to line PQ
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QOR = {tex}\angle{/tex}POR = 90o . . . .(1)
{tex}\angle{/tex}QOS = {tex}\angle{/tex}QOR + {tex}\angle{/tex}ROS. . . .(2)
{tex}\angle{/tex}POS = {tex}\angle{/tex}POR – {tex}\angle{/tex}ROS. . . .(3)
From (2) and (3),
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QOS – {tex}\angle{/tex}POS = ({tex}\angle{/tex}QOR – {tex}\angle{/tex}POR) + 2{tex}\angle{/tex}ROS = 2{tex}\angle{/tex}ROS . . . [Using (1)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle ROS={1\over2}(\angle QOS-\angle POS){/tex}

Q 6

If is given that {tex}\angle XYZ = {64^\circ }{/tex} and XY is produced to point P. Draw a figure from the given information. If ray YQ bisects {tex}\angle {/tex}ZYP, find {tex}\angle {/tex}XYQ and reflex {tex}\angle {/tex}QYP.

Solution

We are given that {tex}\angle{/tex}XYZ = 64o, XY is produced to P and YQ bisects {tex}\angle {/tex}ZYP We can conclude the given below figure for the given situation:

We need to find {tex}\angle {/tex}XYQ and reflex {tex}\angle {/tex}QYP
From the given figure, we can conclude that {tex}\angle {/tex}XYZ and {tex}\angle {/tex}ZYP form a linear pair.
We know that sum of the angles of a linear pair is 180o.
{tex}\angle {/tex}XYZ + {tex}\angle {/tex}ZYP = 180o
But {tex}\angle {/tex}XYZ = 64o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 64o + {tex}\angle {/tex}ZYP = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle {/tex}ZYP = 116o
Ray YQ bisects {tex}\angle {/tex}ZYP,or
{tex}\angle {/tex}QYZ = {tex}\angle {/tex}QYP = {tex}\frac{116^o}{2}{/tex} = 58o
{tex}\angle {/tex}XYQ = {tex}\angle {/tex}QYZ + {tex}\angle {/tex}XYZ
= 58o + 64o = 122o.
Reflex {tex}\angle {/tex}QYP = {tex}{360^\circ }{/tex} - {tex}\angle {/tex}QYP
= 360o - 58o
 = 302o.
Therefore, we can conclude that {tex}\angle {/tex}XYQ = 122o and Reflex {tex}\angle {/tex}QYP = 302o

sections.label: Exercise 6.2
sections.html:
Q 1

In figure, if AB || CD, CD || EF and y : z = 3 : 7, find x.

Solution


As AB || CD and CD || EF
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB || EF
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = z . . . . [Alternate interior angles] . . . (1)
x + y = 180o . . . (2)
z + y = 180o . . . [From (1) and (2)]
y : z = 3 : 7
Sum of the ratios = 3 + 7 = 10
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}y={3\over10}\times180^0=54^0\ and \ z={7\over10}\times180^0=126^0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = z = 126o

Q 2

In figure, if AB || CD, EF ⊥ CD and {tex}\angle{/tex}GED = 126o. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}AGE, {tex}\angle{/tex}GEF and {tex}\angle{/tex}FGE.

Solution

{tex}\angle{/tex}AGE = {tex}\angle{/tex}GED = 126o . . . [Alternate interior angles]
{tex}\angle{/tex}GED = 126o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}GEF + {tex}\angle{/tex}FED = 126o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}GEF + 90o = 126o . . . [As EF ⊥ CD {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}FED = 90o]
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\angle{/tex}GEF = 126o – 90o = 36o
{tex}\angle{/tex}GEC + {tex}\angle{/tex}GEF + {tex}\angle{/tex}FED = 180o
{tex}\angle{/tex}GEC + 36o + 90o = 180o
{tex}\angle{/tex}GEC + 126o = 180o
{tex}\angle{/tex}GEC = 180o – 126o = 54o
{tex}\angle{/tex}FGE = {tex}\angle{/tex}GEC = 54o . . . . [Alternate interior angles]

Q 3

In figure, if PQ || ST, {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = 110o and {tex}\angle{/tex}RST = 130o, find {tex}\angle{/tex}QRS.

Solution

Draw a line RU parallel to ST through point R.

{tex}\angle{/tex}RST + {tex}\angle{/tex}SRU = 180o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 130o + {tex}\angle{/tex}SRU = 180o
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\angle{/tex}SRU = 180o – 130o = 50o . . . (1)
{tex}\angle{/tex}QRU = {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR = 110o . . . . [Alternate interior angles]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QRS + {tex}\angle{/tex}SRU = 110o
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\angle{/tex}QRS + 50o = 110o . . . [Using (1)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QRS = 110o – 50o = 60o

Q 4

In the given figure, if AB || CD, {tex}\angle{/tex}APQ = 50° and {tex}\angle{/tex}PRD = 127°, find x and y.

Solution

We are given that {tex}AB\parallel CD,\angle APQ = {50^\circ }{/tex} and {tex}\angle{/tex}PRD = 127°
We need to find the value of x and y in the figure.
{tex}\angle{/tex}APQ = x = 50° (Alternate interior angles)
{tex}\angle{/tex}PRD = {tex}\angle{/tex}APR = 127° (Alternate interior angles)
{tex}\angle{/tex}APR = {tex}\angle{/tex}QPR + {tex}\angle{/tex}APQ.
127° = y + 50°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 77°.
Therefore, we can conclude that x = 50° and y = 77°
Alternatively, 127° = y + x (because exterior angle is equal to the sum of interior opposite angles).
so, 127° = y + 50°
which gives, x = 50° and y = 77°

Q 5

In figure, PQ and RS are two mirrors placed parallel to each other. An incident ray AB strikes the mirror PQ at B. The reflected ray moves along the path BC and strikes the mirror RS at C and again reflects back along CD. Prove that AB || CD.

Solution

Draw ray BL {tex}\perp{/tex} PQ and ray CM {tex}\perp{/tex} RS.

BL {tex}\perp{/tex} PQ, CM {tex}\perp{/tex} RS and PQ {tex}\parallel{/tex} RS
BL || CM
{tex}\angle{/tex}LBC = {tex}\angle{/tex}MCB . . . (1)
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABL = {tex}\angle{/tex}LBC . . . [Angle of incident = Angle of reflection] . . . .(2)
{tex}\angle{/tex}MCB = {tex}\angle{/tex}MCD . . . [Angle of incident = Angle of reflection] . . . . (3)
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABL = {tex}\angle{/tex}MCD . . . [From (1), (2) and (3)]
{tex}\angle{/tex}LBC + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABL = {tex}\angle{/tex}MCB + {tex}\angle{/tex}MCD . . . [Adding (1) and (3)]
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD are alternate interior angles and are equal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB || CD

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1

In Fig., OA = OB and OD = OC. Show that

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex} AOD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} BOC
  2. AD || BC

Solution
  1. You may observe that in {tex}\triangle{/tex} AOD and {tex}\triangle{/tex} BOC,
    OA = OB (Given)
    OD = OC
    Also, since {tex}\angle{/tex} AOD and {tex}\angle{/tex} BOC form a pair of vertically opposite angles,
    we have {tex}\angle{/tex} AOD = {tex}\angle{/tex} BOC
    So, {tex}\triangle{/tex} AOD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} BOC (by the SAS congruence rule)
  2. In congruent triangles AOD and BOC, the other corresponding parts are also equal.
    So, {tex}\angle{/tex} OAD = {tex}\angle{/tex} OBC and these form a pair of alternate angles for line segments AD and BC.
    Therefore, AD || BC
Q 2

AB is a line segment and line l is its perpendicular bisector. If a point P lies on l, show that P is equidistant from A and B.

Solution

Let C be the mid-point of AB.
Clearly, line l passes through C is perpendicular to AB.
In {tex}\triangle {/tex}PCA and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PCB, we have

AC = BC [{tex}\because{/tex} C is the mid-point of AB]
PC = PC [common side]
{tex}\angle P C A=\angle P C B{/tex} [Each equal to 90o as l {tex}\perp{/tex} AB]
So, by SAS congruence rule, we obtain
{tex}\triangle P C A \cong \triangle P C B{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PA = PB

Q 3

In Fig., line segment AB is parallel to another line segment CD. O is the mid-point of AD. Show that:

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}DOC
  2. O is also the mid-point of BC.

Solution
  1. Consider {tex}\triangle{/tex} AOB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DOC.
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ABO = {tex}\angle{/tex} DCO (Alternate angles as AB || CD and BC is the transversal)
    {tex}\angle{/tex} AOB = {tex}\angle{/tex} DOC (Vertically opposite angles)
    OA = OD (Given)
    Therefore, {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}DOC (AAS rule)
  2. OB = OC (CPCT)
    So, O is the mid-point of BC
Q 4

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, the bisector AD of {tex}\angle{/tex}A is perpendicular to side BC (See figure). Show that AB = AC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is isosceles.

Solution

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC,
BD = CD [AD bisects BC]
{tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = 90° [AD {tex}\bot{/tex} BC]
AD = AD [Common]
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD  {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle {/tex}ACD [By SAS congruency]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = AC [By C.P.C.T.]
Therefore, ABC is an isosceles triangle.

Q 5

E and F are respectively the mid-points of equal sides AB and AC of ∆ ABC (see Fig.). Show that BF = CE

Solution

In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABF and {tex}\triangle{/tex} ACE,
AB = AC (Given)
{tex}\angle{/tex} A = {tex}\angle{/tex} A (Common)
AF = AE (Halves of equal sides)
So, {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABF {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACE (SAS rule)
Therefore, BF = CE (CPCT)

Q 6

In an isosceles triangle ABC with AB = AC, D and E are points on BC such that BE = CD (see Fig.). Show that AD = AE

Solution


In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABD and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACE,
AB = AC (Given) ...(1)
{tex}\angle{/tex} B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C (Angles opposite to equal sides) ...(2)
Also, BE = CD
So, BE – DE = CD – DE
That is, BD = CE ...(3)
So, {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} ACE (Using (1), (2), (3) and SAS rule).
This gives AD = AE (CPCT)

Q 7

AB is a line segment. P and Q are points on opposite sides of AB such that each of them is equidistant from the points A and B (See Figure). Show that the line PQ is the perpendicular bisector of AB.

Solution

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAQ and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PBQ,
AP = BP (Given)
AQ = BQ (Given)
PQ = PQ (Common)
So, {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAQ {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PBQ (SSS rule)
Therefore, {tex}\angle{/tex}APQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}BPQ (CPCT).
Now let us consider {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PBC.
You have: AP = BP (Given)
{tex}\angle{/tex}APC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BPC ({tex}\angle{/tex}APQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}BPQ proved above)
PC = PC (Common)
So, {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PBC (SAS rule)
Therefore, AC = BC (CPCT) .......(i)
{tex}\angle{/tex}ACP = {tex}\angle{/tex}BCP (CPCT)
and {tex}\angle{/tex}ACP + {tex}\angle{/tex}BCP = 180o (Linear pair)
So, {tex}2\angle \mathrm{ACP}{/tex}= 180o
Or, {tex}\angle{/tex}ACP = 90o ......(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we can easily conclude that PQ is the perpendicular bisector of AB.

Q 8

P is a point equidistant from two lines l and m intersecting at point A (see figure). Show that the line AP bisects the angle between them.

Solution


Given: P is a point equidistant from two lines l and m intersect at a point A.  
To Prove: AP bisects the angle between them. i.e. {tex}\angle{/tex}PAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}PAC
Proof: Let PB and PC be perpendiculars from P on lines l and m respectively. Since P is equidistant from lines l and m.
Therefore, PB = PC
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}PAB and {tex}\Delta{/tex}PAC, we have
PB = PC [Given]
{tex}\angle{/tex}PBA = {tex}\angle{/tex}PCA [Each equal to 90o]
and, PA = PA [Common]
{tex}\triangle{/tex}PAB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAC [By RHS congruence criterion]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}PAC
Hence Proved

sections.label: Exercise 7.1
sections.html:
Q 1

In quadrilateral ABCD, AC = AD and AB bisects {tex}\angle {/tex}A (See figure). Show that {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex} \cong {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD. What can you say about BC and BD?

Solution

Given: In quadrilateral ABCD, AC = AD and AB bisects {tex}\angle {/tex}A.
To prove: {tex}\angle {/tex}ABC {tex} \cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD
Proof: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD,
AC = AD [Given]
{tex}\angle {/tex}BAC = {tex}\angle {/tex}BAD [{tex}\because {/tex} AB bisects {tex}\angle {/tex}A]
AB = AB [Common]
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex} \cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD [By SAS congruency]
Thus BC = BD [By C.P.C.T.]

Q 2

ABCD is a quadrilateral in which AD = BC and {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CBA. Prove that

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} BAC
  2. BD = AC
  3. {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC

Solution

In quadrilateral ACBD, we have AD = BC and {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CBA

  1. In ∆ ABC and ∆ BAC,
    AD = BC (Given)
    {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CBA (Given)
    AB = AB (Common)
    {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAC ... [By SAS Congruence]
  2. Since ∆ABD ≅ ∆BAC
    ⇒ BD = AC [By C.P.C.T.]
  3. Since ∆ABD ≅ ∆BAC
    ⇒ {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC [By C.P.C.T.]
Q 3

AD and BC are equal perpendiculars to a line segment AB. Show that CD bisects AB (See figure)

Solution

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BOC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOD,
{tex}\angle{/tex}OBC = {tex}\angle{/tex}OAD = 90° [Given]
{tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD [Vertically Opposite angles]
BC = AD [Given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BOC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOD [By ASA congruency]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OB = OA and OC = OD [By C.P.C.T.]

Q 4

l and m are two parallel lines intersected by another pair of parallel lines p and q. Show that {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}CDA.

Solution

Given : l || m and p || q
To prove : DABC {tex}\cong{/tex} DCDA
Proof : l || m and p || q . . . . [Given]
In DABC and DCDA
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DCA . . . . . [Alternate interior angles as AB || DC]
Similarly, {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD . . . [Alternate interior angles as BC || DA]
AC = AC . . . [Common]
DABC {tex}\cong{/tex} DCDA    [By ASA congruency]

Q 5

Line l is the bisector of an angle {tex}\angle{/tex}A and B is any point on l. BP and BQ are perpendicular from B to the arms of {tex}\angle{/tex}A.

Show that:

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQB
  2. BP = BQ or B is equidistant from the arms of {tex}\angle{/tex}A.
Solution
  1. In {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQB
    {tex}\angle{/tex}BAP = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAQ ...[As l bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}BPA = {tex}\angle{/tex}BQA ...[Each 90°]
    AB = AB ...[Common]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQB proved ...[AAS congruence] ...(1)
  2. {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQB ...[From (1)]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} BP = BQ ...[c.p.c.t.]
Q 6

In figure, AC = AE, AB = AD and {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}EAC. Show that BC = DE.

Solution

Given : AC = AE, AB = AD and {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}EAC.
To prove ; BC = DE
Proof : In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADE
AC = AE, AB = AD and ∠BAD = ∠EAC ...[Given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD + {tex}\angle{/tex}DAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DAC + {tex}\angle{/tex}EAC ...[Adding {tex}\angle{/tex}DAC to both sides]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DAE ...(1)
AC = AE ...[Given]
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DAE ...[From (1)]
AB = AD ...[Given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} DABC {tex}\cong{/tex} DADE ...[By SAS congruence]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = DE ...[c.p.c.t.]

Q 7

AB is a line segment and P is its mid-point. D and E are points on the same side of AB such that {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABE and {tex}\angle{/tex}EPA = {tex}\angle{/tex}DPB.
Show that:

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAP {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}EBP
  2. AD = BE
Solution

Given: AB is a line segment and P is its mid-point. D and E are points on the same side of AB such that {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABE and {tex}\angle{/tex}EPA = {tex}\angle{/tex}DPB.
To prove:

  1. DDAP {tex}\cong{/tex} DEBP
  2. AD = BE

Proof :(ii)
{tex}\angle{/tex}EPA = {tex}\angle{/tex}DPB... [Given]
{tex}\angle{/tex}EPA + {tex}\angle{/tex}EPD = {tex}\angle{/tex}EPD + {tex}\angle{/tex}DPB... [Adding {tex}\angle{/tex}EPD to both sides]
∠APD = ∠BPE... (1)
In DDAP and DEBP
{tex}\angle{/tex}DAP = {tex}\angle{/tex}EBP ...[Given]
AP = BP ...[As P is the mid-point of the line AB]
{tex}\angle{/tex}APD = {tex}\angle{/tex}BPE ...[From (1)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} DDAP {tex}\cong{/tex} DEBP proved ...[ASA Congruence]... (2)
(i) As DDAP {tex}\cong{/tex} DEBP... [From (2)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AD = BE ...[c.p.c.t.]

Q 8

In right triangle ABC, right angled at C, M is the mid-point of hypotenuse AB. C is joined to M and produced to a point D such that DM = CM. Point D is joined to point B. (See figure)

Show that:

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex}AMC {tex} \cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BMD
  2. {tex}\angle {/tex}DBC is a right angle.
  3. {tex}\triangle{/tex}DBC {tex} \cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACB
  4. CM = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} AB
Solution
  1. In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AMC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BMD,
    AM = BM [M is the mid-point of AB]
    {tex}\angle {/tex}AMC ={tex}\angle {/tex} BMD [Vertically opposite angles]
    CM = DM [Given]
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}AMC {tex} \cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BMD [By SAS congruency]
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\angle {/tex} ACM ={tex}\angle {/tex} BDM ...(i)
  2. For two lines AC and DB and transversal DC, we have,
    {tex}\angle {/tex}ACD = {tex}\angle {/tex}BDC [Alternate angles]
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} AC {tex}\parallel {/tex} DB
    Now for parallel lines AC and DB and for transversal BC.
    {tex}\angle {/tex} DBC = {tex}\angle {/tex} ACB [Alternate angles] ...(ii)
    But{tex} \Delta {/tex} ABC is a right angled triangle, right angled at C.
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\angle {/tex}ACB = 90° (iii)
    Therefore {tex}\angle {/tex} DBC = 90° [Using eq. (ii) and (iii)]
    {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}\angle {/tex} DBC is a right angle.
  3. Now in {tex}\triangle{/tex}DBC and {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC,
    DB = AC [Proved in part (i)]
    {tex}\angle {/tex} DBC = {tex}\angle {/tex}ACB = 90° [Proved in part (ii)]
    BC = BC [Common]
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}DBC {tex} \cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACB [By SAS congruency]
  4. Since {tex}\triangle{/tex}DBC {tex} \cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACB [Proved above]
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} DC = AB
    {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} AM + CM = AB
    {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} CM + CM = AB [{tex}\because {/tex} AM = CM]
    {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 2CM = AB
    {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} CM = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} AB

sections.label: Exercise 7.2
sections.html:
Q 1

In an isosceles triangle ABC, with AB = AC, the bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}B and {tex}\angle{/tex}C intersect each other at O. Join A to O. Show that OB = OC and AO bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A.

Solution

Given: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, AB = AC, the bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}B and {tex}\angle{/tex}C intersect each other at O.
Construction: Joint A to O

To prove: OB = OC and AO bisects A.
Proof : AB = AC . . . . [Given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C . . . [{tex}\angle{/tex}s opposite to equal side of a {tex}\triangle{/tex}]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}B =  {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}C
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ∠OBC = ∠OCB . . . [As BO bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}B and CO bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}C]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OB = OC . . . [Sides opposite to equal {tex}\angle{/tex}s of a {tex}\triangle{/tex} ]
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}OAB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}OAC,
AB = AC . . . .[Given]
OB = OC . . . .[As proved above]
OA = OA . . . .[Common]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}OAB {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}OAC . . . [By SSS property]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}OAC . . . [c.p.c.t.]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AO bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A

Q 2

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, AD is the perpendicular bisector of BC (see figure). Show that {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle in which AB = AC.

Solution

Given : AD ⊥ BC.
To prove : ABC is an isosceles triangle in which AB = AC.
Proof : In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC,
DB = DC . . . [As AD ⊥ bisector of BC]
∠ADB = ∠ADC . . . [Each 90o]
AD = AD . . .[Common]
{tex}\therefore \triangle \mathrm { ADB } \cong \triangle \mathrm { ADC }{/tex} . . .[By SAS property]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = AC . . .[c.p.c.t]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle in which AB = AC.

Q 3

ABC is an isosceles triangle in which altitudes BE and CF are drawn to equal sides AC and AB respectively (See figure). Show that these altitudes are equal.

 

Solution

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABE and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACF,
{tex}\angle {/tex}A = {tex}\angle {/tex} A [Common]
{tex}\angle {/tex}AEB = {tex}\angle {/tex}AFC = [90°]
AB = AC [Given]
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABE ≅ {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACF [By ASA congruency]
{tex}\therefore {/tex} BE = CF [By C.P.C.T.]
So Altitudes are equal.

Q 4

ABC is a triangle in which altitudes BE and CF to sides AC and AB are equal. Show that

  1. {tex}\triangle \mathrm { ABE } \cong \triangle \mathrm { ACF }{/tex}
  2. AB = AC, i.e., {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle.

Solution

Given : ABC is a triangle in which altitude BE and CF to side AC and AB are equal.
To Prove : {tex}\triangle \mathrm { ABE } \cong \triangle \mathrm { ACF }{/tex}

  1. AB = AC i.e. {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle.
    Proof : BE = CF ...... [Given]
    ∠BAE = ∠CAF ...... [Common]
    ∠AFB = ∠AFC ....... [Each 90o]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle \mathrm { ABE } \cong \triangle \mathrm { ACF }{/tex} ........ [By AAS property]
  2. {tex}\triangle A B E \cong \triangle A C F{/tex} ....... [As proved]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = AC . . .[c.p.c.t.]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle.
Q 5

ABC and DBC are two isosceles triangles on the same base BC. Show that {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD.

Solution

Given : ABC and DBC are two isosceles triangles on the same base BC.
To Prove : {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD.
Proof : As ABC is an isosceles triangle on the base BC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB . . . .(1)
As ABC is an isosceles triangle on the base BC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB . . . (2)
Adding the corresponding sides of (1) and (2)
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC + {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB + {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD

Q 6

{tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC is an isosceles triangle in which AB = AC. Side BA is produced to D such that AD = AB (See figure). Show that {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD is a right angle.

Solution


From figure & according to the question, in {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC,
AB = AC ..........(1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC ...(2) [Angles opposite to equal sides are equal]
Again given, AD = AB
But AB = AC  [ from (1) ]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AD = AB = AC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  AD = AC..... (3)
Now in {tex}\Delta{/tex} ADC, 
AD = AC [ from (3) ]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A D C = \angle A C D{/tex} …(4) [Angles opposite to equal sides are equal]
In {tex}\Delta{/tex} BCD,
{tex}\angle A B C + \angle B C D + \angle C D A = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}[ Angle sum property ]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A C B + \angle B C D + \angle C D A = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Because {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC, from (2)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A C B + \angle A C B + \angle A C D + \angle C D A = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Because {tex}\angle B C D = \angle A C B + \angle A C D{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle A C B + \angle A C D + \angle C D A = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle A C B + \angle A C D + \angle A C D = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Because {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD, see (4) ]]
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle A C B + 2 \angle A C D = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 ( \angle A C B + \angle A C D ) = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Taking out 2 common]
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle B C D = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [Because, {tex}\angle A C D + \angle A C B = \angle B C D{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle B C D = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
Hence {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD is a right angle.

Q 7

ABC is a right-angled triangle in which {tex}\angle {/tex}A = {tex}{90^ \circ }{/tex} and AB = AC. Find {tex}\angle {/tex}B and {tex}\angle {/tex}C.

Solution

ABC is a right triangle in which,

AB = AC
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}C ={tex}\angle{/tex} B  [ opposite angles of equal side are equal]   .....(i)
We know that, in ABC, {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle {/tex}B + {tex}\angle {/tex}C ={tex}180^\circ {/tex} [ Angle sum property of triangle]
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}{90^ \circ }{/tex}+{tex}\angle {/tex}B + {tex}\angle {/tex}B = {tex}180^\circ {/tex} [{tex}\angle {/tex} A = {tex}{90^ \circ }{/tex}(given) and {tex}\angle {/tex}B = C (from eq. (i)]
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}  2{tex}\angle {/tex}B = {tex}180^\circ {/tex}- {tex}{90^ \circ }{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 2{tex}\angle {/tex}B =  {tex}{90^ \circ }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}{45^ \circ }{/tex}
Also {tex}\angle {/tex}C = {tex}{45^ \circ }{/tex}     [As {tex}\angle {/tex}B = {tex}\angle {/tex}C]

Q 8

Show that the angles of an equilateral triangle are 60° each.

Solution

Let ABC is an equilateral triangle. We know that all the sides of an equilateral triangle are equal.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} AB = BC = CA  ....(1)


To prove :- {tex}\angle A = \angle B = \angle C = 60°{/tex}
Proof :-
In {tex}\Delta ABC{/tex} we have:-
AB = AC [from (1)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle C = \angle B{/tex}   ....(2)
[{tex}\because{/tex} Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]
Again from (1),
BC = AC
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A = \angle B{/tex}   .....(3)
[{tex}\because{/tex} Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal] .             
From (2) & (3) ;
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A = \angle B=\angle C{/tex}    ....(4)
Now, 
{tex}\angle A + \angle B + \angle C = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Angle sum property of a triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A + \angle A + \angle A = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} [From (4) ]
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \angle A = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}|
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle A = \frac { 180 ^ { \circ } } { 3 } = 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \angle A = \angle B = \angle C = 60 ^ { \circ }{/tex}[ from (4) ]. 
Hence, each angle of an equilateral triangle is equal to 60°.

sections.label: Exercise 7.3
sections.html:
Q 1

{tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DBC are two isosceles triangles on the same base BC and vertices A and D are on the same side of BC. If AD is extended to intersect BC at P, show that :

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD {tex}\cong {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACD
  2. {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABP {tex}\cong {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACP
  3. AP bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A as well as {tex}\angle{/tex}D
  4. AP is the perpendicular bisector of BC.

Solution

It is given in the question that:
{tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DBC are two isosceles triangles

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}DBC
    AD = AD (Common)
    AB = AC (Triangle ABC is isosceles)
    BD = CD (Triangle DBC is isosceles)
    By SSS axiom,
    {tex}\triangle A B D \cong \triangle A C D{/tex}
  2. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABP and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACP,
    AP = AP (Common)
    {tex}\angle{/tex}PAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}PAC (By c.p.c.t)
    AB = AC (Triangle DBC is isoceles)
    By SAS axiom,
    {tex}\triangle A B P \cong \triangle A C P{/tex}
  3. {tex}\angle{/tex}PAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}PAC { c.p.c.t }
    AP bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A (i)
    Also,
    In  {tex}\triangle{/tex} BPD and {tex}\triangle{/tex}CPD,
    PE = PD (Common)
    BD = CD (Triangle DBC is isosceles)
    BP = CP ({tex}\triangle A B P \cong \triangle A C P{/tex} so by c.p.c.t)
    By SSS axiom,
    {tex}\triangle B PD \cong \triangle C PD{/tex}
    Thus,
    {tex}\angle{/tex}BDP = {tex}\angle{/tex}CDP { c.p.c.t } (ii)
    By (i) and (ii), we can say that {tex}\angle{/tex}AP bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A as well as D
  4. {tex}\angle{/tex}BPD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CPD (By c.p.c.t)
    And,
    BP = CP (i)
    Also,
    {tex}\angle{/tex}BPD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CPD = {tex}180^\circ{/tex} (BC is a straight line)
    2{tex}\angle{/tex}BPD = {tex}180^\circ{/tex}
    {tex}\angle{/tex}BPD = {tex}90^\circ{/tex} (ii)
    From (i) and (ii), we get
    AP is the perpendicular bisector of BC
Q 2

AD is an altitude of an isosceles triangle ABC in which AB = AC. Show that

  1. AD bisects BC
  2. AD bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A.
Solution


Given : AD is an altitude of an isosceles triangle ABC in which AB = AC
To prove :

  1. AD bisects BC
  2. AD bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A

Proof :

  1. In right  {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB and right {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC,
    AB = AC . . . . [Given]
    Side AD = Side AD . . . . [Common]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC . . . [RHS Rule]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} BD = CD . . . [c.p.c.t.]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AD bisects BC.
  2. {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC . . . [As proved above]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CDA . . . [c.p.c.t.]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AD bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A
Q 3

Two sides AB and BC and median AM of one triangle ABC are respectively equal to sides PQ and QR and median PN of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR (See figure). Show that:

  1. {tex}\triangle ABM \cong \triangle PQN{/tex}
  2. {tex}\triangle ABC \cong \triangle PQR{/tex}
Solution

AM is the median of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} BM = MC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} BC ...(i)
PN is the median of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} QN = NR = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} QR ...(ii)
Now BC = QR [Given] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2} BC =\frac{1}{2} QR{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} BM = QN ...(iii)

  1. Now in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABM and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQN,
    AB = PQ[Given]
    AM = PN[Given]
    BM = QN[From eq.(iii)]
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABM {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQN [By SSS congruency]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q [By C.P.C.T.] ...(iv)
  2. In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR,
    AB = PQ [Given]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}Q [Prove above]
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} PR = QR [Given]
    ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} PQR [By SAS congruency]
Q 4

BE and CF are two equal altitudes of a triangle ABC. Using RHS congruence rule, prove that the triangle ABC is isosceles.

Solution


Given: BE and CF are two equal altitudes of a triangle ABC.
To Prove: {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is isosceles.
Proof : In right {tex}\triangle{/tex}BEC and right {tex}\triangle{/tex}CFB
side BE = side CF ...[Given]
BC = CB ...[Common]
{tex}\triangle{/tex}BEC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CFB ...[By RHS rule]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCE = {tex}\angle{/tex}CBF ...[c.p.c.t.]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = AC ...[Sides opposite to equal angles of a triangle are equal]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is isosceles.

Q 5

ABC is an isosceles triangle with AB = AC. Draw AP {tex}\perp{/tex} BC to show that {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C.

Solution


Given: ABC is an isosceles triangle with AB = AC.
To Prove : {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C
Construction: Draw AP {tex}\perp{/tex} BC
Proof: In right triangle APB and right triangle APC.
AB = AC . . . . [given]
AP = AP . . . .[Common]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}APC . . . [RHS rule]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABP = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACP . . .[c.p.c.t.]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1

Show that each angle of a rectangle is a right angle.

Solution

We know that rectangle is a parallelogram whose one angle is right angle.

Let ABCD be a rectangle. {tex}\angle A = {90^0}{/tex}
To prove {tex}\angle B = \angle C = \angle D = {90^0}{/tex}
Proof: {tex}\because AD\parallel BC{/tex} and AB is transversal
{tex}\therefore \angle A + \angle B = {180^0}{/tex}
{tex}{90^0} + \angle B = {180^0}{/tex}
{tex}\angle B = {180^0} - {90^0} = {90^0}{/tex}
{tex}\angle C = \angle A{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {\text{ }}\angle C = {90^0}{/tex}
{tex}\angle D = \angle B{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {\text{ }}\angle D = {90^0}{/tex} 
Hence, all the angles of the rectangles is a right angle.

Q 2

Show that the diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular to each other.

Solution


Consider the rhombus ABCD.
You know that AB = BC = CD = DA
Now, in {tex}\triangle AOD{/tex} and {tex}\triangle COD{/tex}, OA = OC (Diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other)
OD = OD (Common)
AD = CD
Therefore, {tex}\triangle AOD{/tex} ≅ {tex}\triangle COD{/tex} (SSS congruence rule)
This gives, {tex}\angle AOD{/tex} = {tex}\angle COD{/tex} (CPCT)
But, {tex}\angle AOD{/tex} + {tex}\angle COD{/tex} = 180° (Linear pair)
So, {tex}2\angle AOD{/tex} = 180°
or, {tex}\angle AOD{/tex} = 90°
So, the diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular to each other.

Q 3

In Figure ABC is an isosceles triangle in which AB = AC. AD bisects exterior angle PAC and CD || AB. Show that 

  1. {tex}\angle DAC = \angle BCA{/tex}
  2. ABCD is a parallelogram

Solution
  1. {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} is isosceles in which AB = AC (Given)
    So, {tex}\angle ABC{/tex}= {tex}\angle ACB{/tex} (Angles opposite to equal sides)
    Also, {tex}\angle PAC{/tex} = {tex}\angle ABC{/tex} + {tex}\angle ACB{/tex} (Exterior angle of a triangle)
    or, {tex}\angle PAC{/tex} = 2{tex}\angle ACB{/tex}   ...(1)
    Now, AD bisects {tex}\angle PAC{/tex}.
    So, {tex}\angle PAC{/tex} = 2 {tex}\angle DAC{/tex}    ...(2)
    Therefore, 2{tex}\angle DAC{/tex} = 2{tex}\angle ACB{/tex} [From (1) and (2)]
    or, {tex}\angle DAC{/tex} = {tex}\angle ACB{/tex}
  2. Now, these equal angles {tex}\angle DAC = \angle ACB{/tex} form a pair of alternate angles when line segments BC and AD are intersected by a transversal AC.
    So, BC || AD
    Also, BA || CD (Given)
    Now, both pairs of opposite sides of quadrilateral ABCD are parallel.
    So, ABCD is a parallelogram.
Q 4

Two parallel lines l and m are intersected by a transversal p. Show that the quadrilateral formed by the bisectors of interior angles is a rectangle.

Solution

Here, it is given that QR {tex}\parallel{/tex} PS and transversal p intersects them at points A and C respectively.
The bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}ACR and {tex}\angle{/tex}SAC intersect at D and the bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}PAC and {tex}\angle{/tex}ACQ intersect at B.
We have to prove that quadrilateral ABCD is a rectangle.
From the given figure, we have

 {tex}\angle{/tex}PAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACR    [Alternate angles as l {tex}\parallel{/tex} m and p is a transversal]
So,  {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}PAC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}ACR
i.e., {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACD
These form a pair of alternate angles for lines AB and DC with AC as a transversal and they are equal also.
So, AB {tex}\parallel{/tex} DC
Similarly, BC  {tex}\parallel{/tex} AD   [Considering {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB and {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD]
Therefore, quadrilateral ABCD is a parallelogram
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}PAC + {tex}\angle{/tex}CAS = 180o   [ by the property of Linear pair ]
So, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}PAC + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}CAS = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 180o = 90o
or, {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC + {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD = 90o
or, {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = 90o
So, ABCD is parallelogram in which one angle ( {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD ) is 90o.
Therefore, ABCD is a rectangle.

Q 5

Show that the bisectors of angles of a parallelogram form a rectangle.

Solution


Let P, Q, R and S be the points of intersection of the bisectors of {tex}\angle A{/tex} and {tex}\angle B{/tex}, {tex}\angle B{/tex} and {tex}\angle C{/tex}, {tex}\angle C{/tex} and {tex}\angle D{/tex}, and {tex}\angle D{/tex} and {tex}\angle A{/tex} respectively of parallelogram ABCD.
In {tex}\triangle ASD{/tex},
Since DS bisects {tex}\angle D{/tex} and AS bisects {tex}\angle A{/tex},
therefore,
{tex}\angle DAS{/tex} + {tex}\angle ADS{/tex} = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}{tex}\angle A{/tex} + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}{tex}\angle D{/tex}
= {tex}\frac 12{/tex} ({tex}\angle A{/tex} + {tex}\angle D{/tex})
= {tex}\frac 12 \times 180^o{/tex} ({tex}\angle A{/tex} and {tex}\angle D{/tex} are interior angles on the same side of the transversal)
= 90o
Also, {tex}\angle DAS{/tex} + {tex}\angle ADS{/tex} + {tex}\angle DSA{/tex} = 180o (Angle sum property of a triangle)
or, 90o + {tex}\angle DSA{/tex} = 180o
or, {tex}\angle DSA{/tex} = 90o
So, {tex}\angle PSR{/tex} = 90o (Being vertically opposite to {tex}\angle DSA{/tex})
Similarly, it can be shown that {tex}\angle APB{/tex} = 90o or {tex}\angle SPQ{/tex} = 90o (as it was shown for {tex}\angle DSA{/tex}).
Similarly, {tex}\angle PQR{/tex} = 90o and {tex}\angle SRQ{/tex} = 90o.
So, PQRS is a quadrilateral in which all angles are right angles.
We have shown that {tex}\angle PSR{/tex} = {tex}\angle PQR{/tex} = 90o and {tex}\angle SPQ{/tex} = {tex}\angle SRQ{/tex} = 90o.
So both pairs of opposite angles are equal.
Therefore, PQRS is a parallelogram in which one angle (in fact all angles) is 90o and so, PQRS is a rectangle.

Q 6

In {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex}, D, E and F are respectively the mid-points of sides AB, BC and CA. Show that {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} is divided into four congruent triangles by joining D, E and F.

Solution


As D and E are mid-points of sides AB and BC of the triangle ABC (Mid-points of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side),
DE || AC
Similarly, DF || BC and EF || AB
Therefore ADEF, BDFE and DFCE are all parallelograms.
Now DE is a diagonal of the parallelogram BDFE,
therefore, {tex}\triangle BDE{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle FED{/tex}
Similarly {tex}\triangle DAF{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle FED{/tex}
and {tex}\triangle EFC{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle FED{/tex}
So, all the four triangles are congruent.

Q 7

l, m and n are three parallel lines intersected by transversal p and q such that l, m and n cut-off equal intercepts AB and BC on p Show that l, m and n cut-off equal intercepts DE and EF on q also.

Solution

It is given that AB = BC and we need to prove that DE = EF.
Construction: Join A to F which intersects m at G as shown below.


The trapezium ACFD is divided into two triangles; namely {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACF and {tex}\triangle{/tex}AFD.
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACF, it is given that B is the mid-point of AC (AB = BC)
and BG {tex}\parallel{/tex} CF (Since m{tex}\parallel{/tex}n)
So, By the converse of Mid-point Theorem, G is the mid-point of AF.
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}AFD, by applying the same argument as G is the mid-point of AF, we have GE {tex}\parallel{/tex} AD so E is the mid-point of DF,
i.e., DE = EF
In other words /, m and n cut-off equal intercepts on q also.

sections.label: Exercise 8.1
sections.html:
Q 1

If the diagonals of a parallelogram are equal, then show that it is a rectangle.

Solution

Given: The diagonals of a parallelogram are equal.

To prove: Parallelogram is a rectangle.
Proof : In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BDA,
AC = BD . . . [Given]
AB = BA . . . [Common]
BC = AD . . . [Opposite sides of parallelogram]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACB {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}BDA . . .[By SSS property]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD . . . [c.p.c.t.] . . . .(1)
As AD || BC . . . [Opposite sides of parallelogram]
transversal AB intersects them.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 180o . . . [Sum of interior angle on the same side of a transversal] . . . .(2)
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 90o . . . [From (1) and (2)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 90o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Parallelogram ABCD is a rectangle.

Q 2

Show that the diagonals of a square are equal and bisect each other at right angles.

Solution

Given: A square ABCD.

To Prove : (i) AC = BD and (ii) Diagonals bisect each other at right angles.
Proof :

  1. In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCA, we have
    AD = BC ...[As sides of a square are equal]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC ...[All interior angles are of 90o]
    AB = BA ...[Common]
    {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCA ...[By SAS rule]
    AC = BD ...[c.p.c.t.]
  2. Now in {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}COD, we have
    AB = CD ...[Sides of a square]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = {tex}\angle{/tex}COD ...[Vertically opp. angles]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}OBA = {tex}\angle{/tex}ODC ...[Alternate interior angles are equal]
    {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}COD ...[By ASA rule]
    OA = OC and OB = OD ...[c.p.c.t.] ...(1)
    Now consider {tex}\triangle{/tex}s AOD and COD.
    AD = CD ...[Sides of square]
    OA = OC ...[As proved above]
    OD = OD ...[Common]
    {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}COD ...[By SSS rule]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = {tex}\angle{/tex}COD ...[c.p.c.t.]
    But {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD + {tex}\angle{/tex}COD = 180° ...[linear pair]
    or {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD + {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = 180° ...[As {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = {tex}\angle{/tex}COD]
    or 2{tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = 180° {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = 90° ...(2)
    From equation (1) and (2) it is clear that diagonals of a square bisect each other at right angles.
Q 3

Diagonal AC of a parallelogram ABCD bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A (See figure). Show that:

  1. It bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}C also,
  2. ABCD is a rhombus.
Solution

Diagonal AC bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A of the parallelogram ABCD.

  1. Since AB {tex}\parallel{/tex} DC and AC intersects them.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}3 [Alternate angles] ...(i)
    Similarly {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4 ...(ii)
    But {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 [Given] ...(iii)
    Thus AC bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}C.
  2. {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4 = {tex}\angle{/tex}1
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AD = CD [Sides opposite to equal angles]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = CD = AD = BC
    Hence ABCD is a rhombus.
Q 4

ABCD is a rectangle in which diagonal AC bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}A as well as {tex}\angle{/tex}C. Show that:

  1. ABCD is a square
  2. diagonal BD bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}B as well as {tex}\angle{/tex}D.
Solution


Given : ABCD is a rectangle,
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \angle A=\frac{1}{2} \angle C{/tex} 
To prove: ABCD is a square proof:

  1. {tex}\angle{/tex}DAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}DCA (AC bisects A and C)
    CD = DA (Sides opposite to equal angles are also equal)
    However,
    DA = BC and AB = CD (Opposite sides of a rectangle are equal)
    AB = BC = CD = DA
    ABCD is a rectangle and all of its sides are equal.
    Hence, ABCD is a square
  2. Let us join BD
    In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD,
    BC = CD (Sides of a square are equal to each other)
    {tex}\angle{/tex}CDB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CBD (Angles opposite to equal sides are equal)
    However,
    {tex}\angle{/tex}CDB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD (Alternating interior angles for AB {tex}\parallel{/tex} CD)
    {tex}\angle{/tex}CBD = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD
    BD bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}B
    Also,
    {tex}\angle{/tex}CDB = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD
    BD bisects {tex}\angle{/tex}D.
Q 5

In parallelogram ABCD, two points P and Q are taken on diagonal BD such that DP = BQ.

Show that

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex}APD {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}CQB
  2. AP = CQ
  3. {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQB {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}CPD
  4. AQ = CP
  5. APCQ is a parallelogram.
Solution

In parallelogram ABCD, two points P and Q are taken on diagonal BD such that DP = BQ.
To Prove :

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex}APD {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CQB
  2. AP = CQ
  3. {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CPD
  4. AQ = CP
  5. APCQ is a parallelogram.

Construction: Join AC to intersect BD at O.

  1. In {tex}\triangle{/tex}APD and {tex}\triangle{/tex}CQB
    AD || BC . . . [Opp. sides of || gm ABCD]
    A transversal BD intersects them
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CBD . . .[Alternate angles]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADP = {tex}\angle{/tex}CBQ . . . (1)
    DP = BQ . . . [Given] . . . (2)
    AD = CB . . . [Opp. sides of || gm ABCD] . . . (3)
    According to (1), (2), (3)
    {tex}\triangle{/tex}APD {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}CQB
  2. {tex}\triangle{/tex}APD {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}CQB . . . [As proved above]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AP = CQ . . . [c.p.c.t.]
  3. In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}CPD,
    AB || CD . . . . . . [Opp. sides of || gm ABCD]
    A transversal BD intersects them
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\angle{/tex}CDP . . .[Alternate angles]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}\angle{/tex}ABQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}CDP
    AQ = CP . . . .[From (3)]
    QB = PD . . . .[Given]
    AB = CD . . . .[Opp. sides of || gm ABCD]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQB {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}CPD . . . [By SAS rule]
  4. {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CPD . . . [As proved above]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AQ = CP . . . [c.p.c.t]
    As the diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} OB = OD
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} OB – BQ = OD – DP . . .[Given : BQ = DP]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} OQ = OP . . . (1)
    Also OA = OC . . . [As diagonals of a || gm bisect each other] . . .(2)
    In view of (1) and (2) APCQ is a parallelogram.
Q 6

ABCD is a parallelogram and AP and CQ are perpendiculars from vertices A and C on diagonal BD (see Figure).

Show that:

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB  {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CQD
  2. AP = CQ.
Solution

Given: ABCD is a parallelogram and AP and CQ are perpendicular from vertices A and C on diagonal BD respectively.
To Prove :

  1. {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB {tex}\cong{/tex}  {tex}\triangle{/tex}CQD
  2. AP = CQ.

Proof :

  1. In {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB and {tex}\triangle{/tex}CQD
    AB = CD . . . [Opp. sides of || gm ABCD]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ABP = {tex}\angle{/tex}CDQ . . .[Alternate interior angles for AB||CD]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}APB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CQD . . .[Each 90o]
    {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CQD . . . [By AAS rule]
  2. As {tex}\triangle{/tex}APB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CQD . . .[As proved above]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AP = CQ . . .[c.p.c.t.]
Q 7

ABCD is a trapezium in which AB || CD and AD = BC.

Show that :

  1. {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}B
  2. {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}D
  3. {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD
  4. diagonal AC = diagonal BD
Solution

Given : ABCD is a trapezium in which AB || CD and AD = BC.
To Prove :

  1. {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}B
  2. {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}D
  3. {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex}  {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAD
  4. diagonal AC = diagonal BD.

Construction: Extend AB and draw a line through C parallel to DA intersecting AB produced at E.

Proof :

  1. AB || CD . . . [Given]
    AD || EC . . .[By construction]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AECD is a parallelogram . . . [A quadrilateral is a parallelogram if a pair of Opp. sides are parallel and of equal length]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AD = EC . . .[Opp. sides of a || gm are equal]
    But AD = BC . . .[Given]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} EC = BC
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CBE = {tex}\angle{/tex}CEB . . . [{tex}\angle{/tex}s opposite to equal side of a triangle are equal] . . .(1)
    {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}CBE = 180o . . .[Linear pair axiom]. . . (2)
    As AD || EC . . .[By construction]
    and transversal AE intersect them
    {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}CEB = 180o . . . [The sum of interior angles on the same side of the transversal is 180o] . . . (3)
    From (2) and (3),
    {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}CBE = {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}CEB
    But {tex}\angle{/tex}CBE = {tex}\angle{/tex}CEB . . .[From (1)]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}A or {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}B
  2. As AB || CD
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 180o. . .[The sum of interior angles on the same side of the transversal is 180o]
    and {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180o
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}D = {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C
    But {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}B . . .[As proved in (i)]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}D = {tex}\angle{/tex}C or {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}D
  3. In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC and  {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAD,
    AB = BA . . . [Common]
    BC = AD . . .[Given]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAD . . .[From (i)]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAD . . .[SAS rule]
  4. As {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAD . . [From (iii)]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} AC = BD . . .[c.p.c.t.]

sections.label: Exercise 8.2
sections.html:
Q 1

ABCD is a quadrilateral in which P, Q, R and S are mid-points of the sides AB, BC, CD and DA. AC is a diagonal. Show that

  1. SR || AC and SR = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
  2. PQ = SR
  3. PQRS is a parallelogram.
Solution

Given: ABCD is a quadrilateral in which P, Q, R and S are mid-points of the sides AB, BC, CD and DA. AC is a diagonal.
To Prove :

  1. SR || AC and SR = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
  2. PQ = SR
  3. PQRS is a parallelogram

Proof :

  1. In  {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAC,
    As S is the mid-point of DA and R is the mid-point of DC
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} SR || AC and SR = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC . . . [Mid point theorem]
  2. In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BAC,
    As P is the mid-point of AB and Q is the mid-point of BC
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} PQ || AC and PQ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC . . . [Mid point theorem]
    But from (i) SR = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AC
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} PQ = SR
  3. PQ || AC . . .[From (i)]
    SR || AC . . .[From (i)]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} PQ || SR . . .[Two lines parallel to the same line are parallel to each other]
    Similarly, PQ = SR . . .[From (ii)]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} PQRS is a parallelogram . . . [A quadrilateral is a parallelogram if a pair of opposite sides is parallel and is of equal length]
Q 2

ABCD is a rhombus and P, Q, R and S are the mid-points of the sides AB, BC, CD and DA respectively. Show that the quadrilateral PQRS is a rectangle.

Solution

Given: ABCD is a rhombus, P, Q, R, S are the mid-points of AB, BC, CD, DA respectively. PQ, QR, RS and SP are joined.
To Prove: PQRS is a rectangle.
Construction: Join AC and BD.

Proof : In {tex}\triangle{/tex}RDS and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PBQ
DS = QB . . .[Halves of opp. sides of || gm are equal]
DR = PB . . .[Halves of opp. sides of || gm are equal]
{tex}\angle{/tex}SDR = {tex}\angle{/tex}QBP . . .[Opp. {tex}\angle{/tex}s of || gm are equal]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}RDS {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}PBQ . . . . [c.p.c.t.]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} SR = PQ
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}RCQ and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAS
RC = AP . . .[Halves of opp. sides of || gm are equal]
CQ = AS . . .[Halves of opp. sides of || gm are equal]
{tex}\angle{/tex}RCQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}PAS . . .[Opp. {tex}\angle{/tex}s of || gm are equal]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}RCQ {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}PAS . . . . [c.p.c.t.]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} RQ = SP
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In PQRS,
SR = PQ and RQ = SP
{tex}\therefore{/tex}PQRS is a parallelogram,
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}CDB,
As R and Q are the mid-points of DC and CB respectively.
{tex}\triangle{/tex} RQ || DB {tex}\therefore{/tex} RF || EO
Similarly, RE || FO
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OFRE is a || gm
As opp. {tex}\angle{/tex}s of || gm are equal and diagonals of rhombus intersect at 90o
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  R = EOF = 90o
Thus PQRS is a rectangle.

Q 3

ABCD is a rectangle and P, Q, R and S are mid-points of the sides AB, BC, CD and DA respectively. Show that the quadrilateral PQRS is a rhombus.

Solution

ABCD is a rectangle. P, Q, R and S are the mid-points of AB, BC, CD and DA respectively. PQ, QR, RS and SP are joined.
To Prove : Quadrilateral PQRS is a rhombus.
Construction: Join AC.

Proof: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC,
As P and Q are the mid-points of AB and BC respectively.
PQ || AC and PQ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} AC ...(1)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ADC,
As S and R are the midpoints of AD and DC respectively.
SR || AC and SR = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} AC ...(2)
From (1) and (2)
PQ || SR and PQ = SR
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Quadrilateral PQRS is a parallelogram . . . (3)
In rectangle ABCD,
AD = BC . . .[Opposite sides]
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} AD = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} BC ...[Halves of equals are equal]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AS = BQ
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}APS and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BPQ
As P is the mid-point of AB
AP = BP
AS = BQ . . . [As proved above]
{tex}\angle{/tex}PAS = {tex}\angle{/tex}PBQ . . .[Each 90o]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}APS {tex}\cong{/tex}  {tex}\triangle{/tex}BPQ . . . [By SAS axiom]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PS = PQ . . .[c.p.c.t.] . . . (4)
According to (3), (4) PQRS is a rhombus.

Q 4

ABCD is a trapezium in which AB || DC, BD is a diagonal and E is the mid-point of AD. A line is drawn through E parallel to AB intersecting BC at F. Show that F is the mid-point of BC.

Solution

Given: ABCD is a trapezium in which AB || DC, BD is a diagonal and E is the mid-point of AD. A line is drawn through E parallel to AB intersecting BC at F.
To prove: F is the mid-point of BC.

Proof: Let DB intersect EF at G.
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}DAB,
As E is the mid-point of DA and EG || AB
{tex}\triangle{/tex}G is the mid-point of DB. . . [By converse of mid-point theorem]
Again in {tex}\triangle{/tex}BDC,
As G is the mid-point of BD and GF || AB || DC
{tex}\triangle{/tex} F is the mid point of BC. . . [By converse of mid-point theorem]

Q 5

In a parallelogram ABCD, E and F are the mid-points of sides AB and CD respectively. Show that the line segments AF and EC trisect the diagonal BD.

Solution

Given: In a parallelogram ABCD, E and F are the mid-points of sides AB and CD respectively.
To Prove: Line segments AF and EC intersect the diagonal BD.

Proof: AB || CD . . .[Opp. sides of || gm ABCD]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AE || FC . . . (1)
As AB = DC . . .[Opp. sides of || gm ABCD]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}AB = \frac{1}{2}DC{/tex} . . .[Halves of equals are equal]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AE = CF . . . (2)
According to (1) and (2)
AECF is a parallelogram . . [A quadrilateral is a parallelogram if a pair of opp. sides is parallel and of equal length]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} EC || AF . . . [Opp. sides of || gm AECF] . . .(3)
In {tex}\triangle {/tex}DQC,
As F is the mid-point of DC and FP || CQ . . .[As EC || AF]
P is the mid-point of DQ . . . [By converse of mid-point theorem]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} DP = PQ . . . . (4)
Similarly, In {tex}\triangle {/tex}BAP,
BQ = PQ . . . .(5)
DP = PQ = BQ . . . From (4) and (5)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Line segments AF and EC trisect the diagonal BD.

Q 6

ABC is a triangle right angled at C. A line through the mid-point M of hypotenuse AB and parallel to BC intersects AC at D. Show that

  1. D is the mid-point of AC
  2. MD {tex}\perp{/tex} AC
  3. CM = MA = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AB
Solution

Given: ABC is a triangle right angled at C. A line through the mid-point M of hypotenuse AB and parallels to BC intersects AC at D.

To Prove:

  1. D is the mid-point of AC (ii) MD ⊥ AC
  2. CM = MA = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AB

Proof :

  1. In ACB,
    As M is the mid-point of AB and MD || BC
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} D is the mid-point of AC . . . [By converse of mid-point theorem]
  2. As MD || BC and AC intersects them
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ADM = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB . . . [Corresponding angles]
    But {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 90o . . .[Given]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADM = 90o {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} MD {tex}\perp{/tex} AC
  3. Now {tex}\angle{/tex}ADM + {tex}\angle{/tex}CDM = 180o . . .[Linear pair axiom]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ADM = {tex}\angle{/tex}CDM = 90o
    In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADM and {tex}\triangle{/tex}CDM
    AD = CD . . . [As D is the mid-point of AC]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}ADM = {tex}\angle{/tex}CDM . . .[Each 90o ]
    DM = DM . . .[Common]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADM {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}CDM . . .[By SAS rule]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} MA = MC . . .[c.p.c.t.]
    But M is the mid-point of AB
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}  MA = MB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AB
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}  MA = MC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AB
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} CM = MA = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}AB

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1

If two intersecting chords of a circle make equal angles with the diameter passing through their point of intersection, prove that the chords are equal.

Solution


Given: AB and CD are two chords of a circle with centre O, intersecting at point E. PQ is a diameter through E, such that {tex}\angle{/tex}AEQ = {tex}\angle{/tex}DEQ.
To prove: AB = CD
Construction: Draw OL {tex}\perp{/tex} AB and OM {tex}\perp{/tex} CD
Proof: {tex}\angle {/tex}LOE + {tex}\angle {/tex}LEO + {tex}\angle {/tex}OLE = 180° (Angle sum property of a triangle)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle {/tex}LOE + {tex}\angle {/tex}LEO + 90° = 180°
{tex}\angle {/tex}LOE + {tex}\angle {/tex}LEO =90° .........................(i)
Similarly, {tex}\angle {/tex}MOE + {tex}\angle {/tex}MEO + {tex}\angle {/tex}OME = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\angle {/tex}MOE + {tex}\angle {/tex}MEO + 90° = 180°
{tex}\angle {/tex}MOE + {tex}\angle {/tex}MEO = 90° . .........................(ii)
From (i) and (ii) we get
{tex}\angle {/tex}LOE + {tex}\angle {/tex}LEO = {tex}\angle {/tex}MOE + {tex}\angle {/tex}MEO ..........(iii)
Also, {tex}\angle {/tex}LEO = {tex}\angle {/tex}MEO (Given) ...(iv)
From (iii) and (iv) we obtain
{tex}\angle {/tex}LOE = {tex}\angle {/tex}MOE
Now in triangles OLE and OME
{tex}\angle {/tex}LEO = {tex}\angle {/tex}MEO (Given)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\angle {/tex}LOE = {tex}\angle {/tex}MOE (Proved above)
EO = EO (Common)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  by ASA congruence criterion we have:
  {tex}\triangle{/tex}OLE {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} OME
{tex}\therefore{/tex} OL = OM ( by CPCT)
Thus, chords AB and CD are equidistant from the centre O of the circle. Since, chords of a circle which are equidistant from the centre are equal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = CD

Q 2

In figure, AB is a diameter of the circle, CD is a chord equal to the radius of the circle. AC and BD when extended intersect at a point E. Prove that {tex}\angle{/tex}AEB = 60°.

Solution

Construction: Join OC, OD, BD and BC


Proof:In {tex}\triangle{/tex}OCD, we have
OC = OD (Each equal to radius)
and CD = r (given)
So OC = OD = CD
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ODC is an equilateral triangle.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}COD = 60°
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}COD = 2 {tex}\angle{/tex}CBD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 60° = 2 {tex}\angle{/tex}CBD {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CBD = 30°
Now since {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB is angle in a semi-circle.
{tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 90°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCE = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 180° - 90° = 90°
Thus, in {tex}\triangle{/tex} BCE, we have
{tex}\angle\mathrm{CEB}+\angle\mathrm{ECB}+\angle\mathrm{CBE}=180^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\angle\mathrm{CEB}+90^\circ+30^\circ=180^\circ{/tex}
