{tex}\angle\mathrm{CEB}=180^\circ-120^\circ=60^\circ{/tex}
Hence {tex}\angle\mathrm{CEB}=60^\circ{/tex}

Q 3

In Fig., ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral in which AC and BD are its diagonals. If {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = 55° and {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 45°, find {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD.

Solution

Since angles in the same segment of a circle are equal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD = {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = 55o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CAD + {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 55o + 45o = 100o
But, {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD = 180o [Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateal]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD = 180o - 100o = 80o

Q 4

In the given figure, two circles intersect at two points A and B. AD and AC are diameters to the two circles. Prove that B lies on the line segment DC.

Solution

In the given diagram join AB. Also {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 90°  (because angle in a semicircle is always 90°)
Similarly, we have {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 90°
So, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 90° + 90° = 180°
Therefore, DBC is a line i.e., B lies on the line segment DC.

Q 5

Prove that the quadrilateral formed (if possible) by the internal angle bisectors of any quadrilateral is cyclic.
 

Solution


In Figure, ABCD is a quadrilateral in which the angle bisectors AH, BF, CF and DH of internal angles A, B, C and D respectively form a quadrilateral EFGH.
Now, {tex}\angle FEH = \angle AEB = 180^o – \angle EAB – \angle EBA{/tex} 
= 180o – {tex}\frac 12 (\angle A + \angle B){/tex}
and {tex}\angle FGH = \angle CGD = 180^o – \angle GCD – \angle GDC{/tex} 
= 180o – {tex}\frac 12 (\angle C + \angle D){/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\angle FEH + \angle FGH{/tex} = {tex}180^o – \frac 12 (\angle A + \angle B) + 180^o – \frac 12 (\angle C + \angle D){/tex}
= 360o – {tex}\frac 12 (\angle A+ \angle B +\angle C +\angle D) = 360° – \frac 12 \times 360^o{/tex}
= 360o – 180o = 180o
Therefore, by Theorem If the sum of a pair of opposite angles of a quadrilateral is 180o, the quadrilateral is cyclic., the quadrilateral EFGH is cyclic.

sections.label: Exercise 9.1
sections.html:
Q 1

Recall that two circles are congruent if they have a same radii. Prove that equal chords of congruent circles subtend equal angles at their centres.

Solution

Given: AB and CD are two equal chords of congruent circles with centres O and O' respectively.
To Prove: {tex}\angle A O B=\angle C O^{\prime} D{/tex}
Proof: In {tex}\triangle O A B{/tex} and {tex}\Delta O^{\prime} C D{/tex}
OA=O'C |Radii of congruent circles]
OB = O'D [Radii of congruent circles]

AB = CD ...Given
{tex}\therefore \triangle \mathrm{OAB} \cong \triangle \mathrm{O}^{\prime} \mathrm{CD}{/tex} [SSS Rule]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = {tex}\angle{/tex}CO'D [c.p.c.t]

Q 2

Prove that if chords of congruent circles subtend equal angles at their centres, then the chords are equal.

Solution

Given: In a circle (O), AB and CD subtend two angles at the centre such that {tex}\angle {/tex}AOB ={tex} \angle {/tex}COD

To Prove: AB = CD
Proof: In {tex}\triangle {/tex}AOB and {tex}\triangle {/tex}COD,
AO = CO [Radii of the same circle]
BO = DO [Radii of the same circle]
{tex}\angle {/tex}AOB = {tex}\angle {/tex} COD [Given]
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB {tex} \cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle {/tex}COD[By SAS congruency]
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} AB = CD [By CPCT]
Hence proved.

sections.label: Exercise 9.2
sections.html:
Q 1

Two circles of radii 5 cm and 3 cm intersect at two points and the distance between their centres is 4 cm. Find the length of the common chord.

Solution


We know that if two circles intersect each other at two points, then the line joining their centres is the perpendicular bisector of their common chord.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Length of the common chord
= PQ =2O'P
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3cm = 6cm

Q 2

If two equal chords of a circle intersect within the circle, prove that the segments of one chord are equal to corresponding segments of the other chord.

Solution

Given: Let AB and CD are two equal chords of a circle of centers
O intersecting each other at point E within the circle.
To prove: (a) AE = CE (b) BE = DE
Construction: Draw OM {tex}\bot {/tex} AB, ON {tex}\bot {/tex} CD. Also join OE.

Proof: In right triangles OME and ONE,
{tex}\angle {/tex}OME = {tex}\angle {/tex}ONE = 90°
OM = ON
[Equal chords are equidistance from the centre]
OE = OE [Common]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}OME {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ONE [RHS rule of congruency]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ME = NE [By CPCT] ...(i)
Now, O is the centre of circle and OM {tex}\bot {/tex} AB
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AM = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} AB [Perpendicular from the centre bisects the chord] ...(ii)
Similarly, NC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CD ...(iii)
But AB = CD [Given]
From eq. (ii) and (iii), AM = NC ...(iv)
Also MB = DN ...(v)
Adding (i) and (iv), we get,
AM + ME = NC + NE
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AE = CE [Proved part (a)]
Now AB = CD [Given] ...(v)
AE = CE [Proved] ...(vi)
Subtracting eq. (vi) from eq. (v), we have
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB - AE = CD - CE
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} BE = DE [Proved part (b)]

Q 3

If two equal chords of a circle intersect within the circle, prove that the line joining the point of intersection to the centre makes equal angles with the chords.

Solution

Given: Two equal chords AB and CD of a circle with centre O intersect within the circle. Their point of intersection is E.
To prove {tex}\angle{/tex}OEA = {tex}\angle{/tex}OED 
Construction: Join OA and OD
Proof: In  {tex}\triangle{/tex}OEA and  {tex}\triangle{/tex}OED 
OE = OE |Common
OA = OD |Radii of a circle
AE = DE
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}OEA {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}OED [SSS Rule]
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OEA = {tex}\angle{/tex}OED [c.p.c.t]

Q 4

If a line intersects two concentric circles (circles with the same centre) with centre O at A, B, C and D, prove that AB = CD.

Solution

Given: A line intersects two concentric circles (circles with the same centre) with centre O at A, B, C and D.
To prove : AB = CD
Construction : Draw {tex}O M \perp B C{/tex}

Proof: {tex}\because{/tex} The perpendicular drawn from the centre of a circle to a chord bisects the chord.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AM = DM
and BM = CM
Subtracting (2) from (1), we get
AM - BM = DM - CM
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = CD

Q 5

Three girls Reshma, Salma and Mandip are playing a game by standing on a circle of radius 5 m drawn in a park. Reshma throws a ball to Salma. Salma to Mandip, Mandip to Reshma. If the distance between Reshma and Salma and between Salma and Mandip is 6 m each, what is the distance between Reshma and Mandip?

Solution


In {tex}\Delta \mathrm { NOR }{/tex} and {tex}\Delta \mathrm { NOM }{/tex}
ON = ON |Common
{tex}\angle \mathrm { NOR } = \angle \mathrm { NOM }{/tex} {tex}| \because{/tex} Equal chords of a circle subtend equal
angle at the centre
OR = OM |Radii of a circle
{tex}\therefore \triangle \mathrm { NOR } \cong \Delta \mathrm { NOM }{/tex} [SAS Rule]
{tex}\therefore \angle O N R = \angle O N M{/tex} [c.p.c.t]
and NR = NM [c.p.c.t.]
But {tex}\angle O N R + \angle O N M = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} |Linear Pair Axiom
{tex}\therefore \angle O \mathrm { NR } = \angle \mathrm { O } \mathrm { NM } = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\triangle{/tex} ON is the perpendicular bisector of RM,
Draw bisector SN of {tex}\angle \mathrm { R } \mathrm { SM }{/tex} to intersect the chord RM in N.
In {tex}\Delta \mathrm { RSN }{/tex} and {tex}\Delta \mathrm { MSN }{/tex}
RS = MS (= 6 cm each)
SN = SN [Common]
{tex}\angle R S N = \angle M S N{/tex} [By construction]
{tex}\therefore \Delta R S N \cong \Delta \mathrm { NSN }{/tex} [SAS Rule]
{tex}\therefore \angle R N S = \angle M N S{/tex} [c.p.c.t]
and RN = MN [c.p.c.t]
But {tex}\angle \mathrm { RNS } + \angle \mathrm { MNS } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} |Linear Pair Axion
{tex}\therefore \angle R N S = \angle M N S = 90 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm { SN }{/tex} is the perpendicular bisector of RM and therefore passes through O when produced.
Let ON = x m
Then SN = (5 - x) m
In right triangle ONR,
x2 + RN2 = 52, ------ (1) |By Pythagoras theorem
In right triangle SNR,
(5-x)2 + RN2 = 62 ---- (2) |By Pythagoras theorem
From (1),
RN2 = 52 - x2
From (2),
RN2 = 62 - (5 - x)2
Equating the two values of RN2, we get
52 - x2 = 62 - (5 -x)2
{tex}\Rightarrow 25 - x ^ { 2 } = 36 - ( 25 - 10 x + x ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 25 - x ^ { 2 } = 36 - 25 + 10 x - x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 25 - x ^ { 2 } = 11 + 10 x - x ^ { 2 }{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow 25 - 11 = 10 x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14 = 10 x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}10x = 14
{tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac { 14 } { 10 } = 1.4{/tex}
Putting x = 1.4 in (1), we get
(1.4)2 + RN2 = 52
{tex}\Rightarrow R N ^ { 2 } = 5 ^ { 2 } - ( 1.4 ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm { RN } ^ { 2 } = 25 - 1.96{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm { RN } ^ { 2 } = 23.04 \Rightarrow \mathrm { RN } = \sqrt { 23.04 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RN = 4.8
{tex}\therefore{/tex} RM = 2 RN = {tex}2 \times 4.8 \mathrm { m } = 9.6 \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Hence, the distance between Reshma and Mandip is 9.6 m.

Q 6

A circular park of radius 20 m is situated in a colony. Three boys Ankur, Syed and David are sitting at equal distance on its boundary each having a toy telephone in his hands to talk each other. Find the length of the string of each phone.

Solution

Construction: Draw PM {tex}\perp{/tex} QR and RN {tex}\perp{/tex} PQ
Determination : PQ = QR = RP
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR is equilateral. We know that in an equilateral triangle, the medians and the altitudes are the same. So, PM and RN are median. They intersect at O where O is the centre of the circle.

Also, PO = 2 OM = 20 (medians intersect each other in the ratio 2: 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OM = 10 m  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PM = OP + OM = 20 + 10 = 30 m
Let QM = x
Then, QM = MR = x [{tex}\because{/tex} PM bisects QR]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} QM = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex}QR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex}QR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} QR = 2x
Similarly, PQ = 2x
In right triangle PMQ,
PQ2 = PM2 + QM2 |By Pythagoras Theorem
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2x)2 = (30)2 + x2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x2 = 900 + x2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x2 - x2 = 900
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 = 900 
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}=\frac{900}{3}=300{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\sqrt{300}=10 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PQ = 2x = 2{tex}(10 \sqrt{3}){/tex}
Hence, the length of the string of each phone is {tex}20 \sqrt{3}{/tex} m.

sections.label: Exercise 9.3
sections.html:
Q 1

In figure, A, B and C are three points on a circle with centre O such that {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = 30° and {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = 60°. If D is a point on the circle other than the arc ABC, find {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC.

Solution

{tex}\angle{/tex}AOC = {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB + {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}AOC = 60° + 30° = 90°
Now {tex}\angle{/tex}AOC = 2{tex}\angle{/tex}ADC
[{tex}\because{/tex} Angled subtended by an arc, at the centre of the circle is double the angle subtended by the same arc at any point in the remaining part of the circle]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}AOC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 90° = 45°

Q 2

A chord of a circle is equal to the radius of the circle. Find the angle subtended by the chord at a point on the minor arc and also at a point on the major arc.

Solution

OA = OB = AB |Given
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}OAB  is equilateral
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = 60o 

 {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}AOB
[The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining part of the circle]
 ={tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 60o = 30o 
Now, {tex}\because{/tex} ADBC is a cyclic quadrilateral.
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB + {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB  [The sum of either pair of opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180o]
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB + 30o = 180o 
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 180o - 30o 
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 150o 

Q 3

In figure, {tex}\angle \mathrm { PQR } = 100 ^ { \circ }{/tex}, where P, Q and R are points on a circle with centre O. Find {tex}\angle O P R{/tex}

Solution

Take a point S in the major arc. Join PS and RS.

{tex}\because{/tex} PQRS is a cyclic quadrilateral.
{tex}\therefore \angle \mathrm { PQR } + \angle \mathrm { PSR } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
|The sum of either pair of opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow 100 ^ { \circ } + \angle P S R = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle P S R = 180 ^ { \circ } - 100 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle P S R = 80 ^ { \circ }{/tex} ......... (1)
Now, {tex}\angle \mathrm { POR } = 2 \angle \mathrm { PSR }{/tex}
|The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is double the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining part of the circle
{tex}= 2 \times 80 ^ { \circ } = 160 ^ { \circ }{/tex} ........ (2) |Using (1)
In {tex}\triangle O P R{/tex}
{tex}\because O P = O R{/tex} |Radii of a circle
{tex}\therefore \angle \mathrm { OPR } = \angle \mathrm { ORP }{/tex} ....... (3)
|Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal
In {tex}\triangle O P R{/tex}
{tex}\angle O P R + \angle O R P + \angle P O R = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} | Sum of all the angles of a triangle is 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm { OPR } + \angle \mathrm { OPR } + 160 ^ { \circ } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} |Using (2) and (1)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle O P R + 160 ^ { \circ } = 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \angle O P R = 180 ^ { \circ } - 160 ^ { \circ } = 20 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm { OPR } = 10 ^ { \circ }{/tex}

Q 4

In given figure, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 69°, {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = 31°, find {tex}\angle{/tex}BDC.

Solution

From the given figure, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, we can write
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC + {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB + {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 180o  ( by angle sum property)
69o + 31o + {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 180° -100° = 80°
{tex}\angle{/tex}BDC = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC (Angles in the same segment)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BDC = 80°

Q 5

In figure, A, B, C, and D are four points on a circle. AC and BD intersect at a point E such that {tex}\angle{/tex}BEC = 130° and {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD = 20°. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC.

Solution

Given: {tex}\angle{/tex}BEC = 130° and {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD = 20°
{tex}\angle{/tex}DEC = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}BEC = 180° - 130° = 50° [Linear pair]
Now in {tex}\triangle{/tex}DEC,
{tex}\angle{/tex}DEC + {tex}\angle{/tex}DCE + {tex}\angle{/tex}EDC = 180° [Angle sum property]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 50° + 20° + {tex}\angle{/tex}EDC = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EDC = 110°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = {tex}\angle{/tex}EDC = 110° [Angles in same segment]

Q 6

ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral whose diagonals intersect at a point E. If {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = 70o, {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC is 30o, find {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD. Further, if AB = BC, find {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD.

Solution

{tex}\angle{/tex}CDB = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC |Angles in the same segment of a circle are equal
= 30 o ....... (1)
{tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = 70o  ...... (2)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCD

{tex}\angle{/tex}BCD + {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC + {tex}\angle{/tex}CDB = 180o  [Sum of all the angles of a triangle is 180o]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD + 70o + 30o = 180o [Using (1) and (2)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD + 100o = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD = 180o - 100o 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD = 80o  ........ (3)
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
AB = BC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD = {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC [Angles opposite to equal sides of a triangle are equal]
= 30o ........ (4) [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 30 (given)]
Now {tex}\angle{/tex}BCD = 80o  |From (3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCA + {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD = 80o 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 30o + {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD = 80o 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD = 80o - 30o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ECD = 50o 

Q 7

If diagonals of a cyclic quadrilateral are diameters of the circle through the vertices of the quadrilateral, prove that it is a rectangle.

Solution


 {tex}\triangle{/tex}OAB and  {tex}\triangle{/tex} OCD
OA = OC [Radii of a circle] 
OB = OD |Radii of a circle
 {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = {tex}\angle{/tex}COD [Vertically opposite angles]
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}OAB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex} OCD |SAS rule
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = CD [c.p.c.t]
  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ar CAB = Ar CCD---- (1)
Similarly, we can show that
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Arc AD = Arc CB ---- (2)
Adding (1) and (2), we get
Arc AB + Arc AD = Arc CD + Arc CB
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ar cBAD = ArcBCD
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}BD divides the circle into two equal parts (each a semicircle)
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 90o, {tex}\angle{/tex}C [Angle of a semi-circle is 90o]
Similarly, we can show that
 {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 90o, {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 90o 
 {tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 90o 
 {tex}\therefore{/tex}ABCD is a rectangle.

Q 8

If the non-parallel sides of a trapezium are equal, prove that it is cyclic.

Solution

Given: Non-parallel sides AD and BC of a trapezium are equal.
To prove: ABCD is a cyclic trapezium.
Construction: Draw DK {tex}\perp{/tex} AB and CP{tex}\perp{/tex} AB 
Proof: In it {tex}\Delta ADK{/tex} and {tex}\Delta BCP{/tex}
AD = BC
DK = CP [Distance between || sides]
DKA = CBP [each 90{tex}{}^\circ{/tex}]
By RHS criteria of congruency,
{tex}\triangle{/tex}DKA {tex}\cong{/tex}{tex}\triangle{/tex}CPB
{tex}\angle A=\angle B {/tex}
{tex}\angle ADK=\angle BCP\\ \angle ADK+90{}^\circ =\angle BCP+90{}^\circ {/tex}
{tex}\angle ADC=\angle BCD{/tex}
{tex}\angle D=\angle C{/tex} ................(ii)
{tex}\angle A+\angle B+\angle C+\angle D=360{}^\circ [\angle A= \angle B , \angle C= \angle D]{/tex}
{tex}\angle B+\angle B+\angle D+\angle D=360{}^\circ \\ \angle B+\angle D=180{}^\circ {/tex}
Hence, ABCD is a cyclic trapezium.

Q 9

In given figure, two circles intersect at two points B and C. Through B, two line segments ABD and PBQ are drawn to intersect the circles at A, D and P, Q respectively. Prove that {tex}\angle{/tex}ACP = {tex}\angle{/tex}QCD.

Solution

As angles in the same segment of circle are equal
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABP = {tex}\angle{/tex}ACP ..........................(i)
{tex}\angle{/tex}ABP = {tex}\angle{/tex}QBD (Vertically opposite angles)
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}QCD = {tex}\angle{/tex}QBD (Angles in the same segment)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABP = {tex}\angle{/tex}QCD ...................(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}ACP = {tex}\angle{/tex}QCD.

Q 10

If circles are drawn taking two sides of a triangle as diameters, prove that the point of intersection of these circles lie on the third side.

Solution

Given: Circles are described with sides AB and AC of a triangle ABC as diameters. They intersect in a point D.
To prove: D lies on the third side BC of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC.

Construction: Join AD.
Proof: Circle described on AB as diameter intersects BC in D.
{tex}\angle {/tex}ADB = 90° [Angle in a semi-circle]
But {tex}\angle {/tex}ADB + {tex}\angle {/tex}ADC = 180° [Linear Pair Axiom]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle {/tex}ADC = 90°
Hence, the circle described on AC as diameter must pass through D.
Thus, the two circles intersect in D.
Now {tex}\angle {/tex}ADB + {tex}\angle {/tex}ADC = 180°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Points B, D, C are collinear
{tex}\therefore{/tex} D lies on BC.

Q 11

ABC and ADC are two right triangles with common hypotenuse AC. Prove that {tex}\angle C A D = \angle C B D.{/tex}

Solution

Given: ABC and ADC are two right triangles with common hypotenuse AC.
To prove: {tex}\angle C A D = \angle C B D{/tex}

Proof : AC is the common hypotenuse and ABC and ADC are two right triangles.
{tex}\therefore \angle A B C = 90 ^ { \circ } = \angle A D C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Both the triangles are in the same semi-circle
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Points A, B, D and C are concyclic
{tex}\therefore{/tex}DC is a chord
{tex}\therefore \angle C A D = \angle C B D{/tex} |{tex}\because{/tex} Angles in the same segment are equal.

Q 12

Prove that a cyclic parallelogram is a rectangle.

Solution

Given: ABCD is a cyclic parallelogram.
To prove: ABCD is a rectangle.

Proof: {tex}\because{/tex} ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = 180o  ... (1)
[ {tex}\because{/tex} Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary]
{tex}\therefore{/tex}ABCD is a parallelogram
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2
From (1) and (2),
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = 90o 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} || gm ABCD is a rectangle.

sections.html:
Q 1

Find the area of a triangle, two sides of which are 8 cm and 11 cm and the perimeter is 32 cm.

Solution


Let a, b, c be the sides of the given triangle and 2s be its perimeter such that
a = 8 cm, b = 11 cm and 2s = 32 cm i.e. s = 16 cm
Now,
a + b + c = 2s
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 + 11 + c = 32
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 13
{tex}\therefore{/tex} s - a = 16 - 8 = 8, s - b = 16 - 11 = 5 and s - c = 16 - 13 = 3
Hence, Area of given triangle = {tex}\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{16 \times 8 \times 5 \times 3}{/tex} = 8{tex}\sqrt{30}{/tex} cm2

Q 2

A triangular park ABC has sides 120 m, 80 m and 50 m (in a given figure). A gardener Dhania has to put a fence all around it and also plant grass inside. How much area does she need to plant? Find the cost of fencing it with barbed wire at the rate of ₹ 20 per metre leaving a space 3m wide for a gate on one side.

Solution

Computation of area: Clearly, the park is trianglar with sides 
a = BC = 120 m, b = CA = 80 m and c = AB = 50 m
Ifs denotes the semi-perimeter of the park, then
2s = a + b + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2s = 120 + 80 + 50 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s = 125
{tex}\therefore{/tex} s - a = 125 - 120 = 5, s - b = 125 - 80 = 45 and s - c = 125 - 50 = 75
Hence, Area of the park = {tex}\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{125 \times 5 \times 45 \times 75}{/tex}m2 = 375{tex}\sqrt{15}{/tex} m2
Length of the wire needed for fencing = perimeter of the park - width of the gate
= 250m - 3m = 247 m
Cost of fencing = Rs.(20 {tex}\times{/tex} 247) = Rs.4940

Q 3

The sides of a triangular plot are in the ratio of 3 : 5 : 7 and its perimeter is 300 m. Find its area.

Solution


Suppose that the sides in metres are 3x, 5x and 7x.
Then, we know that 3x + 5x + 7x = 300 (Perimeter of the triangle)
Therefore, 15x = 300, which gives x = 20.
So the sides of the triangles are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 m, 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 m and 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 m
i.e., 60m, 100m and 140m.
We have s = {tex}\frac{60+100+140}{2}{/tex} = 150 m
and area will be = {tex}\sqrt{150(150-60)(150-100)(150-140) }{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{150 \times 90 \times 50 \times 10}{/tex} 
= 1500{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} m2

sections.label: Exercise 10.1
sections.html:
Q 1

A traffic signal board, indicating SCHOOL AHEAD, is an equilateral triangle with side a. Find the area of the signal board, using Heron’s formula. If its perimeter is 180 cm, what will be the area of the signal board?

Solution


A traffic signal board is an equilateral triangle with side a.
Perimeter of the signal board,
2s = a + a + a 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{s}=\frac{3}{2} a{/tex}
Area of triangle {tex}=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{3 a}{2}\left(\frac{3}{2} a-a\right)\left(\frac{3}{2} a-a\right)\left(\frac{3}{2} a-a\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{3 a}{2} \times \frac{a}{2} \times \frac{a}{2} \times \frac{a}{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 a^{4}}{16}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex} sq. units
Now, if perimeter = 180 cm
3a = 180
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 60 cm
{tex}\therefore \text { Area of signal board }= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times(60)^{2}=900 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
So, area of the signal board is {tex}900 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}.

Q 2

The triangular side walls of a flyover have been used for advertisements. The sides of the walls are 122 m, 22 m and 120 m (see Fig.). The advertisements yield an earning of ₹ 5000 per m2 per year. A company hired one of its walls for 3 months. How much rent did it pay?

Solution

Given: = 122 m, = 22 m and = 120 m
Semi-perimeter of triangle (s){tex} =\frac{122+22+120}{2}=\frac{264}{2}{/tex} = 132 m Using Heron’s Formula,
Area of triangle ={tex} \sqrt{s\left( s-a \right)\left( s-b \right)\left( s-c \right)}{/tex}
={tex}\sqrt{132\left( 132-122 \right)\left( 132-22 \right)\left( 132-120 \right)}{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt {132 \times 10 \times 110 \times 12} {/tex}

{tex} = \sqrt {11 \times 12 \times 10 \times 10 \times 11 \times 12} {/tex}
= 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 12
= 1320 m2
{tex}\because {/tex} Rent for advertisement on wall for 1 year = Rs. 5000 per{tex} {{m}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Rent for advertisement on wall for 3 months for 1320 m2; {tex}\frac{5000}{12}\times 3\times 1320{/tex}
= Rs.1650000
Hence rent paid by company = Rs. 16,50,000

Q 3

There is a slide in a park. One of its side walls has been painted in some colour with a message KEEP THE PARK GREEN AND CLEAN, (see figure). If the sides of the wall are 15 m, 11 m and 6 m, find the area painted in colour.

Solution

Since, sides of coloured triangular wall are 15 m, 11 m and 6 m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Semi-perimeter of coloured triangular wall
S = {tex}\frac{15+11+6}{2}{/tex}={tex}\frac{32}{2}{/tex} = 16 m
Now, Using Heron’s formula,
Area of coloured triangular wall
= {tex}\sqrt{s\left( s-a \right)\left( s-b \right)\left( s-c \right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{16\left( 16-15 \right)\left( 16-11 \right)\left( 16-6 \right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{16\times 1\times 5\times 10}{/tex}
= {tex}20\sqrt{2}{{m}^{2}}{/tex}
Hence area painted in blue colour = {tex}20\sqrt{2}{{m}^{2}}{/tex}

Q 4

Find the area of a triangle two sides of which are 18 cm and 10 cm and the perimeter is 42 cm.

Solution

a = 18 cm, b = 10 cm.
Perimeter = 42 cm.
⇒ a + b + c = 42
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 18 + 10 + c = 42
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 28 + c = 42
{tex}\therefore{/tex} c = 42 – 28
{tex}\therefore{/tex} c = 14 cm.
s = {tex}\frac{42}{2}{/tex} = 21 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Areaṁ of the triangle {tex}=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{21(21-18)(21-10)(21-14)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{21(3)(11)(7)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(7)(3)(3)(11)(7)}{/tex}
=  (7)(3){tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex}
= 21{tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex}cm2

Q 5

Sides of a triangle are in the ratio of 12 : 17 : 25 and its perimeter is 540 cm. Find its area.

Solution

Let the sides of the triangle be 12x, 17x and 25x
Therefore, 12x + 17x + 25x = 540
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 54x = 540 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The sides are 120 cm, 170 cm and 250 cm.
Semi-perimeter of triangle {tex}s =\frac{120+170+250}{2}{/tex} = 270 cm
Now, Area of triangle = {tex}\sqrt{s\left( s-a \right)\left( s-b \right)\left( s-c \right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{270\left( 270-120 \right)\left( 270-170 \right)\left( 270-250 \right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{270\times 150\times 100\times 20}{/tex}
= 9000cm2

Q 6

An isosceles triangle has perimeter 30 cm and each of the equal sides is 12 cm. Find the area of the triangle.

Solution


a = 12 cm, b = 12 cm
Perimeter = 30 cm
a + b + c = 30
⇒ 12 + 12 + c = 30
⇒ 24 + c = 30
⇒ c = 30 – 24
⇒ c = 6 cm
s = {tex}\frac{30}{2}{/tex} cm = 15 cm
∴ Area of the triangle {tex}=\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{15(15-12)(15-12)(15-6)}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{15(3)(3)(9)}=9\sqrt{15}{/tex} cm2

sections.html:
Q 1

Find the curved surface area of a right circular cone whose slant height is 10 cm and base radius is 7 cm.

Solution

We know that,
Curved surface area of cone ={tex}\pi r l{/tex}
{tex}= \frac {22}7 \times 7 \times 10 cm^2{/tex}
{tex}={/tex} 220 cm2

Q 2

The height of a cone is 16 cm and its base radius is 12 cm. Find the curved surface area and the total surface area of the cone (Use {tex}\pi{/tex} = 3.14).

Solution

We are given that,

Here, h = 16 cm and r = 12 cm.
So, from l2 = h2 + r2,we have
l = {tex}\sqrt {16^2 + 12^2} cm = 20 cm{/tex}
So, curved surface area = {tex}\pi rl{/tex}
= 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 cm2 = 753.6 cm2
Further, total surface area = {tex}\pi rl + \pi r^2 {/tex}
= (753.6 + 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12) cm2
= (753.6 + 452.16) cm2
= 1205.76 cm2

Q 3

A corn cob (see Fig.), shaped somewhat like a cone, has the radius of its broadest end as 2.1 cm and length as 20 cm. If each 1 cm2 of the surface of the cob carries an average of four grains, find how many grains you would find on the entire cob?

Solution

Since the grains of corn are found on the curved surface of the corn cob.
So, Total number of grains on the corn cob = Curved surface area of the corn cob {tex}\times{/tex} Number of grains of corn on 1 cm2
Now, we will first find the curved surface area of the corn-cob.
We have, r = 2.1 and h = 20
Let l be the slant height of the conical corn cob. Then,
{tex}l = \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } + h ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { ( 2.1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 20 ) ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 4.41 + 400 } = \sqrt { 404.41 }{/tex} = 20.11
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Curved surface area of the corn cub = {tex}\pi r l{/tex}
= {tex}\frac { 22 } { 7 } \times{/tex} 2.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 20.11 cm2
= 132.726 cm2 = 132.73 cm2
Hence, Total number of grains on the corn cob = 132.73 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 530.92
So, there would be approximately 531 grains of corn on the cob.

Q 4

Find the surface area of a sphere of radius 7 cm.

Solution

We known that the surface area S of a sphere of radius r is given by
S = 4 {tex}\pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Here, r = 7 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = 4 {tex}\times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 cm2 = 616 cm2

Q 5

Find the curved surface area and the total surface area of a hemisphere of radius 21 cm.

Solution

We know that the surface area S and total surfaces area S1 of a hemisphere of radius r are given by
S = 2{tex}\pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} and, S1 = 3{tex}\pi r ^ { 2 }{/tex} respectively.
Here, r = 21 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} S = 2 {tex}\times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times{/tex} 21 {tex}\times{/tex} 21 cm2 and, S1 = 3 {tex}\times \frac { 22 } { 7 } \times{/tex} 21 {tex}\times{/tex} 21 cm2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S = 2772 cm2 and, S1 = 4158 cm2

Q 6

The hollow sphere, in which the circus motorcyclist performs his stunts, has a diameter of 7 m. Find the area available to the motorcyclist for riding.

Solution

We are given,
The diameter of the sphere = 7 m
Therefore, the radius is 3.5 m
So, the riding space available for the motorcyclist is the surface area of the ‘sphere’ which is given by
{tex}4\pi r^2 = 4 \times \frac {22}7 \times 3.5 \times 3.5 m^2{/tex}
= 154 m2

Q 7

A hemispherical dome of a building needs to be painted. If the circumference of the base of the dome is 17.6 m, find the cost of painting it, given the cost of painting is ₹ 5 per 100 cm2.

Solution

Since only the rounded surface of the dome is to be painted, we would need to find the curved surface area of the hemisphere to know the extent of painting that needs to be done. Now, circumference of the dome = 17.6 m. Therefore, 17.6 = 2{tex}\pi r{/tex}
2 {tex}\times \frac { 22 } { 7 } r{/tex} = 17.6 m
So, the radius of the dome = 17.6 {tex}\times \frac { 7 } { 2 \times 22 }{/tex} m = 2.8 m
The curved surface area of the dome = 2{tex}\pi r{/tex}2
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac { 22 } { 7 }{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.8 m2
= 49.28 m2
Now, the cost of painting 100 cm2 is Rs. 5.
So, the cost of painting 1 m2 = Rs. 500
Therefore, the cost of painting the whole dome
= Rs. 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 49.28
= Rs. 24640

Q 8

The height and the slant height of a cone are 21 cm and 28 cm respectively. Find the volume of the cone.

Solution

We know that, l2 = r2 + h2,
Therefore , we have
{tex}r=\sqrt{l^{2}-h^{2}}=\sqrt{28^{2}-21^{2}} \mathrm{cm}=7 \sqrt{7} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
So,  volume of the cone = {tex}\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7 \sqrt{7} \times 7 \sqrt{7} \times 21 \mathrm{cm}^{3}{/tex}
= 7546 cm3

Q 9

Monica has a piece of canvas whose area is 551 m2. She uses it to have a conical tent made, with a base radius of 7 m. Assuming that all the stitching margins and the wastage incurred while cutting, amounts to approximately 1 m2, find the volume of the tent that can be made with it.

Solution

Since the area of the canvas = 551 m2 and area of the canvas lost in wastage is 1 m2, therefore the area of canvas available for making the tent is (551 - 1) m2 = 550 m2
Now, the surface area of the tent = 550 m2 and the required base radius of the conical tent = 7 m
Therefore, curved surface area of tent = 550 m2
That is, {tex}\pi rl{/tex} = 550
or, {tex}\frac { 22 } { 7 }{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} l = 550
l = {tex}\frac { 550 } { 22 }{/tex}m = 25 m
Now, l2 = r2 + h2
Therefore, h = {tex}\sqrt { l ^ { 2 } - r ^ { 2 } } = \sqrt { 25 ^ { 2 } - 7 ^ { 2 } } m = \sqrt { 625 - 49 m } = \sqrt { 576 m }{/tex}
= 24 m
So, the volume of the conical tent = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex}r2h = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \frac { 22 } { 7 }{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 24m3 = 1232 m3

Q 10

Find the volume of a sphere of radius 11.2 cm.

Solution

We know that volume of sphere= {tex}\frac 43 \pi r^3{/tex}
= {tex}\frac 43 \times \frac {22}7 \times 11.2 \times 11.2 \times 11.2 cm^3{/tex} = 5887.32 cm3

Q 11

A shot-putt is a metallic sphere of radius 4.9 cm. If the density of the metal is 7.8 g per cm3, find the mass of the shot-put.

Solution

Since the shot-putt is a solid sphere made of metal and its mass is equal to the product of its volume and density, we need to find the volume of the sphere.
Now, volume of the sphere is given by= {tex}\frac 43 \pi r^3{/tex}
= {tex}\frac 43 \times \frac {22}7 \times 4.9 \times 4.9 \times 4.9 cm^3{/tex}
= 493 cm3 (nearly)
Further, mass of 1 cm3 of metal is 7.8 g.
Therefore, mass of the shot-putt will be = 7.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 493 g
= 3845.44 g = 3.85 kg (nearly)

Q 12

A hemispherical bowl has a radius of 3.5 cm. What would be the volume of water it would contain?

Solution

The volume of water the bowl contain = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\pi {{r}^{3}}{/tex}
Radius = 3.5 cm
Then volume = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\times \frac{22}{7}\times {{\left( 3.5 \right)}^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}\times \frac{22}{7}\times 3.5\times 3.5\times 3.5{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}\times \frac{22}{7}\times \frac{35}{10}\times \frac{35}{10}\times \frac{35}{10}{/tex}
= 89.8 cm3

sections.label: Exercise 11.1
sections.html:
Q 1

Diameter of the base of a cone is 10.5 cm and its slant height is 10 cm. Find its curved surface area.

Solution

Diameter = 10.5 cm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Radius (r) = {tex}\frac{10.5}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{21}{4}{/tex} cm
Slant height of cone (l) = 10 cm
Now we have, Curved surface area of cone = {tex}\pi rl{/tex} = {tex}\frac{22}{7}\times \frac{21}{4}\times 10{/tex}
= 165 cm2

Q 2

Find the total surface area of a cone, if its slant height is 21 m and diameter of its base is 24 m.

Solution

Slant height (l) = 21 m
Diameter of base = 24 m
∴ Radius of base (r) = {tex}24\over2{/tex}m = 12 m
∴ Total curved surface area of the cone ={tex}\pi r(l+r){/tex}
= {tex}22\over7{/tex}× 12 × (21 + 12)
={tex}{22\over7}\times12\times33={8712\over7}{/tex}
={tex}1244{4\over7}\ m^2{/tex}

Q 3

Curved surface area of a cone is 308 cm2 and its slant height is 14 cm. Find the radius of the base.

Solution

Slant height of cone = 14 cm
Let radius of circular end of cone be r. 
Curved surface area of cone = {tex}\pi rl{/tex}
308 cm2 = ({tex}\frac { 22 } { 7 }{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} 14) cm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\left( \frac { 308 } { 44 } \right){/tex} cm = 7 cm
Thus, the radius of circular end of the cone is 7 cm.

Q 4

Curved surface area of a cone is 308 cm2 and its slant height is 14 cm. Find total surface area of the cone.

Solution

 Total surface area of cone = Curved surface Area of cone + Area of base
= {tex}\pi rl{/tex} + {tex}\pi r{/tex}2
= [308 + {tex}\frac { 22 } { 7 }{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} (7)2] cm2
= 462 cm2
Thus, the total surface area of the cone is 462 cm2.

Q 5

A conical tent is 10 m high and the radius of its base is 24 m. Find

  1. Slant height of the tent.
  2. Cost of the canvas required to make the tent, if the cost of 1 m2 canvas is ₹ 70.
Solution
  1. h = 10 m, r = 24 m
    {tex}l = { \sqrt{r^2+h^2}}={\sqrt{(24)^2+(10)^2}}{/tex}
    {tex}= {\sqrt{576+100}}={\sqrt{676}}{/tex}
    = 26 m
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} the slant height of the tent is 26 m.
  2. Curved surface area of the tent = {tex}\pi rl{/tex}
    {tex}={22\over7}\times24\times26\ m^2{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of the canvas required to make the tent, if the cost of 1 m2 canvas is ₹ 70.
    {tex}={22\over7}\times24\times26\times70{/tex} = ₹ 137280
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The cost of the canvas is ₹ 137280.
Q 6

What length of tarpaulin 3 m wide will be required to make conical tent of height 8 m and base radius 6 m? Assume that the extra length of material that will be required for stitching margins and wastage in cutting is approximately 20 cm. {tex}\left( \text{Use }\pi \text{ = 3}\text{.14} \right){/tex}

Solution

Height of the conical tent {tex}\left( h \right){/tex} = 8 m and Radius of the conical tent {tex}\left( r \right){/tex} = 6 m
Slant height of the tent {tex}\left( l \right)=\sqrt{{{r}^{2}}+{{h}^{2}}}{/tex}
={tex}\sqrt{{{\left( 6 \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( 8 \right)}^{2}}}{/tex}
={tex}\sqrt{36+64}{/tex}
={tex}\sqrt{100}{/tex}
= 10 m
Area of tarpaulin = Curved surface area of tent = {tex}\pi rl{/tex} {tex}=3.14\times 6\times 10=188.4\text{ }{{m}^{2}}{/tex}
Width of tarpaulin = 3 m
Let Length of tarpaulin = L
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Area of tarpaulin = {tex}Length\times Breadth\text{ }=\text{ }L\times 3{/tex} = 3L
Now According to question, 3L = 188.4
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} L = 188.4/3 = 62.8 m
The extra length of the material required for stitching margins and cutting is 20 cm = 0.2 m.
So the total length of tarpaulin bought is (62.8 + 0.2) m = 63 m

Q 7

The slant height and base diameter of a conical tomb are 25 m and 14 m respectively. Find the cost of white washing its curved surface at the rate of ₹ 210 per 100 m2.

Solution

Slant height (l) = 25 m
Base diameter (d) = 14 m
∴ Base radius (r) = {tex}14\over2{/tex}m = 7 m
∴ Curved surface area of the tomb = {tex}\pi rl{/tex}
= {tex}22\over7{/tex}× 7 × 25 = 550 m2
∴ Cost of white-washing the curved surface of the tomb at the rate of Rs. 210 per 100 m2
= {tex}210\over100{/tex} × 550 = Rs. 1155.

Q 8

A Joker's cap is in the form of a right circular cone of base radius 7 cm and height 24 cm. Find the area of the sheet required to make 10 such caps.

Solution

Radius of cap (r) = 7 cm, Height of cap (h) = 24 cm
Slant height of the cone (l) = {tex}\sqrt{{{r}^{2}}+{{h}^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{{{\left( 7 \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( 24 \right)}^{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{49+576}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{625}{/tex}
= 25 cm
Area of sheet required to make a cap = CSA of cone = {tex}\pi rl{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{22}{7}\times 7\times 25{/tex} 
{tex}=\text{ }550\text{ }c{{m}^{2}}{/tex}=550 cm2
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Area of sheet required to make 10 caps =10 {tex}\times{/tex} 550 = 5500 cm2

Q 9

A bus stop is barricaded from the remaining part of the road, by using 50 hollow cones made of recycled cardboard. Each cone has a base diameter of 40 cm and height 1 m. If the outer side of each of the cones is to be painted and the cost of painting is ₹ 12 per m2, what will be the cost of painting all these cones? (Use {tex}\pi{/tex} = 3.14 and take {tex}\sqrt {1.04}{/tex} = 1.02)

Solution

Diameter of cone = 40 cm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Radius of cone (r) = {tex}\frac{40}{2}{/tex}
= 20 cm
= {tex}\frac{20}{100}{/tex}m
= 0.2 m
Height of cone (h) = 1 m
Slant height of cone {tex}\left( l \right)=\sqrt{{{r}^{2}}+{{h}^{2}}}{/tex}
={tex}\sqrt{{{\left( 0.2 \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( 1 \right)}^{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{1.04}{/tex} m
Curved surface area of cone = {tex}\pi rl{/tex}
= {tex}3.14\times 0.2\times \sqrt{1.04}{/tex}
= 0.64056 m2
{tex}\because{/tex} Cost of painting 1m2 of a cone = Rs.12
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of painting 0.64056m2 of a cone = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.64056 = Rs. 7.68672
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of painting of 50 such cones = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 7.68672 = Rs. 384.34 (approx.)

sections.label: Exercise 11.2
sections.html:
Q 1

Find the surface area of a sphere of radius 10.5 cm.

Solution

r = 10.5 cm
Surface area of a sphere {tex}=4\pi r^2{/tex}
= {tex}4\times{22\over7}\times(10.5)^2{/tex}
= {tex}4\times22\times10.5\times1.5{/tex}
= 1386 cm2

Q 2

Find the surface area of a sphere of radius 5.6 cm.

Solution

r = 5.6 cm
Surface area of a sphere = {tex}4\pi {r^2}{/tex}
{tex} = 4 \times \frac{{22}}{7} \times {(5.6)^2} = 394.24\;c{m^2}{/tex}

Q 3

Find the surface area of a sphere of radius 14 cm.

Solution

r = 14 cm
Surface area of a sphere ={tex}4\pi r^2{/tex}
{tex}=4\times{22\over7}\times(14)^2{/tex}
{tex}=4\times22\times17\times2{/tex}
= 2464 cm2

Q 4

Find the surface area of a sphere of diameter 14 cm.

Solution

Diameter = 14 cm
∴ Radius (r) = {tex}14\over2{/tex} cm = 7 cm
Surface area of sphere = {tex}4\pi r^2{/tex}
= 4×{tex}22\over7{/tex} × (7)2 = 616 cm2

Q 5

Find the surface area of a sphere of diameter 21 cm.

Solution

Diameter = 21 cm
∴ Radius (r) = {tex}21\over2{/tex} cm
Surface area of sphere ={tex}4\pi r^2{/tex}
= {tex}4\times{22\over7}\times({21\over2})^2{/tex}

= {tex}4\times{22\over7}\times{21\over2}\times{21\over2}{/tex}

=1386 cm2

Q 6

Find the surface area of a sphere of diameter 3.5 m.

Solution

Diameter = 3.5 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius (r) = {tex}3.5\over2{/tex} m = 1.75 m
Surface area of sphere = {tex}4\pi r^2{/tex}
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}22\over7{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} (1.75)2 = 38.5 m2

Q 7

Find the total surface area of a hemisphere of radius 10 cm. (Use {tex}\pi{/tex} = 3.14)

Solution

r = 10 cm
Total surface area of the hemisphere = {tex}3\pi r^2{/tex}
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (10)2 = 942 cm2

Q 8

The radius of a spherical balloon increases from 7 cm to 14 cm as air is being pumped into it. Find the ratio of surface areas of the balloon in the two cases.

Solution

Case I : r = 7 cm
Surface area = {tex}4\pi r^2{/tex}
= 4 × {tex}22\over7{/tex} × (7)2 = 616 cm2
Case II : r = 14 cm
Surface area = {tex}4\pi r^2{/tex}
= 4 ×{tex}22\over7{/tex} × (14)2 = 2464 cm2
∴ Ratio of surface area of the balloon = 616 : 2464 

={tex}1:4{/tex}

Q 9

A hemispherical bowl made of brass has inner diameter 10.5 cm. Find the cost of tin-plating it on the inside at the rate of ₹ 16 per 100 cm2.

Solution

Inner diameter of bowl = 10.5 cm
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Inner radius of bowl {tex}\left( r \right)=\frac{10.5}{2}{/tex} = 5.25 cm
Now, Inner surface area of bowl = {tex}2\pi {{r}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=2\times \frac{22}{7}\times 5.25\times 5.25{/tex}
{tex}=2\times \frac{22}{7}\times \frac{21}{4}\times \frac{21}{4}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{693}{4}c{{m}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\because {/tex} Cost of tin-plating per{tex} 100\text{ }c{{m}^{2}}{/tex} = ₹ 16
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Cost of tin-plating per{tex} 1\text{ }c{{m}^{2}}=\frac{16}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Cost of tin-plating per {tex}\frac{693}{4}c{{m}^{2}}=\frac{16}{100}\times \frac{693}{4}{/tex} = ₹ 27.72

Q 10

Find the radius of a sphere whose surface area is 154 cm2.

Solution

Let the radius of the sphere be r cm.
Surface area = 154 cm2
⇒ {tex}4\pi r^2{/tex} = 154
⇒ {tex}4\times{22\over7}\times r^2=154{/tex}
⇒ {tex}r^2={{154\times7}\over4\times22}={49\over4}{/tex}
⇒ r = {tex}{\sqrt{49\over4}}={7\over2}{/tex}
⇒ r = 3.5 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} the radius of the sphere is 3.5 cm.

Q 11

The diameter of the moon is approximately one fourth of the diameter of the earth. Find the ratio of their surface areas.

Solution

Let diameter of Earth = x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of Earth {tex}\left( r \right)=\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Surface area of Earth = {tex}4\pi {{r}^{2}}{/tex}= {tex}4\pi \times \frac{x}{2}\times \frac{x}{2}=\pi {{x}^{2}}{/tex}
 
Now, Diameter of Moon = {tex}\frac{1}{4}th{/tex} of diameter of Earth = {tex}\frac{x}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of Moon {tex}\left( r \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{8}{/tex}
Surface area of Moon = {tex}4\pi {{r}^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}4\pi \times \frac{x}{8}\times \frac{x}{8}=\frac{\pi {{x}^{2}}}{16}{/tex}
Now, Ratio = {tex}\frac{\text{Surface area of Moon}}{\text{Surface area of Earth}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{\pi {{x}^{2}}}{16}}{\pi {{x}^{2}}}{/tex}={tex}\frac{\pi {{x}^{2}}}{16}\times \frac{1}{\pi {{x}^{2}}}{/tex}={tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Required ratio = 1 : 16

Q 12

A hemispherical bowl is made of steel, 0.25 cm thick. The inner radius of the bowl is 5 cm. Find the outer curved surface area of the bowl.
 

Solution

Inner radius of bowl {tex}\left( r \right){/tex} = 5 cm
Thickness of steel {tex}\left( t \right){/tex} = 0.25 cm
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Outer radius of bowl (R) = {tex}r+t{/tex} = 5 +0.25 = 5.25 cm
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Outer curved surface area of bowl = {tex}2\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}}{/tex}= {tex}2\times \frac{22}{7}\times 5.25\times 5.25{/tex}
= {tex}2\times \frac{22}{7}\times \frac{21}{4}\times \frac{21}{4}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{693}{4}{/tex}
 {tex}=\text{ }173.25\text{ }c{{m}^{2}}{/tex}

Q 13

A right circular cylinder just encloses a sphere of radius r (see Figure). Find

  1. surface area of the sphere.
  2. curved surface area of the cylinder.
  3. ratio of the areas obtained in (i) and (ii).
Solution
  1. Surface area of the sphere = {tex}4\pi r^2{/tex}
  2. For cylinder
    Radius of the base = r
    Height = 2r
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Curved surface area of the cylinder = {tex}2\pi rh=2\pi (r)(2r)=4\pi r^2{/tex}
  3. Ratio of the areas obtained in (i) and (ii)
    {tex}={{surface\ area\ of\ the\ sphere}\over{curved\ surface\ area\ of\ the\ cylinder}}{/tex} 
    {tex}={{4\pi r^2}\over{4\pi r^2}}={1\over1}=1:1{/tex}

sections.label: Exercise 11.3
sections.html:
Q 1

Find the volume of the right circular cone with radius 6 cm and height 7 cm.

Solution

r = 6 cm, h = 7 cm
∴ Volume of the right circular cone = {tex}{1\over3}\pi r^2h{/tex}
{tex}={1\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(6)^2\times7{/tex} = 264 cm3

Q 2

Find the volume of the right circular cone with radius 3.5 cm and height 12 cm.

Solution

r = 3.5 cm, h = 12 cm.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the right circular cone {tex}={1\over3}\pi r^2h{/tex}
{tex}={1\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(3.5)^2\times12{/tex} = 154 cm3

Q 3

Find the capacity in litres of a conical vessel with radius 7 cm, slant height 25 cm.

Solution

r = 7 cm, l = 25 cm.
r2 + h2 = l2
⇒ (7)2 + h2 = (25)2
⇒ h2 = (25)2 – (7)2
⇒ h2 = 625 – 49
⇒ h2 = 576
⇒ h = {tex}\sqrt{576}{/tex}
⇒ h = 24 cm
∴ Capacity {tex}={1\over3}\pi r^2h{/tex}
{tex}={1\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(7)^2\times24{/tex}
= 1232 cm3 = 1.232 l

Q 4

Find the capacity in litres of a conical vessel with height 12 cm, slant height 13 cm.

Solution

12 cm, l = 13 cm.
r2 + h2 = l2
⇒ r2 + (12)2 = (13)2
⇒ r2 + 144 = 169
⇒ r2 = 169 – 144
⇒ r2 = 25
⇒ r = {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex}
⇒ r = 5 cm
∴ Capacity {tex}={1\over3}\pi r^2h{/tex}
{tex}={1\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(5)^2\times12{/tex}
{tex}={2200\over7}\ cm^3={2200\over7000}l{/tex}
{tex}={11\over35}l{/tex}

Q 5

The height of a cone is 15 cm. If its volume is 1570 cm3, find the radius of the base. (Use {tex}\pi{/tex} = 3.14)

Solution

Let the radius of the base of the cone be r cm.
h = 15 cm, Volume = 1570 cm3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{1\over3}\pi r^2h{/tex} = 1570
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}1\over3{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} r2 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 1570
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r^2={{1570\times3}\over3.14\times15}{/tex} ⇒ {tex}r^2=100{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r=\sqrt{100}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 10 cm.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} the radius of the base of the cone is 10 cm.

Q 6

If the volume of a right circular cone of height 9 cm is 48{tex}\pi{/tex} cm3, find the diameter of its base.

Solution

Let the radius of the base of the right circular cone be r cm.
h = 9 cm, volume = 48{tex}\pi{/tex} cm3
⇒{tex}{1\over3}\pi r^2h{/tex} = 48{tex}\pi{/tex}
⇒{tex}{1\over3}r^2h=48{/tex} 
⇒ {tex}{1\over3}\times r^2\times9=48{/tex}
⇒ r2 = {tex}{48\times3}\over9{/tex}
⇒ r2 = 16 ⇒ r = {tex}\sqrt{16}{/tex} = 4 cm
⇒ 2r = 2(4) = 8 cm.
∴ the diameter of the base of the right circular cone is 8 cm.

Q 7

A conical pit of top diameter 3.5 m is 12 m deep. What is its capacity in kilolitres?

Solution

For conical pit : Diameter = 3.5 m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius (r) = {tex}{3.5}\over2{/tex}m = 1.75 m
Depth (h) = 12 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacity of the conical pit = {tex}{1\over3}\pi r^2h{/tex}
{tex}{1\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(1.75)^2\times12\ m^3{/tex}
= 38.5 m3 = 38.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 l
= 38.5 kl.

Q 8

The volume of a right circular cone is 9856 cm3. If the diameter of the base is 28 cm, find

  1. Height of the cone
  2. Slant height of the cone
  3. Curved surface area of the cone.
Solution
  1. Diameter of cone = 28 cm
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of cone = 14 cm
    Volume of cone = {tex}9856\text{ }c{{m}^{3}}{/tex}

     
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}\pi {{r}^{2}}h{/tex} = 9856
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}\times \frac{22}{7}\times 14\times 14\times h=9856{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}h=\frac{9856\times 3\times 7}{22\times 14\times 14}{/tex} = 48 cm
  2. Slant height of cone {tex}\left( l \right)=\sqrt{{{r}^{2}}+{{h}^{2}}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt{{{\left( 14 \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( 48 \right)}^{2}}}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt{196+2304}{/tex}
    = {tex}\sqrt{2500}{/tex} = 50 cm
  3. Curved surface area of cone = {tex}\pi rl=\frac{22}{7}\times 14\times 50{/tex} = {tex}2200\text{ }c{{m}^{2}}{/tex}
Q 9

A right triangle ABC with sides 5 cm, 12 cm and 13 cm is revolved about the side 12 cm. Find the volume of the solid so obtained.

Solution


The solid obtained will be a right circular cone whose radius of the base is 5 cm. and height is 12 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r = 5 cm, h = 12 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume = {tex}{1\over3}\pi r^2h{/tex}
{tex}{1\over3}\times\pi \times (5)^2\times12\ cm^3{/tex}
= 100{tex}\pi{/tex} cm3 
The volume of the solid so obtained is 100{tex}\pi{/tex} cm3

Q 10

A right triangle ABC with sides 5 cm, 12 cm, and 13 cm is revolved about the side 12 cm. Find the volume of the solid so obtained. If the triangle ABC is revolved about side 5 cm, then find the volume of the solid so obtained. Find also the ratio of the volumes of the two solids obtained.

Solution

Let ABC be a right triangle with AB = 12 cm, BC = 5 cm and AC = 13 cm.
When this triangle is revolved about AB, it forms a right circular cone of radius = BC = 5 cm and height AB = 12 cm.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} V1 = Volume of the solid formed = Volume of the cone of radius 5 cm and height 12 cm
 {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 cm3 = 100 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm3
When triangle ABC is revolved about BC, it forms a right circular cone of radius AB = 12 cm and height BC = 5 cm.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} V2 = Volume of the solid formed
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V2 = Volume of the cone of radius 12 cm and height 5 cm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V2 {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \times \pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 cm3 = 240 {tex}\pi{/tex} cm3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required ratio = V1 : V2 = 100{tex}\pi{/tex} : 240 {tex}\pi{/tex} = 5 : 12

Q 11

A heap of wheat is in the form of a cone whose diameter is 10.5 m and height is 3 m. Find its volume. The heap is to be covered by canvas to protect it from rain. Find the area of the canvas required.

Solution

For heap of wheat
Diameter = 10.5 m
∴ Radius (r) = {tex}10.5\over2{/tex} cm = 5.25 m
Height (h) = 3 m
∴ Volume = {tex}{1\over3}\pi r^2h{/tex}
{tex}={1\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(5.25)^2\times3{/tex}
= 86.625 m3
Slant height,  {tex}l=\sqrt{r^2+h^2}{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt{(5.25)^2+(3)^2}=\sqrt{27.5625+9}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{36.5625}{/tex} = 6.05 m
∴ Curved surface area ={tex}\pi{/tex}rl
= {tex}22\over7{/tex} × 5.25 × 6.05 = 99.825 m2
∴ The area of the canvas required is 99.825 m2

sections.label: Exercise 11.4
sections.html:
Q 1

Find the volume of a sphere whose radius is 7 cm.

Solution

r = 7 cm
∴ Volume = {tex}{4\over3}\pi r^3{/tex}
{tex}={4\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(7)^3={4312\over3}\ cm^3=1437{1\over3}\ cm^3{/tex}

Q 2

Find the volume of a sphere whose radius is 0.63 m.

Solution

r = 0.63 m
∴ volume ={tex}{4\over3}\pi r^3{/tex}
{tex}={4\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(0.63)^3=1.05\ m^3{/tex}

Q 3

Find the amount of water displaced by a solid spherical ball of diameter 28 cm.

Solution

Diameter = 28 cm
∴ Radius (r) ={tex}28\over2{/tex} cm = 14 cm
∴ Amount of water displaced =  Volume of solid spherical ball = {tex}{4\over3}\pi r^3{/tex}
{tex}={4\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(14)^3={34496\over3}\ cm^3=11498{2\over3}\ cm^3{/tex}

Q 4

Find the amount of water displaced by a solid spherical ball of diameter 0.21 m.

Solution

Diameter = 0.21 m
∴ Radius (r) = {tex}{0.21}\over2{/tex}m = 0.105 m
∴ Amount of water displaced = {tex}{4\over3}\pi r^3{/tex}
{tex}={4\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(0.105)^3=0.004851\ m^3{/tex}

Q 5

The diameter of a metallic ball is 4.2 cm. What is the mass of the ball, if the density of the metal is 8.9 g per cm3?

Solution

Diameter of metallic ball = 4.2 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of metallic ball {tex}\left( r \right){/tex}={tex}\frac{4.2}{2}{/tex} = 2.1 cm
Volume of metallic ball = {tex}\frac{4}{3}\pi {{r}^{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{3}\times \frac{22}{7}\times 2.1\times 2.1\times 2.1{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{4}{3}\times \frac{22}{7}\times \frac{21}{10}\times \frac{21}{10}\times \frac{21}{10} =38.808 c{{m}^{3}}{/tex}
Density of metal = 8.9 g per cm3
 Density x Volume = Mass
{tex}\because{/tex} Mass of 1 cm3 = 8.9 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of 38.808 cm3 4 = 8.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 38.808 = 345.3912 g = 345.39 g (approx.)

Q 6

The diameter of the moon is approximately one-fourth of the diameter of the earth. What fraction of the volume of the earth is the volume of the moon?

Solution

Let diameter of earth be {tex}x{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of earth {tex}\left( r \right)=\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
Now, Volume of earth = {tex}\frac{4}{3}\pi {{r}^{3}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Earth is considered to be a sphere]
= {tex}\frac{4}{3}\times \pi \times \frac{x}{2}\times \frac{x}{2}\times \frac{x}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{8}\times \frac{4}{3}\pi {{x}^{3}}{/tex} ………..(i)
According to question, Diameter of moon = {tex}\frac{1}{4}\times{/tex} Diameter of earth = {tex}\frac{1}{4}\times x=\frac{x}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of moon (R) = {tex}\frac{x}{8}{/tex}
Now, Volume of Moon = {tex}\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{3}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Moon is considered to be a sphere]
= {tex}\frac{4}{3}\times \pi \times \frac{x}{8}\times \frac{x}{8}\times \frac{x}{8}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{512}\times \frac{4}{3}\pi {{x}^{3}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{64}\times \left[ \frac{1}{8}\times \frac{4}{3}\pi {{x}^{3}} \right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{64}\times{/tex} Volume of Earth [From eq. (i)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of moon is {tex}\frac{1}{64}th{/tex} the volume of earth.

Q 7

How many litres of milk can a hemispherical bowl of diameter 10.5 cm hold?

Solution

Diameter = 10.5 cm.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius (r) ={tex}10.5\over2{/tex}=5.25 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Amount of milk = {tex}{2\over3}\pi r^3{/tex}
{tex}={2\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(5.25)^3\ cm^3{/tex}
= 303 cm3 = 0.303 l

Q 8

A hemispherical tank is made up of an iron sheet 1 cm thick. If the inner radius is 1 m, then find the volume of the iron used to make the tank.

Solution

Inner radius of hemispherical tank (r) = 1 m = 100 cm
Thickness of sheet = 1 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Outer radius of hemispherical tank (R) = 100 + 1 = 101 cm
Volume of iron of hemisphere = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\pi{/tex} [R3 - r3] cm2
= {tex}\frac{2}{3}\times \frac{22}{7}\times \left[ {{\left( 101 \right)}^{3}}-{{\left( 100 \right)}^{3}} \right]{/tex} cm2
= {tex}\frac{44}{21}{/tex} [1030301 - 1000000] cm2
=  0.06348 m2

Q 9

Find the volume of a sphere whose surface area is 154 cm2.

Solution

Let the radius of the sphere be r cm.
Surface area = 154 cm2
{tex}\Rightarrow\ 4\pi r^2{/tex} = 154
{tex}\Rightarrow\ 4\times{22\over7}\times r^2=154{/tex}
 {tex}\Rightarrow\ r^2={154\times7\over4\times22}\Rightarrow {r^2={49\over4} }{/tex} 
 {tex}\Rightarrow r=\sqrt{49\over4}\Rightarrow r={7\over2}{/tex} cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Volume of the sphere ={tex}{4\over3}\pi r^3{/tex}
{tex}={4\over3}\times{22\over7}\times({7\over2})^2={539\over3}\ cm^3{/tex}
{tex}=179{2\over3}\ cm^3{/tex}

Q 10

A dome of a building is in the form of a hemisphere. From inside, it was white-washed at the cost of ₹ 4989.60. If the cost of white-washing is ₹ 20 per square metre, find the

  1. inside surface area of the dome.
  2. Volume of the air inside the dome.
Solution
  1. Inside surface area of the dome {tex}={4989.60\over20}{/tex} = 249.48 m2
  2. Let the radius of the hemisphere be r m.
    Inside surface area = 249.48 m2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\pi {/tex}r2 = 249.48
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}22\over7{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} r2 = 249.48
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = {tex}{249.48\times7}\over2\times22{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = 39.69
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = {tex}\sqrt{39.69}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 6.3 m
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the air inside the dome = {tex}{2\over3}\pi r^3{/tex}
    {tex}={2\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(6.3)^3{/tex} = 523.9 m3
Q 11

Twenty-seven solid iron spheres, each of radius r and surface area S are melted to form a sphere with surface area S'. Find the

  1. radius r' of the new sphere,
  2. ratio of S and S'.
Solution

Volume of 27 solid sphere, each of radius, {tex}r = 27{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}r^3 = 36{/tex}{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}r^3 {/tex}
According to the question,
Volume of sphere of radius r' = Volume of 27 solid spheres
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}(r')^3 = 36{/tex}{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}r^3{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(r')^3 = 27r^3 = (3r)^3{/tex}
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r' = 3r{/tex}W
We have,
S' = 4{tex}\pi{/tex}r'2 = 4{tex}\pi{/tex}(3r)2 = 36{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{S}{S'}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \pi r^2}{36 \pi r^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}S : S' = 1 : 9.{/tex}

Q 12

A capsule of medicine is in the shape of a sphere of diameter 3.5 mm. How much medicine (in mm3) is needed to fill this capsule?

Solution

Diameter of the capsule = 3.5 mm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius of the capsule (r) = {tex}3.5\over2{/tex} mm = 1.75 mm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacity of the capsule = {tex}{4\over3}\pi r^3{/tex}
{tex}={4\over3}\times{22\over7}\times(1.75)^3\ mm^3{/tex}
= 22.46 mm3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 22.46 mm3 of medicine is needed to fill this capsule.

sections.html:
Q 1

In a particular section of Class IX, 40 students were asked about the months of their birth and the following graph was prepared for the data so obtained:

Observe the bar graph given above and answer the following questions:

  1. How many students were born in the month of November?
  2. In which month were the maximum number of students born?
Solution

from the graph it is clear that.

  1. 4 students were born in the month of November.
  2. The Maximum number of students were born in the month of August.
Q 2

A family with a monthly income of ₹ 20,000 had planned the following expenditures per month under various heads:

Heads Expenditure
(in thousand rupees)
Grocery 4
Rent 5
Education of children 5
Medicine 2
Fuel 2
Entertainment 1
Miscellaneous 1

Draw a bar graph for the data above.

Solution

We draw the bar graph of this data in the following steps. Note that the unit in the second column is thousand rupees. So, ‘4’ against ‘grocery’ means ₹ 4000.

  1. We represent the Heads (variable) on the horizontal axis choosing any scale, since the width of the bar is not important. But for clarity, we take equal widths for all bars and maintain equal gaps in between. Let one Head be represented by one unit.
  2. We represent the expenditure (value) on the vertical axis. Since the maximum expenditure is ₹ 5000, we can choose the scale as 1 unit = ₹ 1000.
  3. The bar graph is drawn in Fig.
  4. Here, you can easily visualise the relative characteristics of the data at a glance, e.g., the expenditure on education is more than double that of medical expenses. Therefore, in some ways it serves as a better representation of data than the tabular form.
Q 3

A teacher wanted to analyse the performance of two sections of students in a mathematics test of 100 marks. Looking at their performances, she found that a few students got under 20 marks and a few got 70 marks or above. So she decided to group them into intervals of varying sizes as follows: 0 - 20, 20 - 30, . . ., 60 - 70, 70 - 100. Then she formed the following table:

Marks Number of students
0 - 20 7
20 - 30 10
30 - 40 10
40 - 50 20
50 - 60 20
60 - 70 15
70 - above 8
Total 90

Draw a histogram for this table?

Solution

The histrogram is given below.

Q 4

Consider the marks, out of 100, obtained by 51 students of a class in a test, given in Table.

Marks Number of students
0 - 10 5
10 - 20 10
20 - 30 4
30 - 40 6
40 - 50 7
50 - 60 3
60 - 70 2
70 - 80 2
80 - 90 3
90 - 100 9
Total 51

Draw a frequency polygon corresponding to this frequency distribution table.

Solution

The OABCDEFGHIJKL is the frequency polygon, which is shown in Fig.

Frequency polygons can also be drawn independently without drawing histograms. For this, we require the mid-points of the class-intervals used in the data. These mid-points of the class-intervals are called class-marks.

Q 5

In a city, the weekly observations made in a study on the cost of living index are given in the following table:

Cost of living index Number of weeks
140 - 150 5
150 - 160 10
160 - 170 20
170 - 180 9
180 - 190 6
190 - 200 2
Total 52

Draw a frequency polygon for the data above (without constructing a histogram)

Solution

Since we want to draw a frequency polygon without a histogram, let us find the class-marks of the classes given above, that is of 140 - 150, 150 - 160,.... For 140 - 150, the upper limit = 150, and the lower limit = 140
So, the class-mark = {tex}\begin{equation} \frac{150+140}{2}=\frac{290}{2}=145 \end{equation}{/tex}
Continuing in the same manner, we find the class-marks of the other classes as well.
So, the new table obtained is as shown in the following table:

Table

Cost of living index Class-marks Number of weeks
140 - 150 145 5
150 - 160 155 10
160 - 170 165 20
170 - 180 175 9
180 - 190 185 6
190 - 200 195 2
Total 52

We can now draw a frequency polygon by plotting the class-marks along the horizontal axis, the frequencies along the vertical-axis, and then plotting and joining the points B(145, 5), C(155, 10), D(165, 20), E(175, 9), F(185, 6) and G(195, 2) by line segments. We should not forget to plot the point corresponding to the class-mark of the class 130 - 140 (just before the lowest class 140 - 150) with zero frequency, that is, A(135, 0), and the point H (205, 0) occurs immediately after G(195, 2). So, the resultant frequency polygon will be ABCDEFGH (see Fig.)
