Q 10

In which of the following conditions, the distance between the molecules of hydrogen gas would increase?

  1. Increasing pressure on hydrogen contained in a closed container
  2. Some hydrogen gas leaking out of the container
  3. Increasing the volume of the container of hydrogen gas
  4. Adding more hydrogen gas to the container without increasing the volume of the container
Q 11

A sample of water under study was found to boil at 102° C at normal pressure. Is water pure? Will this water freeze at 0° C? Comment.

Solution

The boiling point of pure water is 100 °C at 1 atm. The freezing point of pure water is 0 °C. The sample of water under study boils at 102 °C at normal pressure. Hence, the sample is not pure water. The sample of water is not pure, it will not freeze at 0 °C. It will freeze at a temperature below 0 °C.

Q 12

A student heats a beaker containing ice and water. He measures the temperature of the content of the beaker as a function of time. Which of the following Fig. would correctly represent the result? Justify your choice.

Solution

Graph (d) represents the result correctly. Before the student starts to heat the mixture, the temperature of the mixture would be zero since ice and water are in equilibrium. When the student heats the mixture, heat supplied (equivalent of latent heat of fusion) is initially utilized by ice in melting. Hence, the temperature does not change till all the ice in the beaker melts. This explains the first part of the graph (constant temperature). When the student heats the mixture further, he will observe an increase in the temperature of the water inside the beaker. This explains the second part / slope of the graph (increasing temperature).

Q 13

Evaporation of a liquid at room temperature leads to a ________ effect.

Q 14

At room temperature, the forces of attraction between the particles of solid substances are ________ than those which exist in the gaseous state.

Q 15

The arrangement of particles is less ordered in the ________ state. However, there is no order in the ________ state.

Q 16

________ is the change of solid state directly to a gaseous state without going through the ________ state.

Q 17

The phenomenon of change of a liquid into the gaseous state at any temperature below its boiling point is called ________.

Solution

Evaporation

Q 18

The phenomenon of change of a liquid into the gaseous state at any temperature below its boiling point is called ________.

Q 19

Match the physical quantities given in column A to their SI units given in column B:

(A) (B)
(a) Pressure (i) cubic metre
(b) Temperature (ii) kilogram
(c) Density (iii) pascal
(d) Mass (iv) Kelvin
(e) Volume (v) kilogram per cubic metre
Solution

The correct order of match is given as (a) - (iii), (b) - (iv), (c) - (v), (d) - (ii), (e) - (i).

Q 20

The non-SI and SI units of some physical quantities are given in column A and column B respectively. Match the units belonging to the same physical quantity:

(A) (B)
(a) Degree celsius (i) Kilogram
(b) Centimeter (ii) Pascal
(c) Gram per centimeter cube (iii) Metre
(d) Bar (iv) Kelvin
(e) Milligram (v) Kilogram per metre cube
Solution

The correct order of match is given as (a) - (iv), (b) - (iii), (c) - (v), (d) - (ii), (e) - (i).

Q 21

Osmosis is a special kind of diffusion. Explain.

Solution

Osmosis is a special kind of diffusion. In both the phenomena, there is a movement of particles from the region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration. However, in case of osmosis, the movement is of "solvent particles" from a less concentrated "solution" and the movement is through a semi-permeable membrane.

Q 22

Classify the following into osmosis/diffusion:-

  1. Swelling up of a raisin on keeping in water.
  2. Spreading of the virus on sneezing.
  3. Earthworm dying on coming in contact with common salt.
  4. Shrinking of grapes kept in thick sugar syrup.
  5. Preserving pickles in salt.
  6. Spreading of the smell of cake being baked throughout the house.
  7. Aquatic animals using oxygen dissolved in water during respiration.
Solution
  1. Osmosis
  2. Diffusion
  3. Osmosis
  4. Osmosis
  5. Osmosis
  6. Diffusion
  7. Diffusion
Q 23

Water as ice has a cooling effect, whereas water as steam may cause severe burns. Explain these observations.

Solution

In the case of ice, the water molecules have low energy while in the case of steam the water molecules have high energy. When water as ice melts, it absorbs a certain amount of heat from the surface (skin) in contact and thus produces a cooling effect on the surface. Whereas when water as steam touches the skin, it condenses on the skin, and releases the heat (The heat which the boiling water absorbs to get converted into steam is known as latent heat of vaporization). Therefore, it may cause severe burns.

Q 24

Alka was making tea in a kettle. Suddenly she felt intense heat from the puff of steam gushing out of the spout of the kettle. She wondered whether the temperature of the steam was higher than that of the water boiling in the kettle. Comment.

Solution

The temperature of both boiling water and steam is 100°C, but steam has more energy because of latent heat of vaporisation. Latent heat of vapourisation makes it much more hotter than hot boiling water.

Q 25

A glass tumbler containing hot water is kept in the freezer compartment of a refrigerator. If you could measure the temperature of the content of the tumbler, which of the following graph would correctly represent the change in its temperature as a function of time?

Q 26

Suggest which of the vessels A, B, C or D in Fig. will have the highest rate of evaporation? Explain.

Solution

Vessel (c) will have the highest rate of evaporation. Evaporation is a surface phenomenon; the rate of evaporation increases with an increase in surface area. The moving fan will reduce humidity and increase the rate of evaporation.The particles of water vapours will move away with wind (moving air) with an increase in air speed. Surface area in vessel (b) is lesser than that in vessel (a) and vessel (c) whereas vessel (d) is completely closed and offers no surface area for evaporation.

Q 27
  1. Conversion of solid to vapours is called sublimation. Name the term used to denote the conversion of vapours to solid.
  2. Conversion of solid-state to liquid state is called fusion; what is meant by the latent heat of fusion?
Solution
  1. The conversion of vapour to solid without changing into liquid is known as 'deposition'.
  2. The amount of heat required to convert 1 kg of any solid into liquid at its melting point at one atmosphere pressure is known as the latent heat of fusion of that solid.
Q 28

You are provided with a mixture of naphthalene and ammonium chloride by your teacher. Suggest an activity to separate them with well labelled diagram.

Solution

Naphthalene is insoluble in water. It is volatile at room temperature. Ammonium chloride is highly soluble in water and volatile at a higher temperature.  The property of solubility in water can be utilized in separating the mixture of naphthalene and ammonium chloride.
Method:

Add water to the mixture of naphthalene and ammonium chloride. The crystalline white salt, ammonium chloride, is highly soluble in water gets dissolved in it whereas naphthalene being insoluble in water remains undissolved.  Therefore, naphthalene can be separated from the solution by using the simple process of filtration.  Filtration is a process by which insoluble solids can be removed from a liquid by using a filter paper.

In the end, ammonium chloride can be recovered from the solution using the process of evaporation.

Q 29

It is a hot summer day, Priyanshi and Ali are wearing cotton and nylon clothes respectively. Who do you think would be more comfortable and why?

Solution

Cotton is a soft fibre whereas nylon is a synthetic polymer. Cotton absorbs water (moisture) better as compared to nylon. Therefore, cotton helps in a faster absorption of sweat followed by evaporation. Evaporation of sweat from a cotton shirt, particularly during a windy weather, leads to a cooling effect. So, Priyanshi would be more comfortable in a cotton shirt, whereas Ali (wearing a nylon shirt) would not be that comfortable.

Q 30

You want to wear your favorites shirt to a party, but the problem is that it is still wet after a wash. What steps would you take to dry it faster?

Solution

Conditions that can increase the rate of evaporation of water are:

  1. An increase in the surface area by spreading the shirt.
  2. An increase in the temperature by putting the shirt under the sun.
  3. Increase in the wind speed by spreading it under the fan.
Q 31

Comment on the following statements:

  1. Evaporation produces cooling.
  2. Rate of evaporation of an aqueous solution decreases with an increase in humidity.
  3. Sponge though compressible is solid.
Solution
  1. Evaporation produces cooling as the particles at the surface of the liquid gain energy from the surroundings and changes into vapour thereby producing a cooling effect.
  2. The air around us cannot hold more than a definite amount of water vapour at a given temperature which is known as humidity. So, if the air is already rich in water vapour, it will not take up more water therefore, the rate of evaporation of water will decrease.
  3.  A sponge has minute holes in which air is trapped. Also, the material is not rigid. When we press it, the air is expelled out and we are able to compress it.
Q 32

Why does the temperature of a substance remain constant during its melting point or boiling point?

Solution

The temperature of a substance remains constant at its melting and boiling points until all the substance melts or boils because, the heat supplied is continuously used up in changing the state of the substance by overcoming the forces of attraction between the particles. This heat energy absorbed without showing any rise in temperature is given the name latent heat of fusion/latent heat of vaporisation.

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Which of the following statements are true for pure substances?

  1. Pure substances contain only one kind of particles
  2. Pure substances may be compounds or mixtures
  3. Pure substances have the same composition throughout
  4. Pure substances can be exemplified by all elements other than nickel
Q 2

Rusting of an article made up of iron is called

Q 3

A mixture of sulphur and carbon disulphide is

Q 4

Tincture of iodine has antiseptic properties. This solution is made by dissolving

Q 5

Which of the following are homogeneous in nature

  1. ice
  2. wood
  3. soil
  4. air
Q 6

Which of the following are physical changes?

  1. Melting of iron metal
  2. Rusting of iron
  3. Bending of an iron rod
  4. Drawing a wire of iron metal
Q 7

Which of the following are physical changes?

  1. Decaying of wood
  2. Burning of wood
  3. Sawing of wood
  4. Hammering of a nail into a piece of wood
Q 8

Two substances, A and B were made to react to form a third substance, A2B according to the following reaction {tex}2 A+B \rightarrow A_{2} B{/tex}. Which of the following statements concerning this reaction are incorrect?

  1. The product A2B shows the properties of substances A and B
  2. The product will always have a fixed composition
  3. The product so formed cannot be classified as a compound
  4. The product so formed is an element
Q 9

Two chemical species X and Y combine together to form a product P which contains both X and  {tex}\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y} \rightarrow \mathrm{P}{/tex}, X and Y cannot be broken down into simpler substances by simple chemical reactions. Which of the following concerning the species X, Y, and P are correct? 

  1. P is a compound
  2. X and Y are compounds
  3. X and Y are elements
  4. P has a fixed composition
Q 10

Suggest separation technique(s) one would need to employ to separate the following mixtures:

  1. Mercury and water
  2. Potassium chloride and ammonium chloride
  3. Common salt, water and sand
  4. Kerosene oil, water and salt
     
Solution
  1. Separating funnel- Mercury and water are immiscible liquids. Mercury is denser than water and can be separated by using a separating funnel.
  2. Sublimation- Ammonium chloride is a sublimating compound and can be separated from the mixture through sublimation.
  3. Sand can be separated by sedimentation, decantation, and filtration. After that, salt can be separated by evaporation.
  4. Separating funnel to separate kerosene oil followed by evaporation/distillation of water.
Q 11

Which of the tubes in Fig (a) and (b) will be more effective as a condenser in the distillation apparatus?

Solution

The tube in figure (a) will be more effective as a condenser in the distillation apparatus.
This is because due to the presence of beads in the tube (a) will give a large surface area as compared to the tube in which there are no beads. And, the tube with large surface area leads to effective cooling. Thus, tube (a) will be the more effective condenser in the distillation apparatus. A condenser condenses steam to water in a distillation process. It converts gases into liquids by cooling them.

Q 12

Salt can be recovered from its solution by evaporation. Suggest some other technique for the same?

Solution

Salt can also be recovered from its solution by crystallization. Crystallization helps in obtaining pure crystals of a salt from its solution.

Q 13

'The Seawater can be classified as homogeneous as well as a heterogeneous mixture.' Comment.

Solution

Seawater is a mixture of several gases and salts in water. Therefore, seawater is considered as a homogeneous mixture. But other than salts and water, seawater also contains mud, decayed plants, etc. So it is considered as a heterogeneous mixture. Therefore, seawater can be classified as a homogeneous as well as a heterogeneous mixture.

Q 14

While diluting a solution of salt in water, a student by mistake added acetone (boiling point 56 °C). What technique can be employed to get back the acetone? Justify your choice.

Solution

Acetone can be separated from the mixture of salt solution and water by distillation. There is a considerable difference in the boiling point of acetone (56°C) and the boiling point of water (100°C). So, when the solution is heated, acetone will evaporate before water does and it can be collected after condensation.

Q 15

What would you observe when:

  1. A saturated solution of potassium chloride prepared at 60 °C is allowed to cool to room temperature.
  2. An aqueous sugar solution is heated to dryness.
  3. a mixture of iron filings and sulphur powder is heated strongly.
Solution
  1. Solid potassium chloride will separate out from the saturated solution when the temperature of the solution reduces from 60 °C to the room temperature. This is because saturation decreases with decrease in temperature. A change in temperature affects the solubility of the solid solute.
  2. When the aqueous sugar solution is heated, the water will initially evaporate. When the solution is heated to dryness, the sugar will be left behind in the container after vaporization of water. The sugar left in the container will get charred because of more heating.

  3. A black coloured compound called iron sulphide is formed when a mixture of iron filings and sulphur powder is heated strongly. The chemical equation showing the reaction between iron filings and sulphur powder is as  {tex}\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{S} \quad \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \text { FeS } \\ \text { ronsings } \end{array}{/tex}.

Q 16

Explain why particles of a colloidal solution do not settle down when left undisturbed, while in the case of a suspension they do.

Solution

We know that the particles in a colloid show random zigzag motion called brownian movements. These brownian movements counters the force of gravity acting on colloidal particles and helps in providing stability to colloidal particles by not allowing them to settle down. On the other hand, Particle size in a suspension is larger than those in a colloidal solution. Also molecular interaction in a suspension is not strong enough to keep the particles suspended and hence they settle down.

Q 17

Smoke and fog both are aerosols. In what way are they different?

Solution

Both - fog and smoke - have gas as the dispersion medium (the continuous phase). The difference lies in the dispersed phase (the suspended phase). The dispersed phase in fog is liquid (water droplets) whereas the dispersed phase in smoke is solid (particulate matter).

Q 18

Classify the following as physical or chemical properties:

  1. The composition of a sample of steel is 98% iron, 1.5% carbon and 0.5% other elements.
  2. Zinc dissolves in hydrochloric acid with the evolution of hydrogen gas.
  3. Metallic sodium is soft enough to be cut with a knife.
  4. Most metal oxides form alkalis on interacting with water.
Solution
  1. Physical property of iron: Steel is an alloy of iron with about 1 percent carbon.
  2. The chemical property of zinc: Zinc is a reactive metal. It displaces hydrogen from hydrochloric acid and forms zinc chloride.
  3. The physical property of sodium: Sodium is a soft metal.
  4. The chemical property of metallic oxides: Metallic oxides react with water and form alkalies.
Q 19

The teacher instructed three students ‘A’, ‘B’ and ‘C’ respectively to prepare a 50% (mass by volume) solution of sodium hydroxide (NaOH). ‘A’ dissolved 50 g of NaOH in 100 ml of water, ‘B’ dissolved 50 g of NaOH in 100 g of water while ‘C’ dissolved 50 g of NaOH in water to make 100 ml of solution. Which one of them has made the desired solution and why?

Solution

Student ‘C’ has made the desired solution.
Mass by volume% = {tex}=\frac{\text { Mass of solute }}{\text { volume of solution }} \times 100{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{50 \mathrm{g}}{100 \mathrm{ml}} \times 100=50 \%{/tex} mass by volume

Q 20

Name the process associated with the following:

  1. Dry ice is kept at room temperature and at one atmospheric pressure.
  2. A drop of ink placed on the surface of water contained in a glass spreads throughout the water.
  3. A potassium permanganate crystal is in a beaker and water is poured into the beaker with stirring.
  4. A acetone bottle is left open and the bottle becomes empty.
  5. Milk is churned to separate cream from it.
  6. Settling of sand when a mixture of sand and water is left undisturbed for some time.
  7. Fine beam of light entering through a small hole in a dark room, illuminates the particles in its paths.
Solution
  1. Sublimation
  2. Diffusion
  3. Diffusion/dissolution
  4. Evaporation/diffusion
  5. Centrifugation
  6. Sedimentation
  7. Tyndall effect
Q 21

You are given two samples of water labeled as ‘A’ and ‘B’. Sample ‘A’ boils at 100°C and sample ‘B’ boils at 102 °C. Which sample of the water will not freeze at 0 °C? Comment.

Solution

Sample ‘B’ will not freeze at 0 °C because may contain impurities. At 1 atm, the boiling point of pure water is 100 °C and the freezing point of pure water is 0 °C.

Q 22

What are the favorable qualities given to gold when it is alloyed with copper or silver for the purpose of making ornaments?

Solution

Pure gold (24-carat gold) is very soft. It is alloyed with silver or copper to impart strength while making ornaments. An alloy that contains 20 parts of gold and 4 parts of silver is called a 20-carat gold.

Q 23

An element is sonorous and highly ductile. Under which category would you classify this element? What other characteristics do you expect the element to possess?

Solution

Metals are sonorous and highly ductile; hence, this element can be classified as a metal. Other characteristics expected to be possessed by this element are – lustre, malleability, heat and electrical conductivity.

Q 24

Give an example each for the mixture having the following characteristics. Suggest a suitable method to separate the components of these mixtures:

  1. A volatile and a non-volatile component.
  2. Two volatile components with an appreciable difference in boiling points.
  3. Two immiscible liquids.
  4. One of the components changes directly from solid to gaseous state.
  5. Two or more coloured constituents soluble in some solvent.
Solution
  1. Water and acohol; can be separated by distillation.
  2. Petroleum contains many volatile components and they can be separated by fractional distillation.
  3. Water and kerosene oil; can be separated by separating funnel.
  4. Salt and ammonium chloride mixture; can be separated by sublimation.
  5. Ink; different pigments can be separated by chromatography.
Q 25

A colloid is a ________ mixture and its components can be separated by the technique known as ________.

Q 26

Ice, water, and water vapour look different and display different ________ properties but they are ________ the same.

Q 27

A mixture of chloroform and water are taken in a separating funnel that is mixed and left undisturbed for some time. The upper layer in the separating funnel will be of ________ and the lower layer will be that of ________.

Q 28

A mixture of two or more miscible liquids, for which the difference in the boiling points is less than 25 K can be separated by the process called ________.

Q 29

When light is passed through water containing a few drops of milk, it shows a bluish tinge. This is due to the Scattering of light by milk and the phenomenon is called ________.

Q 30

Sucrose (sugar) crystals obtained from sugarcane and beetroot are mixed together. Will it be a pure substance or a mixture? Give reasons for the same.

Solution

When sucrose (sugar) crystals obtained from sugarcane and beetroot are mixed together, it will not lead to a mixture. It will be a pure substance because the chemical composition of sucrose crystals will be the same whether the sucrose crystals are obtained from sugarcane or from beetroot. The combined substance will have sucrose crystals of only one kind and will have a definite set of properties - that of sucrose.

Q 31

Give some examples of Tyndall effect observed in your surroundings?

Solution

Following are some examples of Tyndall effect:-

  1. Sunlight entering a room through ventilation near the ceiling.
  2. A beam of light coming inside a forest through a canopy of trees.
  3. Shining a flashlight beam into a glass of milk.
  4. The visible beam of headlights in fog. 
Q 32

Can we separate alcohol dissolved in water by using a separating funnel? If yes, then describe the procedure. If not, explain.

Solution

Alcohol as well as water is a good solvent. They are highly soluble in each other. Hence, alcohol and water cannot be separated by using a separating funnel. However, they can be separated by fractional distillation.

Q 33

On heating calcium carbonate gets converted into calcium oxide and carbon dioxide.

  1. Is this a physical or a chemical change?
  2. Can you prepare one acidic and one basic solution by using the products formed in the above process? If so, write the chemical equation involved.
Solution
  1. This is a chemical change.
  2. Since metallic oxides are basic in nature hence aqueous solution of calcium oxide will be basic. Non-metallic oxides are acidic in nature hence aqueous solution of carbon dioxide would be acidic.
    CaO + H2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ca(OH)2 (basic)
    CO2 + H2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2CO3 (acidic)
Q 34

Non-metals are usually poor conductors of heat and electricity. They are non-lustrous, non-sonorous, non-malleable and are coloured.

  1. Name a lustrous non-metal.
  2. Name a non-metal which exists as a liquid at room temperature.
  3. The allotropic form of a non-metal is a good conductor of electricity. Name the allotrope.
  4. Name a non-metal which is known to form the largest number of compounds.
  5. Name a non-metal other than carbon which shows allotropy.
  6. Name a non-metal which is required for combustion.
     
Solution
  1. Iodine
  2. Bromine
  3. Graphite
  4. Carbon
  5. Sulphur, phosphorus
  6. Oxygen
Q 35

Classify the substances given in Fig. into elements and compounds:

 

Solution

Elements: Cu, Zn, O2, F2, Hg, Diamond
Compounds: H2O, CaCO3

Q 36

Which of the following are not compounds?

  1. Chlorine gas
  2. Potassium chloride 
  3.  Iron
  4. Iron sulphide
  5. Aluminium
  6. Iodine
  7. Carbon
  8. Carbon monoxide
  9. Sulphur powder
Solution

The list of elements that is not a compound is given below:
(i) Chlorine gas consists of chlorine molecules. Chlorine is a non-metal.
(iii) Iron, Iron is a metal.
(v) Aluminium, Aluminum is a metal
(vi) Iodine, Iodine is a non-metal.
(vii) Carbon, Carbon is a non-metal.
(ix)  Sulphur powder, Sulphur is a non-metal.
Others are compounds because they are formed by the combination of different elements.

Q 37

Fractional distillation is suitable for the separation of miscible liquids with a boiling point difference of about 25 K or less. What part of fractional distillation apparatus makes it efficient and possesses an advantage over a simple distillation process? Explain using a diagram.

Solution

Fractional distillation is a process to separate the volatile liquids. In the apparatus, a fractionating column is the most important part of the fractional distillation apparatus. This column has glass beads in it which provides a large surface area for the vapours to collide and lose energy so that they can be quickly condensed and distilled. Apart from the presence of bead, if the length of the column is increased, then it would also increase the efficiency. The vapours of high boiling liquid get condensed earlier (at a lower level).

Advantages:

  1. This method can separate the liquids with a boiling point difference of about or less than 25 K. 
  2. During the process, both evaporation and condensation take place simultaneously.
  3. A mixture (like petroleum) can also be separated by fractional distillation process which contains several components.
Q 38
  1. Under which category of mixtures will you classify alloys and why?
  2. Whether a solution is always liquid or not. Comment.
  3. Can a solution be heterogeneous?
Solution
  1. Alloys are a homogeneous mixture of metals or non-metals because
    1. It shows the properties of its constituents, and
    2. It has variable composition, e.g. brass is considered a mixture because it shows the properties of its constituents, copper and zinc; and it has a variable composition.
  2. No, a solution is not generally a liquid always. For e.g. alloys are known to be solid solutions.
  3. The term solution is generally used for ‘true solution’. In this case, the solution is always homogeneous.
    In the case of ‘colloidal solution’, that is not a true solution i.e. the solution is heterogeneous.
Q 39

Iron filings and sulphur were mixed together and divided into two parts, A and B. Part A was heated strongly while part B was not heated. Dilute hydrochloric acid was added to both the parts and evolution of gas was seen in both the cases. How will you identify the gases evolved?

Solution

Part A: {tex} {Fe + S}\xrightarrow{\Delta }FeS{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {FeS}\limits + 2HCL(dil.) \to FeC{l_2} + {H_2}S \uparrow {/tex}
Here H2S gas is produced, which is identified by its characteristic smell of rotten eggs.
Part B: {tex} {Fe + S}\rightarrow mixture{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {Fe}+S + 2HCL(dil.) \to FeC{l_2} + {H_2} \uparrow {/tex}
Here H2 gas is produced. Hydrogen gas is tested by bringing a burning matchstick near the mouth of the test tube. It burns with a pop sound and water is formed.

Q 40

A child wanted to separate the mixture of dyes constituting a sample of ink. He marked a line by the ink on the filter paper and placed the filter paper in a glass containing water as shown in Fig. The filter paper was removed when the water moved near the top of the filter paper.

  1. What would you expect to see, if the ink contains three different coloured components?
  2. Name the technique used by the child.
  3. Suggest one more application of this technique.
Solution
  1. If the ink contains three different coloured components, three different bands will be seen on the filer paper.
  2. The child used the technique of Paper Chromatography.
  3. Paper Chromatography can be used to separate the pigments present in chlorophyll.
Q 41

A group of students took an old shoe box and covered it with a black paper from all sides. They fixed a source of light (a torch) at one end of the box by making a hole in it and made another hole on the other side to view the light. They placed a milk sample contained in a beaker/tumbler in the box as shown in the Fig. They were amazed to see that milk taken in the tumbler was illuminated. They tried the same activity by taking a salt solution but found that light simply passed through it?

  1. Explain why the milk sample was illuminated. Name the phenomenon involved.
  2. Same results were not observed with a salt solution. Explain.
  3. Can you suggest two more solutions which would show the same effect as shown by the milk solution?

     
Solution
  1. Milk is a colloid. The particulate matter present inside milk scatter the light passing through milk and shows Tyndall effect.
  2. Salt solution is a homogeneous solution. Small particles present in a salt solution do not scatter light and hence, a salt solution does not exhibit Tyndall effect.
  3. Detergent solution and sulphur solution will also show Tyndall effect.
Q 42

Classify each of the following as a physical or a chemical change. Give reasons.

  1. Drying of a shirt in the sun.
  2. Rising of hot air over a radiator.
  3. Burning of kerosene in a lantern.
  4. Change in the colour of black tea on adding lemon juice to it.
  5. Churning of milk cream to get butter.
Solution
  1. It is a physical change because moisture in the shirt is converted from its liquid state to gaseous state because of the heat of the Sun.
  2. It is a physical change because water in the radiator is converted from a liquid state to gaseous state.
  3. It is a chemical change because combustion of kerosene occurs and new products are formed.
  4. It is a chemical change because there is a reaction between citric acid present in lemon and the compounds of the tea resulting in the formation of new products.
  5. It is a physical change because the cream suspended in milk is separated by churning (centrifugation).
Q 43

During an experiment, the students were asked to prepare a 10% (Mass/Mass) solution of sugar in water. Ramesh dissolved 10 g of sugar in 100 g of water while Sarika prepared it by dissolving 10 g of sugar in water to make 100 g of the solution.

  1. Are the two solutions of the same concentration.
  2. Compare the mass % of the two solutions.
Solution
  1. Concentrations of two solutions are different.
  2. Solution A has 10 g sugar and 100 g water
    Mass % = {tex}\frac {Mass of solute}{Mass of solution}\times 100{/tex} = {tex}\frac {10 g}{110 g} \times 100{/tex} = 9.09%
    Solution B has 10 g sugar and 90 g water
    Mass % = {tex}\frac {Mass of solute}{Mass of solution}\times 100{/tex} = {tex}\frac {10 g}{110 g} \times 100{/tex} = 10%
Q 44

You are provided with a mixture containing sand, iron filings, ammonium chloride and sodium chloride. Describe the procedure you would use to separate these constituents from the mixture?

Solution

Components of the given mixture can be separated by the following methods:-

  1. By using a magnet: Moving a magnet over the mixture will result in iron fillings getting stuck to the magnet. Thus, iron will be separated from the mixture.
  2. Sublimation: The remaining mixture is heated in a china dish. Ammonium chloride is a sublimating substance and hence it will evaporate without passing through the liquid phase. The crust of ammonium chloride can be collected by placing an inverted funnel on top of the china dish.
  3. Sedimentation, decantation, and filtration: The remaining mixture is dissolved in water and allowed to settle for some time. Sand, being insoluble in water, settles at the bottom. The liquid is decanted in another beaker. The liquid is then filtered to remove any traces of sand.
  4. Evaporation: The liquid is now a solution of salt in water. This is heated in a beaker so that the water evaporates. Once all the water evaporates, salt remains in the beaker.
Q 45

Arun has prepared 0.01% (by mass) solution of sodium chloride in water. Which of the following correctly represents the composition of the solutions?

Q 46

Calculate the mass of sodium sulphate required to prepare its 20% (mass per cent) solution in 100 g of water.

Solution

Let the mass of sodium sulphate required be 'x' g
The mass of water = 100 g
The mass of solution =(mass of sodium sulphate + mass of water) = (x + 100)g
Percentage of sodium sulphate in solution {tex}=\;\frac{Mass\;of\;sodium\;sulphate}{Mass\;of\;solution}\times100{/tex}
{tex}20=\frac{x}{x+100} \times 100{/tex}
20x + 2000 = 100x 
80x = 2000
x = {tex}\frac{2000}{80}{/tex}
= 25 g
Therefore,  the mass of sodium sulphate required is 25 g.

Q 12

Write the molecular formulae for the following compounds:

  5. Mercury (II) chloride

Q 16

Which of the following symbols of elements are incorrect? Give their correct symbols.

  1. Cobalt CO
  2. Carbon c
  3. Aluminium AL
  4. Helium He
  5. Sodium So
Solution
  1. Cobalt CO is an incorrect symbol. The correct symbol for Cobalt is Co.
  2. Carbon c is an incorrect symbol. Its correct symbol is C.
  3. Aluminium AL is an incorrect symbol. Its correct symbol is Al.
  4. Sodium So is an incorrect symbol. Its correct symbol is Na. (It is derived from Latin name ‘Natrium’).
  5. Helium, He is the correct symbol.
Q 17

Give the chemical formulae for the following compounds and compute the ratio by mass of the combining elements in each one of them. (You may use appendix-III).

  1. Ammonia
  2. Carbon monoxide
  3. Hydrogen chloride
  4. Aluminium fluoride
  5. Magnesium sulphide
Solution
  1. NH3 - ratio N : H × 3 = 14 : 3
  2. CO - ratio C : O = 3 : 4
  3. HCl - ratio H : Cl = 1 : 35.5 or 2 : 71
  4. AIF3 - ratio Al : F× 3  = 9 : 19
  5. MgS - ration Mg : S = 3 : 4
Q 18

State the number of atoms present in each of the following chemical species

  1. CO32-
  2. PO43-
  3. P2O5
  4. CO
Solution
  1. The number of atoms present in CO32- = 1C + 3(O) = 4
  2. The number of atoms present in PO43- = 1P + 4(O) = 5
  3. The number of atoms present in P2O5 = 2P + 5(O) = 7
  4. The number of atoms present inCO = 1C + 1(O) = 2
Q 19

What is the fraction of the mass of water due to neutrons?

Solution

Mass of one mole (Avogadro Number) of neutrons = 1 g
Mass of one neutron = {tex}\frac{1}{{{\rm{Avogadro Number(}}{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{)}}}}g{/tex}
Mass of one molecule of water = {tex}\frac{{{\rm{Molar mass}}}}{{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{A}}}}} = \frac{{18}}{{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{A}}}}}g{/tex}
There are 8 neutrons in one atom of oxygen
Mass of 8 neutrons = {tex}\frac{8}{{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{A}}}}}{/tex}
Fraction of mass of water due to neutrons {tex}\frac{8}{{18}}{/tex}

Q 20

Does the solubility of a substance change with temperature? Explain with the help of an example.

Solution

Yes, the solubility of a substance change with temperature. The solubility generally, increases with increase in temperature; and decreases on decreasing the temperature.
For example, you can dissolve more sugar in hot water than in cold water.

Q 22

You are provided with a fine white coloured powder which is either sugar or salt. How would you identify it without tasting?

Solution

When salt is heated, it does not get charrd but sugar gets charred on heating. When salt is dissolved in water, the solution conducts electricity. This done not happen with aqueous solution of sugar.

Q 23

Calculate the number of moles of magnesium present in a magnesium ribbon weighing 12 g. Molar atomic mass of magnesium is 24 g mol–1.

Solution

Number of moles = Mass of magnesium ribbon / Molar mass = 12g / 24g = 0.5 mol.
Hence, 0.5 moles of magnesium are present in a magnesium ribbon weighing 12 g.

Q 24

Verify by calculating that

  1. 5 moles of CO2 and 5 moles of H2O do not have the same mass.
  2. 240 g of calcium and 240 g of magnesium elements have a mole ratio of 3 : 5.
Solution

1 Mole of molecules = Gram molecular mass

  1. The molecular mass of CO2  =  (1{tex} \times{/tex} atomic mass of carbon) + (2 {tex} \times{/tex} atomic mass of oxygen).
    = (1{tex} \times{/tex}12) +( 2{tex} \times{/tex}16) = 12+32 = 44g
    CO2 has molar mass = 44 g mol-1
    5 moles of CO2 have molar mass = Gram molecular mass of CO2 {tex} \times{/tex}5  =   44 {tex} \times{/tex} 5 = 220 g
    H2O has molar mass = 18 g mol-1
    5 moles of H2O have mass = 18{tex} \times{/tex}5 g = 90 g
    Hence,  5 moles of CO2 and 5 moles of H2O do not have the same mass. 

  2. Number of moles in 240 g Ca meta = 240/40 = 6
    Number of moles in 240 g of Mg metal = 240/24 = 10
    Ratio is 6:10 or, 3:5

Q 25

Find the ratio of the mass of the combining elements in the following compounds: (You may use Appendix-III)

  1. CaCO3
  2. MgCl2
  3. H2SO4
  4. C2H5OH
  5. NH3
  6. Ca(OH)2
Solution
  1. CaCO3
    Ca : C : O {tex} \times{/tex} 3
    40 : 12 : 16 {tex} \times{/tex} 3
    40 : 12 : 48
    10 : 3 : 12
  2. MgCl2
    Mg : Cl {tex} \times{/tex} 2
    24 : 35.5 {tex} \times{/tex} 2
    24 : 71
  3. H2SO4
    H {tex} \times{/tex} 2 : S : O {tex} \times{/tex} 4
    1 {tex} \times{/tex} 2 : 32 : 16 {tex} \times{/tex} 4
    2 : 32 : 64
    1 : 16 : 32
  4. C2H5OH
    C {tex} \times{/tex} 2 : H {tex} \times{/tex} 6 : O
    12 {tex} \times{/tex} 2 : 1 {tex} \times{/tex} 6 : 16
    24 : 6 : 16
    12 : 3 : 8
  5. NH3
    N : H {tex} \times{/tex} 3
    14 : 1 {tex} \times{/tex} 3
    14 : 3
  6. CaOH2
    Ca : O × 2 : H {tex} \times{/tex} 2
    40 : 16 × 2 : 1 {tex} \times{/tex} 2
    40 : 32 : 2
    20 : 16 : 1
Q 26

Calcium chloride, when dissolved in water, dissociates into its ions according to the following equation.
CaCl2 (aq) {tex} \to {/tex} Ca2+ (aq) + 2Cl- (aq)
Calculate the number of ions obtained from CaCl2 when 222 g of it is dissolved in water.

Solution

1 mole of calcium chloride = Molecular mass of CaCl2 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} Ca +2 {tex}\times{/tex} Cl=1{tex}\times{/tex} 40 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex}35.5= 40 + 71 = 111 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 222 g of CaCl2 is equivalent to 2 moles of CaCl2

Calcium chloride, when dissolved in water, dissociates into its ions as given by chemical equation:
CaCl2 (aq) {tex} \to {/tex} Ca2+ (aq) + 2Cl- (aq)
Since 1 formula unit CaCl2 gives 3 ions

Therefore, 1 mole of CaCl2 will give 3 moles of ions.
So, 2 moles of CaCl2 would give = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 6 moles of ions.
Number of ions = Number of moles of ions {tex}\times{/tex} Avogadro number = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 = 36.132 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 = 3.6132 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 ions.

Q 27

The difference in the mass of 100 moles each of sodium atoms and sodium ions is 5.48002 g. Compute the mass of an electron.

Solution

A sodium atom and ion differ by one electron. For 100 moles each of sodium atoms and ions there would be a difference of 100 moles of electrons.
Mass of 100 moles of electrons = 5.48002 g
Mass of 1 mole of electron = {tex}\frac{{5.48002}}{{100}}g{/tex}
Mass of one electron = {tex}\frac{{5.48002}}{{100 \times 6.022 \times {{10}^{23}}}}{/tex}
=9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-28g
= 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31kg

Q 28

Cinnabar (HgS) is a prominent ore of mercury. How many grams of mercury are present in 225 g of pure HgS? Molar mass of Hg and S are 200.6 g mol-1 and 32 g mol-1 respectively.

Solution

Molar mass of HgS = 200.6 + 32 = 232.6 g mol-1
Mass of Hg in 232.6 g of HgS = 200.6 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of Hg in 225 g of HgS
{tex}=\frac{200.6}{232.6} \times 225{/tex} = 194.04g

Q 29

The mass of one steel screw is 4.11 g. Find the mass of one mole of these steel screws. Compare this value with the mass of the Earth {tex}\left( {5.98 \times {{10}^{24}}kg} \right){/tex}. Which one of the two is heavier and by how many times?

Solution

1 mole of steel screws = {tex}6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}screws
Mass of 1 screw = 4.11 g
Therefore, Mass of 1 mole of screws = {tex}4.11 \times 6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}g
= {tex}24.75 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}g
= {tex}2.475 \times {10^{24}}{/tex}g

One mole of screw weighs = {tex}2.475 \times {10^{24}}{/tex}= {tex}2.475 \times {10^{21}}{/tex}kg


{tex}\frac{{Mass \ of \ the\ Earth}}{{Mass \ of\ 1 \ mole \ of \ screws}}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{{5.98 \times 1{0^{24}}kg}}{{2.475 \times 1{0^{21}}kg}}{/tex}= {tex}2.4 \times 1{0^3}{/tex}


Mass of Earth is {tex}2.4 \times 1{0^3}{/tex}times the mass of screws.
The Earth is 2400 times heavier than one mole of screws.

Q 30

A sample of vitamin C is known to contain 2.58 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 oxygen atoms. How many moles of oxygen atoms are present in the sample?

Solution

1 mole of oxygen atoms = 6.022 × 1023 atoms
Number of moles of oxygen atoms {tex}=\frac{2.58 \times 10^{24}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}=4.28 \mathrm{mol}{/tex}
4.28 moles of oxygen atoms are present in the given sample of vitamin C.

Q 31

Raunak took 5 moles of carbon atoms in a container and Krish also took 5 moles of sodium atoms in another container of same weight.

  1. Whose container is heavier?
  2. Whose container has more number of atoms?
Solution

We know that, 1 mole = Gram atomic mass = Avogadro's number 

  1. Mass of sodium atoms carried by Krish = (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 23) g = 115 g
    Mass of carbon atoms carried by Raunak = (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 12) g = 60 g
    Thus, Krish’s container is heavier than Raunak's container .
  2. Both the bags have same number of atoms as they have same number of moles of atoms.
Q 32

Fill in the missing data in the following table.

Species property H2O CO2 Na atom MgCl2
Number of moles  2 - - 0.5
Number of particles - 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 - -
Mass 36 g - 115 g -
Solution

For H2O
Given, number of moles = 2 and mass = 36 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of particles = number of moles {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 = 1.2044 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024
{tex}\left[\because \text { Number of moles }=\frac{\text { Number of particles }}{\text { Avogadro's number }}\right]{/tex}
For CO2
Given, number of particles = 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of moles of CO2
= {tex}\frac{\text { Number of particles }}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}=\frac{3.011 \times 10^{23}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{/tex} = 0.5 mol
mass of CO2 = moles {tex}\times{/tex} molar mass
= 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 44 = 22g
({tex}\because{/tex} Molar mass of CO2 = 12 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 44)
For Na atom
Given, mass = 115 g
Number of moles = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Molar mass }}=\frac{115}{23}{/tex} = 5 mol
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of particles = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
= 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
For MgCl2
Given, number of moles = 0.5 mole
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of particles = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
=  3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
Mass = number of moles {tex}\times{/tex} molar mass
(Molar mass of MCl2 = 24 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 35.5
= 24 + 71
= 95 g mol-1
= 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 95 = 47.5 g
Thus, the completed table is as

Species property H2O CO2 Na Atom MgCl2
Number of moles  2 0.5 0.5 0.5
Number of particles 12044×1024 3.011×1023 3.011×1024 3.011×1023
Mass 36 g 22g 115 g 47.59g
Q 33

The visible universe is estimated to contain 1022 stars. How many moles of stars are present in the visible universe?

Solution

Given, Number of stars in visible universe = 1022.

One mole= {tex}{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{/tex}

Therefore, Number of moles of stars = {tex}\frac{10^{22}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{/tex} = 0.0166 mol.

What is the SI prefix for each of the following multiples and submultiples of a unit?

  1. 103
  2. 10-1
  3. 10-2
  4. 10-6
  5. 10-9
  6. 10-12

  1. 103 - kilo
  2. 10-1 - deci
  3. 10-2 - centi
  4. 10-6 - micro
  5. 10-9 - nano

Q 35

Express each of the following in kilograms:

  1. 5.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 mg
  2. 58.34 g
  3. 0.584 g
  4. 5.873 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-21 g
Solution
  1. 5.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 kg
  2. 5.834 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 kg
  3. 5.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 kg
  4. 5.873 - 10-24 kg
Q 36

Compute the difference in masses of 103 moles each of magnesium atoms and magnesium ions. (Mass of an electron = 9.1 × 10-31 kg)

Solution

Mg2+ ion and Mg atom differ by two electrons.
103 moles of Mg2+ and Mg atoms would differ by 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 moles of electrons.
Mass of 2 {tex}\times{/tex}103 moles of electrons
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.023 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 kg
= 109.6004 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 kg
= 1.096 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 kg

Q 37

Which has more number of atoms? 100 g of N2 or 100 g of NH3 

Solution

Mass of nitrogen molecule = 28 g/mol
So, number of molecules in 100 g nitrogen = {tex}\frac {100g}{28g}{/tex} mole
Hence, no. of atoms in 100 g nitrogen
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {100g}{28g}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 = 43.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
Mass of ammonia molecule = 14 + 3 = 17 g/mole
Number of atoms in 1 molecule of ammonia = 3
Number of atoms in 100 g ammonia
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {100g}{17g}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 = 141.69 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
This shows that 100 g of ammonia has more number of atoms than 100 g nitrogen.

Q 38

Compute the number of ions present in 5.85 g of sodium chloride.

Solution

molecular mass of NaCl = mass of Na atom + mass of Cl atom

= 23 + 35.5

= 58.5 g

5.85 g of NaCl = mass of sodium chloride/ molecular mass ofNaCl ={tex}\frac{{5.85}}{{58.5}}{/tex}= 0.1 moles
or 0.1 moles of NaCl particle.
Each NaCl particle is equivalent to 2 ions, i.e., one Na+ and one Cl-
Total moles of ions = {tex}0.1 \times 2{/tex}= 0.2 moles
Number of ions = {tex}0.2 \times 6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}
= {tex}1.2042 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}ions.

Q 39

A gold sample contains 90 percent of gold and the rest copper. How many atoms of gold are present in one gram of this sample of gold?

Solution

One gram of gold sample will contain =90/100 = 0.9 g of gold
Number of moles of gold = = {tex}\frac{{0.9}}{{197}}{/tex}= 0.0046
One mole of gold contains NA atoms = {tex}{\rm{6}}{\rm{.022}} \times {\rm{1}}{{\rm{0}}^{23}}{/tex}atoms
Therefore, 0. 0046 moles of gold will contain = {tex}0.0046 \times 6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}
= {tex}2.77 \times {10^{21}}{/tex}atoms

Q 40

What are ionic and molecular compounds? Give examples.

Solution

Ionic compound: The ionic compounds which are made, up of ions are known as an ionic compound. For example, sodium chloride(NaCl)is an ionic compound which is made up of an equal number of positively charged sodium ions (Na+) and negatively charged chloride ions (Cl- ).

Molecular compounds are formed by the combination of two different non-metals element. example; H2 S, CO2.

Q 41

Compute the difference in masses of one mole each of aluminium atoms and one mole of its ions. (Mass of an electron is {tex}9.1 \times {10^{ - 28}}g{/tex}). Which one is heavier?

Solution

Mass of 1 mole of the aluminium atom = Molar mass of aluminium = 27 g mol-1.
An aluminium atom needs to lose three electrons to become an ion, Al3+.
For one mole of Al3+ ion, three moles of electrons are to be lost.
The mass of three moles of electrons = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} (9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-28) {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023g
= 27.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 g
= 164.400 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5g
= 0.00164 g
Molar mass of Al3+ = (27 - 0.00164) g
= 26.9984 g
Difference = 27 - 26.9984
= 0.0016 g

Q 42

A silver ornament of mass m gram is polished with a gold equivalent to 1% of the mass of silver. Compute the ratio of the number of atoms of gold and silver in the ornament.

Solution

Given, Mass of silver = m g
Mass of gold =1% of the mass of Ag =  {tex}\frac{m}{100}{/tex}g
Number of atoms of silver = {tex}\frac{Mass \ of \ silver}{Atomic \ mass \ of \ silver}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}NA = {tex}\frac{m}{108}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex}NA 
Number of atoms of gold = {tex}\frac{m}{100 \times 197}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}NA 
Ratio of number of atoms of gold to silver in ornament = Number of atoms of Au : Number of atoms of Ag ={tex}\frac{m}{100 \times 197}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}NA : {tex}\frac{m}{108}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex}NA = 108 : 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 197 = 108 : 19700 = 1 : 182.41

Therefore, Ratio  of number of atoms of gold to silver in ornament = 1 : 182.41

Q 43

A sample of ethane (C2H6) gas has the same mass as 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 molecules of methane (CH4). How many C2H6 molecules does the sample of gas contain?

Solution

Molecular Mass of methane, CH4 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} C + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} H = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 12+4= 16 u

Molecular mass of ethane, C2H6 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} C +6 {tex}\times{/tex}H= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 =24 +6 =30u

Mass of 1 molecule of methane,CH4 = {tex}\frac{Molecular \ Mass}{\ Avogadro's \ number}{/tex}={tex}\frac{16 \mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}}{/tex}
Mass of 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 molecules of methane = {tex}\frac{1.5 \times 10^{20} \times 16}{N_{A}} g{/tex}
Mass of 1 molecule of ethane, C2H6 ={tex}\frac{Molecular \ Mass}{\ Avogadro's \ number}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{30}{N_A}{/tex}g
Mass of 1.5 × 1020  molecules of ethane, C2H6 = {tex}\frac{1.5 \times 10^{20} \times 16}{N_A}{/tex}g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of molecules of ethane ={tex}\frac{1.5 \times 10^{20} \times 16}{N_A}{/tex}=  {tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\frac{N_A}{30}{/tex} = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 

Q 44

In a chemical reaction, the sum of the masses of the reactants and products remains unchanged. This is called ________.

Q 45

A group of atoms carrying a fixed charge on them is called ________.

Q 46

The formula unit mass of Ca3 (PO4)2 is ________.

Q 47

Formula of sodium carbonate is ________ and that of ammonium sulphate is ________.

Q 48

Complete the following crossword puzzle (Fig.) by using the name of the chemical elements. Use the data given in Table.

Across Down
2. The element used by Rutherford during his a-scattering experiment. 1. A white lustrous metal used for making ornaments and which tends to get tarnished black in the presence of moist air
3. An element which forms rust on exposure to moist air 4. Both brass and bronze are alloys of the element
5. A very reactive non–metal stored underwater 6. The metal which exists in the liquid state at room temperature
7. Zinc metal, when treated with dilute hydrochloric acid produces gas of this element which when tested with burning splinter produces a pop sound. 8. An element with symbol Pb

Solution

The complete crossword puzzle is as follows:

Across Down
2. Gold 1. Silver
3. Iron 4. Copper
5. Phosphorus 6. Mercury
7. Hydrogen 8. Lead

Q 49
  1. In this crossword puzzle (Fig), the names of 11 elements are hidden. The symbols of these are given below. Complete the puzzle.
    1. Cl
    2. H
    3. Ar
    4. O
    5. Xe
    6. N
    7. He
    8. F
    9. Kr
    10. Rn
    11. Ne

  1. Identify the total number of inert gases, their names, and symbols from this crossword puzzle.
Solution
  1. The names of these elements are shown as below:
    1. Cl - Chlorine
    2. H - Hydrogen
    3. Ar - Argon
    4. O - Oxygen
    5. Xe - Xenon
    6. N - Nitrogen
    7. He - Helium
    8. F - Fluorine
    9. Kr - Krypton
    10. Rn - Radon
    11. Ne - Neon

  1. The total number of inert gases in this crossword puzzle is 6. Their names and symbols are as mentioned below:
S. No. Name of gas Symbol
1 Helium He
2 Neon Ne
3 Argon Ar
4 Krypton Kr
5 Xenon Xe
6 Radon Rn
Q 50

Write the formulae for the following and calculate the molecular mass for each one of them.

  1. Caustic potash
  2. Baking soda 
  3. Limestone
  4. Caustic soda 
  5. Ethanol
  6. Common salt
Solution
  1. Caustic potash (KOH)
    Molecular mass of KOH = 39 + 16 + 1 = 56 u
  2. Baking powder (NaHCO3)
    Molecular mass of NaHCO3 = 23 + 1+ 12+3 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 23 + 1 + 12 + 48 = 84 u
  3. Lime stone (CaCO3)
    Molecular mass of CaCO3 =  40 + 12 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex}16 = 100 u
  4. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH)
    Molecular mass of NaOH = 23 + 16 + 1 = 40u 
  5. Ethanol (C2H5OH)
    Molecular mass of C2H5OH or C2H6O
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 16 = 24 + 6 + 16 = 46u
  6. Sodium chloride (NaCI)
    Molecular mass of NaCl = 23 + 35.5 = 58.5 u 
Q 51

In photosynthesis, 6 molecules of carbon dioxide combine with an equal number of water molecules through a complex series of reactions to give a molecule of glucose having a molecular formula C6H12O6. How many grams of water would be required to produce 18 g of glucose? Compute the volume of water so consumed assuming the density of water to be 1g cm-3.

Solution

The chemical reaction occurs during photosynthesis is given below: 

{tex}6C{O_2} + 6{H_2}O\xrightarrow[{Sunlight}]{{Chlorophyll}}{C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6} + 6{O_2}{/tex}

From balanced chemical equation, it is clear that;
1 mole of glucose needs 6 moles of water
180 g of glucose needs (6 {tex}\times{/tex}18) g of water ( M.M of glucose = 180 u and M.M. of water = 18 u)
1 g of glucose will need {tex}\frac{108}{180}{/tex}g of water.
18g of glucose would need {tex}\frac{108}{180}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex}18g of water = 10.8 g
Therefore, Volume of water used = {tex}\frac{Mass}{Density}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{10.8 g}{1 g cm^{-3}}{/tex} = 10.8 cm3

Q 1

Which of the following correctly represent the electronic distribution in the Mg atom?

Q 2

Rutherford’s alpha ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) particles scattering experiment’ resulted into the discovery of _________ in the atom.

Q 3

The number of electrons in an element x is 15 and the number of neutrons is 16. Which of the following is the correct representation of the element ?

Q 4

Dalton’s atomic theory successfully explained

  1. Law of conservation of mass
  2. Law of constant composition
  3. Law of radioactivity
  4. Law of multiple proportions
Q 5

Which of the following statements about Rutherford’s model of atom are correct?

  1. considered the nucleus as positively charged
  2. established that the {tex}\alpha{/tex}–particles are four times as heavy as a hydrogen atom
  3. can be compared to the solar system
  4. was in agreement with Thomson’s model
Q 6

Which of the following are true for an element?

  1. Atomic number = number of protons + number of electrons
  2. Mass number = number of protons + number of neutrons
  3. Atomic mass = number of protons = number of neutrons
  4. Atomic number = number of protons = number of electrons
Q 7

In Thomson’s model of the atom, which of the following statements are correct?

  1. the mass of the atom is assumed to be uniformly distributed over the atom
  2. the positive charge is assumed to be uniformly distributed over the atom
  3. the electrons are uniformly distributed in the positively charged sphere
  4. the electrons attract each other to stabilise the atom
Q 8

Rutherford’s {tex}\alpha{/tex}–particle scattering experiment showed that

  1. electrons have negative charge
  2. the mass and positive charge of the atom is concentrated in the nucleus
  3. neutron exists in the nucleus
  4. most of the space in atom is empty

Which of the above statements are correct?

Q 9

The ion of an element has 3 positive charges. Mass number of the atom is 27 and the number of neutrons is 14. What is the number of electrons in the ion?

Q 10

Identify the Mg2+ ion from the figure where, n and p represent the number of neutrons and protons respectively.

Q 11

In a sample of ethyl ethanoate (CH3COOC2H5) the two oxygen atoms have the same number electrons but different number of neutrons. Which of the following is the correct reason for it ?

Q 12

Elements with valency 1 are

Q 13

The first model of an atom was given by

Q 14

An atom with 3 protons and 4 neutrons will have valency of

Q 15

The electron distribution in an aluminium atom is

Q 16

Which of the following in the figure do not represent Bohr's model of an atom correctly?

Q 17

Which of the following statement is always correct?

Q 18

Atomic models have been improved over the years. Arrange the following atomic models in the order of their chronological order

  1. Rutherford’s atomic model
  2. Thomson’s atomic model
  3. Bohr’s atomic model
Q 19

Is it possible for the atom of an element to have one electron, one proton and no neutron. If so, name the element.

Solution

Yes, hydrogen is such element.

Q 20

Write any two observations which support the fact that atoms are divisible.

Solution

Discovery of electrons and protons led the scientists to discover that atoms are divisible.

Q 21

Will 35Cl and 37Cl have different valencies? Justify your answer.

Solution

35Cl and 37Cl are isotopes of the same element - chlorine. Isotopes have the same number of electrons. Hence, their valencies do not differ. They have the same valency.

Q 22

Why did Rutherford select a gold foil in his {tex}\alpha{/tex}–ray scattering experiment?

Solution

For the scattering experiment, Rutherford wanted a metal sheet which could be as thin as possible. Gold is the most malleable of all known metals. It can easily be converted into very thin sheets. Hence, Rutherford selected a gold foil for his alpha-ray scattering experiment.

Q 23

Find out the valency of the atoms represented by the fig. (a) and (b).

Solution

Atom (a) has zero valency since it has 8 electrons in the valence shell and has achieved  a stable configuration. Atom (b) has a valency of 1 since it has 7 electrons in the valence shell.
Atom (b) can accept on more electron in order to achieve a stable (octet) configuration.

Q 24

One electron is present in the outer most shell of the atom of an element X. What would be the nature and value of charge on the ion formed if this electron is removed from the outer most shell?

Solution

When the single electron present in the outer most shell of the atom of an element X is removed, the atom loses negative charge and forms a positively charged ion with +1 charge. The value of the net charge on the ion will be equal to the amount of charge present on one electron.

Q 25

Write down the electron distribution of chlorine atom. How many electrons are there in the L shell? (Atomic number of chlorine is 17).

Solution

Since the atomic number (Z) of chlorine is 17, the electronic configuration of chlorine will be 2, 8, 7. There are eight electrons in the L shell (second shell).

Q 26

In the atom of an element X, 6 electrons are present in the outermost shell. If it acquires noble gas configuration by accepting requisite number of electrons, then what would be the charge on the ion so formed?

Solution

Since 6 electrons are already present in the outermost shell of the atom, this element requires two more electrons in order to acquire the noble gas configuration. When the atom accepts 2  more electrons , the charge on the ion formed would be -2.

Q 27

What information do you get from the figure about the atomic number, mass number and valency of atoms X, Y and Z?
Give your answer in a tabular form.

Solution

The tabular form is as below:

Element Atomic Number (= no. of p) Mass Number {= no. of (p+n)} Number of Electrons (= no. of p) Electronic Configuration Valency
X 5 5 + 6 = 11 5 2, 3 3
Y 8 8 + 10 = 18 8 2, 6 2
Z 15 15 + 16 = 31 15 2, 8, 5 3, 5
Q 28

In response to a question, a student stated that in an atom, the number of protons is greater than the number of neutrons, which in turn is greater than the number of electrons. Do you agree with the statement? Justify your answer.

Solution

The statement of the student is not correct. The number of protons in an atom can never be greater than the number of neutrons. The number of protons will be equal to or less than the number of neutrons. The number of electrons and protons are always equal in a neutral atom.

Q 29

Calculate the number of neutrons present in the nucleus of an element 'X' which is represented as {tex}_{15}^{31}X{/tex}.

Solution

From the given symbol of 'X', we have mass number = 31 and atomic number = 15.

No. of protons = Atomic number = 15 ... (1)

Mass number = 31

or No. of protons + No. of neutrons = 31

or Number of neutrons = 31 - Number of protons

or Number of neutrons = 31 - 15 [From (1)]

or Number of neutrons = 16

The number of neutrons present in the nucleus of an element  {tex}_{15}^{31}X{/tex} is 16.

Q 30

Match the names of the Scientists given in column A with their contributions towards the understanding of the atomic structure as given in column B 

(A) (B)
(a) Ernest Rutherford (i) Indivisibility of atoms
(b) J.J.Thomson (ii) Stationary orbits
(c) Dalton (iii) Concept of nucleus
(d) Neils Bohr (iv) Discovery of electrons
(e) James Chadwick (v) Atomic number
(f) E. Goldstein (vi) Neutron
(g) Mosley (vii) Canal rays
Solution

 The correct order of match is given as (a) - (iii), (b) - (iv), (c) - (i), (d) - (ii), (e) - (vi), (f) - (vii), (g) - (v).

Q 31

The atomic number of calcium and argon are 20 and 18 respectively, but the mass number of both these elements is 40. What is the name given to such a pair of elements?

Solution

Elements with the same mass number but different atomic numbers are called isobars.

Q 32

Complete the table on the basis of information available in the symbols given below.

  1. {tex}^{35}_{17}Cl{/tex}
  2. {tex}^{12}_{6}C{/tex}
  3. {tex}^{81}_{35}Cl{/tex}
    Element Np nn
         
Solution

Atomic number is written on the lower left side of the symbol of element whereas mass number is written on the upper left side of the symbol of element. Number of protons (np) = Atomic number of atoms and number of neutrons (nn) = Mass number - atomic number.

 

Element

 Np nn
(i) {tex}^{35}_{17}Cl{/tex} 17 35 - 17 = 18
(ii) {tex}^{12}_{6}C{/tex} 6 12 - 6 = 6
(iii) {tex}^{81}_{35}Cl{/tex} 35 81 - 35 = 46
Q 33

Helium atom has 2 electrons in its valence shell but its valency is not 2. Explain.

Solution

Helium atom has 2 electrons in its outermost shell and its duplet is complete. Hence the valency of helium atom is zero. It has no propensity to give or take electrons.

Q 34

Rutherford’s {tex}\alpha{/tex} - particle scattering experiment led to the discovery of the ________.

Q 35

Isotopes have same ________ but different ________.

Q 36

Neon and chlorine have atomic numbers 10 and 17 respectively. Their valencies will be ________ and ________ respectively.

Q 37

The electronic configuration of silicon is ________ and that of sulphur is ________.

Q 38

An element 'X' has mass number 4 and atomic number 2. Write the valency of this element. Will it react with other atoms of different elements?

Solution

Only valence electrons of an atom take part in bond formation with different atoms. In the atom of element ‘X’, there are only two electrons since atomic number is 2 and the number of electrons is equal to the number of protons. Thus, K shell for this atom is fully filled. Hence, its valency is zero. Atoms of element ‘X’ will not react with other atoms of different elements.

Q 39

Why do helium, neon and argon have a zero valency?

Solution

Helium has two electrons (duplet configuration) in its only energy shell, while neon and argon have eight electrons (octet configuration) in their valence shells. As these have the maximum number of electron in their valence shells, they do not have any tendency to combine with other elements. Hence, they have zero valency.

Q 40

The ratio of the radii of the hydrogen atom and its nucleus is {tex}\sim{/tex}105. Assuming the atom and the nucleus to be spherical,

  1. What will be the ratio of their sizes?
  2. If an atom is represented by planet Earth 'Re'= 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m. Estimate the size of the nucleus.
Solution
  1. As atom and nucleus is considered to be spherical.Volume of the sphere = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}{tex}\pi{/tex}r3 
     Let R be the radius of the atom and r be that of the nucleus.
    Here, {tex}\frac Rr=\;10^5\;\;\;\;\;{/tex} (Given)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = 105 r 
    Volume of the atom of radius 'R'  = {tex}\frac{4}{3}\pi{/tex}R3
    Volume of atom = {tex}\frac{4}{3}\pi{/tex} (105r)3                   ({tex}\because{/tex} R = 105r)
    Or, Volume of the atom  ={tex}\frac{4}{3}\pi{/tex}r3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 
    Now, Volume of the nucleus = {tex}\frac{4}{3}\pi{/tex}r3  
    Therefore, Ratio of the size of atom to that of nucleus = {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{3} \times 10^{15} \times \pi r^{3}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}}{/tex} = 1015 
  2.  If the atom is represented by the planet Earth (Re = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex}106m), then the radius of the nucleus would be 
    rn = {tex}\frac{R_e}{10^{15}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.4 \times 10^6m}{10^5}{/tex} = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10m = 64 m.
Q 41

Enlist the conclusions drawn by Rutherford from his {tex}\alpha{/tex}-ray scattering experiment.

Solution

Rutherford concluded from the α-particle scattering experiment that:

  1. Most of the space inside the atom was empty because most of the α-particles passed through the gold foil without any deflection.
  2. Very few particles were deflected from their path, indicating that the positive charge occupied very little space inside the atom.
  3. A very small fraction of α-particles were deflected by 180o, indicating that the positive charge and the mass of the gold atom were concentrated in a very small volume within the atom.

From the data, he also calculated that the radius of the nucleus was about 105 times smaller than the radius of the atom.

Q 42

In what way is Rutherford’s atomic model different from that of Thomson’s atomic model?

Solution
Thomson's Model Rutherford's Model
Atom was similar to Christmas pudding. Atom is spherical with lot of empty space.
The whole atom is positively charged; with electrons embedded in positively charged body. Positive charge of atom is centred in the nucleus; with electrons revolving around the nucleus.
Mass of atom is spread throughout the atom. Mass of atom is centred in nucleus.
Q 43

What were the drawbacks of Rutherford’s model of an atom?

Solution

Rutherford had proposed a model in which the electrons revolve around the nucleus in well-defined orbits. However, the orbital revolution of the electron was not expected to be stable. Any particle in a circular orbit would undergo acceleration and the charged particles would radiate energy. Thus, the revolving electrons would lose energy and finally fall into the nucleus. Revolution of electrons around the atom would make the atom highly unstable and the matter would not exist. But this is contrary to our common observations.

Q 44

What are the postulates of Bohr’s model of an atom?

Solution

The postulates put forth by Neils Bohr’s about the model of an atom are as follows:-

  1. Only certain special orbits known as discrete orbits, are allowed inside the atom. These discrete orbits are called energy levels. Energy levels in an atom are shown by concentric circles. These orbits are represented by the letters K, L, M, N, … or the numbers, n = 1, 2, 3, 4, ….
  2. While revolving in the discrete orbits, the electrons do not radiate any energy.
Q 45

Show diagrammatically the electron distribution in a sodium atom and a sodium ion and also give their atomic number.

Solution

The atomic number of sodium atom is 11. Therefore, it has 11 electrons. K shell can have a maximum of 2 electrons. L shell accommodates a maximum number of 8 electrons. M shell will have 1 electron (remaining electron = 11 - 2 - 8).
A positively charged sodium ion (Na+) is formed by the removal of one electron from a sodium atom. So, a sodium ion has 11 - 1 = 10 electrons. Thus, electronic distribution of a sodium ion will be 2, 8 (2 + 8 = 10).
The atomic number of an element is equal to the number of protons in its nucleus. A sodium atom, as well as a sodium ion, contains the same number of protons. Therefore, both of them have an atomic number of 11.

Q 46

In the gold foil experiment of Geiger and Marsden, that paved the way for Rutherford’s model of an atom, {tex}\sim{/tex}1.00% of the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles were found to deflect at angles > 50°. If one mole of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles were bombarded on the gold foil, compute the number of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles that would deflect at angles less than 50°.

Solution

The total of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles used for bombardment = 1 mole = 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex}1023 particles.

Percentage  of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles deflected more than 50° = 1% of a-particles. (Given)
Percentage of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles deflected less than 50° = 100 - 1 = 99%
Actual number of particles deflected at an angle less than 50°= {tex}\frac{99}{100}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex}1023 = {tex}\frac{596.178}{100}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex}1023  = 5.96 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
