Q 29

Select the odd one out

Q 30

Why are lysosomes known as suicide bags?

Solution

Lysosomes are a kind of waste disposal system of the cell. Lysosomes contain digestive enzymes against all types of organic materials. If their covering membrane breaks as it happens during injury to cell, the digestive enzymes will spill over the cell contents and digest the same. Therefore, lysosomes are called suicide bags as they can kill the cells possessing them.

Q 31

Do you agree "A cell is a building unit of an organism". If yes, explain why.

Solution

An organism is made up of various organ systems like digestive system, nervous system, etc. These organ systems in turn are made up of various organs which are made up of tissues. Also tissues are a group of cells performing the same function. Hence, a cell is the building unit of an organism.
Cell -> tissue -> organ -> organ system -> organism

Q 32

Why does the skin of your fingers shrinks when you wash clothes for a long time?

Solution

The solution of soaps and detergent is hypertonic as compared to the osmotic concentration of our skin. Therefore, the washing solution results in exosmosis in skin cells that come in contact with it for some time. Due to this reason, the skin over the fingers shrinks, while washing clothes for a long time.

Q 33

Why is endocytosis found in animals only?

Solution

For endocytosis to occur, the outermost membrance should be flexible. But in plant cells, cell wall is the outermost membrane which is very rigid. Hence, no endocytosis occurs in plants.

Q 34

A person takes concentrated solution of salt. After sometime he starts vomiting. What is the phenomenon responsible for such a situation? Explain.

Solution

Solution of salt is hypertonic solution, so it causes irritation and excessive dehydration in stomach due to exosmosis, which make the person uncomfortable causing reverse movements and thus vomiting.

Q 35

Name any cell organelle which is non-membranous.

Solution

Ribosome

Q 36

We eat food composed of all the nutrients like carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals and water. After digestion, these are absorbed in the form of glucose, aminoacids, fatty acids, glycerol etc. What mechanisms are involved in absorption of digested food and water?

Solution

Digested food and water are absorbed by the process of diffusion and osmosis.

Q 37

If you are provided with some vegetables to cook, we generally add salt to the vegetables during cooking process. After adding salt, vegetables releases water. What mechanism is responsible for this?

Solution

After adding salt, the concentration of solvent decreases, hence water form vegetable move out from higher concentration to lower concentration.

Q 38

If cell of onion peel and RBC are separately placed in hypotonic solution, what among the following will take place? Explain the reason.

  1. Both the cells will swell
  2. RBC will burst early while cells of onion peel will resist bursting to some extent
  3. Both a and b are correct
  4. RBC and onion peel cells will behave similarly
Solution

(b) RBC will burst early whereas onion peel cell well resist bursting to some extent. This is due to the presence of cell wall.

Q 39

Bacteria do not have chloroplast but some bacteria are photoautotrophic in nature and perform photosynthesis. Which part of the bacterial cell performs this?

Solution

Small vesicles that are associated with the plasma membrane are present in such bacteria. These vesicles contain pigments that can trap solar energy to produce food.

Q 40

Match the following A and B

(A) (B)
(a) Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (i) Amoeba
(b) Lysosome (ii) Nucleus
(c) Nucleoid (iii) Bacteria
(d) Food vacuoles (iv) Detoxification
(e) Chromatin material and nucleolus (v) Suicidal bag
Solution

The correct order of matchi is given as (a)-(iv), (b)-(v), (c)-(iii), (d)-(i), (e)-(ii)

Q 41

Write the name of different plant parts in which chromoplast, chloroplast and leucoplast are present.

Solution

Flower and Fruit - Chromoplast,
Leaves of the plant - Chloroplast,
Root of the plant - Leucoplast

Q 42

Name the organelle which shows the analogy as transporting channels of the cell.

Solution

Endoplasmic reticulum

Q 43

Name the organelle which show the analogy as power house of the cell.

Solution

The organelle which shows the analogy as a powerhouse of the cell is Mitochondria.

Q 44

Name the organelle which show the analogy as packaging and dispatching unit of the cell.

Solution

The organelle that shows the analogy as the packaging and dispatching unit of the cell is Golgi complex.

Q 45

Name the organelle which shows the analogy as a digestive bag of the cell.

Solution

The organelle that shows the analogy as a digestive bag of the cell is the lysosome.

Q 46

Name the organelle which shows the analogy as storage sacs of the cell.

Solution

The organelle that shows the analogy as storage sacs of the cell is vacuole.

Q 47

Name the organelle which shows the analogy as kitchen of the cell.

Solution

The organelle which shows the analogy as the kitchen of the cell is the chloroplast.

Q 48

Name the organelle which shows the analogy as control room of the cell.

Solution

Nucleus is the organelle which shows the analogy as control room of the cell.

Q 49

How is a bacterial cell different from an onion peel cell?

Solution
Bacterial cell Onion peel cell
The cell wall is made of peptidoglycan. The cell wall is made of cellulose.
The nucleus is absent. The nucleus is present.
The vacuole is absent. The vacuole is present.
These are prokaryotes. These are eukaryotes.
Q 50

How do substances like CO2 and water move in and out of the cell? Discuss.

Solution

The exchange of gases (CO2 and O2) between cells takes place by diffusion process.
Diffusion is defined as movement of a substance from a region of higher concentration to lower concentration. CO2 is formed inside cell due to respiration and it accumulates in the cell so its concentration is high in the cell as compared to the surroundings. On the other hand O2 is utilized inside cell during respiration and therefore its concentration decreases inside the cell but remains comparatively high in the surroundings. As a result CO2 diffuses out and O2) diffuses into the cell.

Q 51

How does an Amoeba obtain its food?

Solution

Amoeba feeds on microorganisms (like planktons) which float on water. It develops false feet or pseudopodia to surround the food finally captures the food within a sac-like structure called the food vacuole inside which digestion of food takes place.

Q 52

Name the two organelles in a plant cell that contain their own genetic material and ribosomes.

Solution

Mitochondria and plastids are the two organelles that contain their own genetic material. Both these organelles have their own DNA and ribosome.

Q 53

Why are lysosomes also known as scavengers of the cells?

Solution

Lysosomes contain enzymes that are used for destroying worn out parts of the cell. Lysosomes also destroy waste materials. Due to this, lysosomes are also known as ‘scavengers of the cells’.

Q 54

Which cell organelle controls most of the activities of the cell?

Solution

Nucleus

Q 55

Which kind of plastid is more common in roots of the plant.

Solution

Roots of the plant contain leucoplast which are colourless plastids and act as storage of food.

Q 56

Which kind of plastid is more common in leaves of the plant?

Solution

The kind of plastid that is more common in the leaves of the plant is Chloroplast.

Q 57

Which kind of plastid is more common in flowers and fruits?

Solution

 The kind of plastid that is more common in flowers and fruits is Chromoplast.

Q 58

Why do plant cells possess large-sized vacuole?

Solution

Large vacuoles contain cell sap which helps in maintaining the turgidity of a cell. This is especially important for plant cells because they are always exposed to the vagaries of the environment. Vacuole also stores important substances in them.

Q 59
How are chromatin, chromatid and chromosomes related to each other?
Solution
Chromatin is a thin thread-like structure which is composed of DNA (deoxy ribonucleic acid) and proteins to form a rod-like chromatid. Two similar chromatids attach to a centromere to form a chromosome.
Q 60

What are the consequences of the condition of a cell containing higher water concentration than the surrounding medium?

Solution

The cell will lose water and will shrink, i.e. exosmosis.

Q 61

What are the consequences of a cell having lower water concentration than the surrounding medium?

Solution

If a cell having lower water concentration than the surrounding medium then the cell will gain water and will swell, i.e. endosmosis.

Q 62

What are the consequences of a cell having equal water concentration to its surrounding medium?

Solution

If a cell having equal water concentration to its surrounding medium then there will be no movement of water across the cell membrane as the isotonic condition persists in the solvent.

Q 63

Draw a plant cell and label the parts which

  1. determines the function and development of the cell
  2. packages materials coming from the endoplasmic reticulum
  3. provides resistance to microbes to withstand hypotonic external media without bursting
  4. is site for many biochemical reactions necessary to sustain life.
  5. is a fluid contained inside the nucleus
Solution
  1. Nucleus
  2. Golgi apparatus
  3. Cell wall
  4. Cytoplasm
  5. Nucleoplasm.

Figure: A plant cell

Q 64

Illustrate only a plant cell as seen under electron microscope. How is it different from animal cell?

Solution
Animal cell Plant cell
Cell wall absent. Cell wall present.
Vacuoles are small. Vacuoles are large.
Chloroplast absent. Chloroplast present.
Q 65

Draw a neat labelled diagram of an animal cell.

Solution

Diagram of an animal cell is given below:

Q 66

Draw a well-labeled diagram of a eukaryotic nucleus. How is it different from the nucleoid?

Solution

Figure: An eukaryotic nucleus

Nucleus Nucleoid
Nuclear membrane is present. Nuclear membrane absent.
Nucleolus present. Nucleolus absent.
Genetic materials are enclosed in nucleus. Genetic materials are not contained in any closed structure.
Found in eukaryotes. Found in prokaryotes.
Q 67

Differentiate between rough and smooth endoplasmic reticulum. How is the endoplasmic reticulum important for membrane biogenesis?

Solution
Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum Smooth Endoplasmic Reticulum
Ribosomes are present on the surface. Ribosomes are absent.
Involved in the synthesis of protein. Involved in the synthesis of fat and lipid.

ER makes lipid and protein which are the two important constituents of the plasma membrane. The biogenesis of the plasma membrane is dependent on the endoplasmic reticulum.

Q 68

In brief, state what happens when dry apricots are left for some time in pure water and later transferred to sugar solution?

Solution

When dry apricots are left for some time in pure water, the apricots become swollen because they absorb water due to osmosis. After that, when the swollen apricots are kept in a sugar solution, they lose water because of exosmosis and shrink again.

Q 69

In brief state what happens when a Red Blood Cell is kept in a concentrated saline solution?

Solution

When a Red Blood Cell is kept in a concentrated saline solution, it loses water and leads plasmolysis in the RBC.

Q 70

In brief state what happens when the plasma-membrane of a cell breaks down?

Solution

If the plasma membrane ruptures or breakdown then the cell will not be able to exchange material from its surrounding by diffusion or osmosis. Thereafter the protoplasmic material will be disappeared and the cell will die.

Q 71

In brief state what happens when Rheo leaves are boiled in water first and then a drop of sugar syrup is put on it?

Solution

When Rheo leaves are boiled in water first and then a drop of sugar syrup is put on it, osmosis does not occur due to the death of the cells of the leaf.

Q 72

In brief state what happens when Golgi apparatus is removed from the cell?

Solution

The Golgi apparatus is responsible for the transport of proteins and lipids and fatty materials to other cell organelles. If it is removed some cell organelles will stop working.

Q 73

Draw a neat diagram of plant cell and label any three parts which differentiate it from an animal cell.

Solution

Figure: A plant cell

Q 34

Animals of colder regions and fishes of cold water have a thicker layer of subcutaneous fat. Describe why?

Solution

The subcutaneous fat or adipose tissue lies just below the skin surface or in between internal organs. Being a poor conductor of heat it acts as a good insulator. It reduces the heat loss from the body, i.e., regulates the body temperature.

Q 35

Match the column (A) with the column (B)

Column A Column B
(a) Fluid connective tissue (i) Subcutaneous layer
(b) Filling of space inside the organs (ii) Cartilage
(c) Striated muscle (iii) Skeletal muscle
(d) Adipose tissue (iv) Areolar tissue
(e) Surface of joints (v) Blood
(f) Stratified squamous epithelium (vi) Skin
Solution

The correct order for match is (a) - (v), (b) - (iv), (c) - (iii), (d) - (i), (e) - (ii), (f) - (vi).

Q 36

Match the column (A) with the column (B)

Column A Column B
(a) Parenchyma (i) Carbon fixation
(b) Photosynthesis (ii) Sclerenchyma
(c) Aerenchyma (iii) Localized thickening
(d) Collenchyma (iv) Buoyancy
(e) Permanent tissue (v) Thin-walled, packing cells
Solution

The correct order for match is (a) - (v), (b) - (i), (c) - (iv), (d) - (iii), (e) - (ii).

Q 37

If a potted plant is covered with a glass jar, water vapours appear on the walls of the glass jar. Explain why?

Solution

When a potted plant is covered with a glass jar, water lost by the plant appears on the walls of the glass jar as water vapours. Plants always lose water to the atmosphere from the surface of the leaves. This process is known as transpiration. Water reaches leaves by xylem vessels, where evaporation takes place through the stomata.

Q 38

Name the different components of xylem and draw a living component?

Solution

Xylem consists of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma, and xylem fibers.

Q 39

Draw and identify different elements of phloem.

Solution

Elements of phloem: Sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem fibers and phloem parenchyma.

Q 40

Epithelial tissue is protective tissue in animal body.

Q 41

The lining of blood vessels, lung alveoli, and kidney tubules are all made up of epithelial tissue.

Q 42

Epithelial cells have a lot of intercellular spaces.

Q 43

The epithelial layer is a permeable layer.

Q 44

The epithelial layer does not allow the regulation of materials between the body and external environment.

Q 45

Differentiate between voluntary and involuntary muscles. Give one example of each.

Solution

Voluntary Muscles

Involuntary Muscles
The voluntary muscles can be moved according to our conscious will. The involuntary muscles do not move according to our will.
These muscles are mostly attached to the bones and help in body movement. Hence,they are also called skeletal muscles. These muscles are mostly attached to visceral organs like the alimentary canal.
They show striation (alternate light and dark bands); hence, they are also called striated muscles. They do not show striation; hence, also called smooth muscles or unstriated muscles.
The cells of this tissue are cylindrical. The cells of this tissue are long with pointed ends (spindle-shaped).
E.g. Muscles of the limbs E.g. Muscles of the heart / Iris of the eye
Q 46

Differentiate the following activities on the basis of voluntary (V) or involuntary (IV) muscles.

  1. Jumping of frog
  2. Pumping of the heart
  3. Writing with hand
  4. Movement of chocolate in your intestine
Solution
(a) Jumping of frog (a) V
(b) Pumping of the heart (b) IV
(c) Writing with hand (c) V
(d) Movement of chocolate in your intestine (d) IV

Explanation: Jumping of frog and writing with hand are voluntary activities carried out with the help of voluntary muscles. Our limbs move when we want them to move. Pumping of the heart involves involuntary cardiac muscles. Movement of chocolate in the intestine is due to peristalsis – a wave-like contraction and expansion of involuntary muscles in the alimentary canal (digestive system).

Q 47

Lining of blood vessels is made up of ________.

Q 48

Lining of small intestine is made up of ________.

Q 49

Lining of kidney tubules is made up of ________.

Q 50

Epithelial cells with cilia are found in ________ of our body.

Q 51

Water hyacinth floats on water surface. Explain.

Solution

Water hyacinth floats on the surface of water due to the presence of aerenchyma. Aerenchyma is a modified form of parenchyma (a simple permanent tissue). Cells of parenchyma are usually loosely packed. There are large intercellular spaces that form air cavities. The air cavities in water hyacinth provide buoyancy to them which helps them to float.

Q 52

Which structure protects the plant body against the invasion of parasites?

Solution

The epidermis has thick cuticles. Epidermal cells secrete waxy substances (cutin) on their outer surface. This prevents invasion by parasites.

Q 53

Cork cells possess ________ on their walls that makes it impervious to gases and water.

Q 54

________ have tubular cells with perforated walls and are living in nature.

Q 55

Bone possesses a hard matrix composed of ________ and ________.

Q 56

Why is epidermis important for the plants?

Solution

The outermost protective covering of the plants is called the epidermis. It is important for the plants due to its functions.

  1. The main function of epidermis is protection against water loss. In desert plants, epidermal cells secrete a waxy, water-resistant layer of cutin on the outer surface of the plants which reduces loss of water.
  2. It also consists of small pores called stomata which help in the exchange of gases with the atmosphere.
  3. The function of the epidermal cells of the roots is absorption of water from the soil.
  4. It also aids against mechanical injury and invasion by parasitic fungi.
Q 57

________ and ________ are forms of complex tissue.

Q 58

________ have guard cells.

Q 59

Cells of cork contain a chemical called ________.

Q 60

Husk of coconut is made of ________ tissue.

Q 61

________ gives flexibility in plants.

Q 62

________ and ________ are both conducting tissues.

Q 63

Xylem transports ________ and ________ from soil.

Q 64

Phloem transport ________ from ________ to other parts of the plant.

Q 65

Differentiate between sclerenchyma and parenchyma tissues. Draw a well-labeled diagram.

Solution
Parenchyma Sclerenchyma
Cells are live. Cells are dead.
Cells have thin cell walls. The cell wall is thick due to the deposition of lignin.
Intercellular spaces are present between cells. No intercellular spaces are found between the cells.
Cells are oval in shape. Cells are long in shape.
Some cells contain chloroplast. The chloroplast is absent.
Storage of food is a major function. Structural rigidity is the main function.
Found in soft parts. Found in hard parts.

Q 66

Describe the structure and function of different types of epithelial tissues. Draw the diagram for each type of epithelial tissue.

Solution

Epithelial tissues can be (A) Squamous epithelium (Either simple squamous epithelium or stratified squamous epithelium) (B) Columnar epithelium (C) Cuboidal epithelium or (D) Glandular epithelium

The structure and function of different types of epithelial tissues are as follows:-

  1. Squamous epithelium can be of two types:-
    1. Simple squamous epithelium: Simple squamous epithelial cells are a simple flat kind of epithelium. They are extremely thin and flat. They form a delicate lining. They are present in the lining of the blood vessels or the alveoli (in lungs) where transportation of substances occurs through a selectively permeable membrane. They are also present in the esophagus and the lining of the mouth are also covered with this type of cells.
    2. Stratified squamous epithelium: Stratified squamous epithelial cells are arranged in a pattern of layers. E.g. Skin epithelial cells are arranged in many layers to prevent wear and tear.
  2. Columnar epithelium: Columnar epithelial cells are present where absorption and secretion occur as in the inner lining of the intestine. These cells are long or columnar (pillar-like). They facilitate movement across the epithelial barrier. In the respiratory tract, the columnar epithelial tissue has hair-like projections (cilia) on the outer surfaces of epithelial cells. The movement of the cilia pushes the mucus forward and clears it.
  3. Cuboidal epithelium: Cuboidal epithelium is made up of cube-shaped cells which provide mechanical support. They form the lining of the kidney tubules and ducts of salivary glands.
  4. Glandular epithelium: A multicellular gland or glandular epithelium is formed when a portion of the epithelial tissue folds inward and a multicellular gland is formed. An epithelial cell sometimes acquires additional specialisation as a gland cell. Gland cells can secrete substances at the surface of the epithelium.
Q 67

Draw well-labeled diagrams of various types of muscles found in the human body.

Solution

The three main types of muscular tissues found in the human body are:

  1. Skeletal (striated) muscle tissue
  2. Smooth (Non-striated) muscle tissue
  3. Cardiac muscle tissue.

The well-labelled diagrams of these tissues are as follows:

  1. Skeletal muscle tissue.
  2. Smooth muscle tissue
  3. Cardiac muscle tissue
Q 68

Meristematic cells have a prominent nucleus and dense cytoplasm but they lack vacuole. Give reason?

Solution

Meristematic cells are active and continuously dividing cells so they have a prominent nucleus and dense cytoplasm. But since meristematic cells do not store food material or waste materials, they lack vacuole.

Q 69

Intercellular spaces are absent in sclerenchymatous tissues. Give reason?

Solution

Intercellular spaces are absent in sclerenchymatous tissues because they have a cementing substance (known as lignin) around their cell membrane which makes them stiff and hard.

Q 70

We get a crunchy and granular feeling when we chew pear fruit. Given reason?

Solution

We get a crunchy and granular feeling while eating a pear due to the presence of sclereids. Sclereids are a reduced form of sclerenchyma cells with highly thickened, lignified cellular walls. The presence of numerous sclereids forms the gritty texture of pears.

Q 71

Branches of a tree move and bend freely in high wind velocity. Given reason?

Solution

Branches of a tree move and bend freely in high wind velocity. It is because of the presence of collenchyma tissue. Collenchyma is simple permanent tissue. The collenchyma tissue has a property of flexibility and shock-absorbing capacity due to the deposition of pectin which doesn't allow breaking of the branch.

Q 72

It is difficult to pull out the husk of a coconut tree. Given reason?

Solution

The husk of a coconut tree is made up of sclerenchymatous cells which have lignified cell walls. Hence, it is very difficult to pull out the husk of a coconut tree.

Q 73

List the characteristics of cork. How are they formed? Mention their role.

Solution

Cork covers the old stems of woody trees.
Characteristics of cork are as follows:

  1. Cells of cork are dead at maturity.
  2. These cells are compactly arranged.
  3. Cells do not contain intercellular spaces.
  4. Cells possess chemical substance suberin in their walls.
  5. They are several layers thick.
  6. Cork is impervious to gases and water.

As plants grow older, a strip of the secondary lateral meristem (called cork cambium) develops in the cortical region. It cuts cells towards both the outer and inner sides. Gradually, this secondary tissue replaces the epidermal layer of the stem. This forms several layer thick corks.
Role of cork is mentioned below:

  1. It protects the internal tissues from mechanical injury and from parasitic attacks.
  2. It contains small pores (called lenticels) for gaseous exchange.
  3. It provides mechanical strength.
Q 74

Why are xylem and phloem called complex tissues? How are they different from one other?

Solution

Both xylem and phloem consist of more than one type of cells, which coordinate to perform a common function.

Xylem Phloem
Is composed of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma and xylem fibres. Is composed of sieve tubes, phloem parenchyma, phloem fibres and companion cells.
Only xylem parenchyma is composed of a living cell in xylem tissue. Only phloem fibre is composed of the dead cell is in the phloem.
Transports water and minerals. Transports food.
Movement of materials is in one direction. Movement of materials is in both directions.
Q 75

Differentiate between meristematic and permanent tissues in plants.

Solution

Differences between meristematic and permanent tissues are:

Meristematic tissue Permanent tissue
Cells of this tissue divide throughout their life. Cells of this tissue lose the ability to divide to take up specific functions.
Meristematic tissues are located at specific regions of the plant. Permanent tissues are distributed throughout the plant body.
Cells of this tissue are very active, have dense cytoplasm, thin walls, and prominent nuclei. They lack vacuoles. Cells of this tissue are vacuolated, vary in shape and size. Their cell walls may be thin or thick.
The cell wall of the cells is cellulosic. Cells wall may be made up of cellulose or lignin or suberin.
Cells of this tissue are living. Cells of this tissue may be living (parenchyma) or dead(sclerenchyma).
Intercellular spaces are not present in cells. Intercellular space may be present or absent.
Q 76

Define the process of differentiation.

Solution

In a multicellular organism, the cells become specialized to perform different functions. The different functions are taken up by different group of cells. Accordingly, the cells become morphologically different. This phenomenon of taking up a permanent shape, size and function by a meristmatic tissue is known as differentiation. Permanent tissues lose the ability to divide.

Q 77

Name any two simple and two complex permanent tissues in plants.

Solution
  • Simple permanent tissues in plants are parenchyma, collenchyma, and sclerenchyma.
  • Complex permanent tissues in plants are xylem and phloem.

Q 51

Classify the following organisms based on the absence/presence of true coelom (i.e., acoelomate, pseudocoelomate and coelomate)

Solution

  • Pseudocoelomates: Wuchereria, Ascaris.
  • Coelomates: Nereis, Earthworm, Scorpion, Birds, Fishes, and Horse.

Torpedo, Sting ray, Dog fish, Rohu, Angler fish, Exocoetus.

  • Bony endoskeleton- Rohu, Angler fish, Exocoetus.
Q 53

Classify the following based on number of chambers in their heart.
Rohu, Scoliodon, Frog, Salamander, Flying lizard, King Cobra, Crocodile, Ostrich, Pigeon, Bat, Whale

Solution

  • 3-Chambered Heart - Frog, Salamander, Flying lizard, king cobra. 
  • 4-Chambered Heart - Crocodile, Ostrich, Pigeon, Bat, Whale.
Q 54

Classify Rohu, Scoliodon, flying lizard, king cobra, frog, salamander, ostrich, pigeon, bat, crocodile, and whale into the cold-blooded/warm-blooded animals and give reasons to support your answer.

Solution

Cold-blooded animals - Rohu, Scoliodon, Flying lizard, King cobra, Frog, Salamander, Crocodile
Warm-blooded animals - Ostrich, Pigeon, Bat, Whale

Cold-blooded creatures take on the temperature of their surroundings. They are hot when their environment is hot and cold when their environment is cold. Cold-blooded animals are much more active in warm environments and are very sluggish in cold environments. This is because their muscle activity depends on chemical reactions which become fast when it is hot and slow when it is cold.
Warm-blooded creatures, like mammals and birds, try to keep their bodies at a constant temperature. They do this by generating their own heat when they are in a cooler environment and by cooling themselves when they are in a hotter environment. To generate heat, warm-blooded animals convert the food they eat into energy. They have to eat a lot of food as compared to cold-blooded animals to maintain constant body temperature. 

Q 55

Name two egg laying mammals?

Solution

Mammals give birth to young one but some mammals lay eggs like – platypus and echidna.

Label a,b,c and d. given in Fig. Give the function of (b)

Solution
  1. Dorsal fin
  2. Caudal fin
  3. Pelvic fin
  4. Pectoral fin

Function of Caudal fin–Caudal fin helps in streamlined movement in water.

Q 75

Fill in the boxes given in figure with appropriate characteristics/plant group (s).

Solution
  1. Thallophytes
  2. Without specialized vascular tissue
  3. Pteridophyta
  4. Phanerogams
  5. Bear naked seeds
  6. Angiosperms
  7. Have seed with two cotyledons
  8. Monocots.
Q 76

Write names of few thallophytes. Draw a labelled diagram of Spirogyra.

Solution

Ulothrix, Spirogyra, Cladophora, Ulva, and Chari are few thallophytes.

Q 77

Thallophyta, Bryophyta and Pteridophyta are called as cryptogams whereas gymnosperms and angiosperms are called as phanerogams. Discuss. Draw one example of gymnosperm.

Solution

The Thallophyta, Bryophyta, and Pteridophyta are called as ‘cryptogams’ because the reproductive organs of these groups are inconspicuous or hidden. Seeds are absent.
On the other hand, plants that make seeds and have well-differentiated reproductive tissues are called phanerogams. In these plants, seeds consist of the embryo along with stored food. The stored food serves the purpose of the initial growth of the embryo during germination.
On the basis of the presence or absence of naked seeds, these are divided into two groups Embryo develops into a seed: gymnosperms (naked seed) and angiosperms (covered seed).
One example of a gymnosperm is Cynas. Its diagram is shown below:

Q 78

Define the terms and give one example of each:

  1. Bilateral symmetry
  2. coelom
  3. Triptoblastic
Solution

Bilateral symmetry – Body can be divided into two similar halves only by one plane that passes through the central or median axis eg – tortoise, humans.
Coelom – It is the body cavity which is lined externally as well as by regular layer of mesoderm
Triploblastic – When the body of an animal develops form three germ layer – ectoderm, mesoderm and endoderm, are called triploblastic

Q 79

You are given leech, Nereis, Scolopendra, prawn and scorpion and all have segmented body organisations. Will you classify them in one group? If no, give important characters based on which you will separate these organisms into different groups.

Solution

All organisms given in the question do not belong to the same group. Leech and Nereis belong to phylum Annelida because they have

  1.  A metamerically segmented body and divided by septums.
  2. Bilateral symmetry, excretion occur through nephridia.
  3. Respiration occurs generally through body surface.

But Scolopendra, prawn and scorpion belong to phylum Arthropoda because of presence of features mentioned below:

  1. Segmented body without septum unlike annelida, body divided into head, thorax and abdomen.
  2. Presence of jointed legs.
  3. Respiration occurs through gills, trachea and book lungs.
  4. Exoskeleton is made up of chitin.
Q 80

Which organism is more complex and evolved among bacteria, mushroom and mango tree? Give reasons.

Solution

Mango tree is more complex and evolved among bacteria and mushroom because of the following reasons:

  1.  It is eukaryotic, autotrophic, terrestrial sporophyte with covered seed.
  2. Mango tree has more differentiated body, and seeds are present inside the fruit.
  3. Mango tree posses vascular tissues for conduction of water, food and minerals
  4. Mango tree posses embryonic stage in the development.

The bacteria are unicellular prokaryotic cell while Mushroom is a fungi and do not posses roots, stems and leaves. i.e. these  are the heterotrophic, simple thallophyte with no tissue system.

Q 81

Differentiate between flying lizard and bird. Draw the diagram.

Solution
  1. Flying lizard belongs to the group ‘reptiles’ and Birds belong to aves.
  2. Flying birds have scales on their body but birds have feathers.
  3. Forelimbs of bird are modified as wings but not in case of lizards.
  4. Flying lizard have long tail but birds have tail feathers.
  5. Flying lizards have three chambered hearts and birds have four chambered hearts.
  6. Flying lizard are cold blooded but birds are warm blooded.

 
Lizard


Pigeon

Q 82

List some common features in cat, rat, and bat.

Solution

Bat, rat and cat belong to Class Mammalia and have the following common features

  1. All have notochord at some stage of their life.
  2. All are warm blooded.
  3. All have four chambered heart.
  4. Skin covered with hair and with sweat and oil glands is found in the given animals.
Q 83

Why do we keep both snake and turtle in the same class?

Solution

Both snake and turtle are kept in the same class because both are

  1. cold-blooded
  2. have scales
  3. breathe through lungs
  4. have three-chambered hear
  5. lay eggs with thick covering

Q 8

Four cars A, B, C and D are moving on a levelled road. Their distance-time graph is given as under. The correct statement is:

 

Q 9

Which of the following figures represent uniform motion of moving object correctly?

Q 10

Slope of a velocity-time graph gives

Q 11

In which of the following cases of motion, the distance moved and the magnitude of displacement are equal?

Q 12

The displacement of a moving object in a given interval of time is zero. Would the distance traveled by the object also be zero? Justify your answer.

Solution

When the displacement is zero, it does not mean that distance is also zero. Displacement can be zero when the moving object comes back to its original position. Displacement is either equal to or less than distance but the distance traveled is always more than zero.

Q 13

How will the equations of motion for an object moving with a uniform velocity change?

Solution

If the velocity is uniform then,

Acceleration a = 0  
So, the equations of motion will become: 

First equation: v = u
Second equation: s = ut
Third equation: {tex}v^{2\;}-\;u^2\;=\;0{/tex}

Q 14

A girl walks along a straight path to drop a letter in the letterbox and comes back to her initial position. Her displacement–time graph is shown in Fig. Plot a velocity-time graph for the same.

Solution

For the initial 50 seconds, velocity is 2 m/s. After that, velocity drops of zero; as shown by a vertical line in the graph.

For the next 50 seconds, velocity is taken in negative because displacement is becoming zero.

Q 15

A car starts from rest and moves along the X-axis with constant acceleration 5 ms-2 for 8 seconds. If it is continue with constant velocity, what distance will the car cover in 12 seconds since it started from the rest?

Solution

Here, Initial velocity of car, u = 0
By Using the relation, S = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}at2
The distance travelled in first 8s, S1 = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}at2 = 0 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 82 = 160 m
At this point the velocity, v = u + at = 0 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 40 ms-1.

Since the velocity remains constant, So, the distance covered in last four seconds, S2= constant velocity {tex}\times{/tex} time = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 160 m

Hence, total distance, S = S1 + S2= 160 m + 160 m = 320 m

Q 16

A motorcyclist drives from A to B with a uniform speed of 30 km h-1 and returns back with a speed of 20 km h-1. Find its average speed. 

Solution

Let, The distance from point 'A' to point 'B'= AB = x,

Time taken to reach from A to B, when motorcyclist drives with speed 30 km h-1 = t1 = {tex}\frac{x}{30}{/tex}

Similarly,Time taken to reach from B to A, when motorcyclist drives with speed 20 km h-1 =  t2 = {tex}\frac{x}{20}{/tex}
Now, Total time = t1 + t2 {tex}=\frac{5 x}{60} h{/tex}
Average speed for entire journey = {tex}\frac{\text { Total distance }}{\text { Total time }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\ 2x}{\frac{5 x}{60}}{/tex}= 24 km h-1.

Q 17

The velocity-time graph shows the motion of a cyclist. Find

  1. its acceleration
  2. its velocity and
  3. the distance covered by the cyclist in 15 seconds.
Solution
  1. From graph, it is clear that, velocity is not changing, therefore acceleration is equal to zero.
  2. From graph, velocity of cyclist = 20 ms-1
  3. Distance covered by cyclist in 15 seconds, s = Velocity {tex}\times{/tex} time = u {tex}\times{/tex} t = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 300 m.
Q 18

Draw a velocity versus time graph of a stone thrown vertically upward and then coming downwards after attaining the maximum height.

Solution

Q 19

An object is dropped from rest at a height of 150 m and simultaneously another object is dropped from rest at a height 100 m. What is the difference in their heights after 2 s if both the objects drop with same accelerations. How does the difference in heights vary with time?

Solution

Since, the object is dropped from rest, Its initial velocity = '0' m/s.

Initial difference in height = (150 - 100) m = 50 m

By using , equation, S =ut +{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} at2, we have, 
Distance travelled by first body in 2 s = h = 0 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}g(2)2 =2g   (For downward motion, g = Positive) 
Distance travelled by another body in 2 s = h2 = 0 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}g(2)2 = 2g
After 2s, height at which the first body will be = h'1 = 150 - 2g
After 2 s, height at which the second body will be = h'2 = 100 - 2g
Thus, after 2 s, difference in height = 150 - 2g - (100-2g) = 150 -2g -100 +2g = 50 m = initial difference in height.
Therefore, difference in height does not vary with time.

Q 20

An object starting from rest travels 20 m in the first 2 s and 160 m in the next 4 s. What will be the velocity after 7 s from the start?

Solution

After first 2 s; u = 0, s = 20 m and t = 2 s
Using distance formula;
s = ut + {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} at2
or 20m = 0 + {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} a {tex}\times{/tex} (2)2 s2 or a = 10 ms-2
Final velocity after 2s = u + at = 0 + 10 ms-2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2s = 20 ms-1
For the next 4 s; u = 20 m/s, t = 4 s and s = 160 m
Using distance formula;
s = ut + {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} at2
or 160 m = 20 ms-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 s + {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} a {tex}\times{/tex} 16 s2
or 160 m = 80 m + a {tex}\times{/tex} 8s2
or a = {tex}\frac {80m}{8s^2}{/tex} = 10 ms-2
Velocity after 7 s can be calculated as follows:
v = u + at = 0 + 10 ms-2 {tex}\times{/tex} 7s = 70 ms-1

Q 21

Using following data, draw time-displacement graph for a moving object:

Time (s) 0 2 4 6 8 10 12 14 16
Displacement(m) 0 2 4 4 4 6 4 2 0

Use this graph to find average velocity for first 4s, for next 4s and for last 6s.
 

Solution


Average velocity for first 4s {tex}=\frac{\text { change in displacement }}{\text { time }}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {4m - 0m}{4s}{/tex} = 1 ms-1
Average velocity for next 4s = {tex}\frac {4m - 4m}{8s - 4s}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {0}{m}{/tex} = 0 ms-1
Average velocity for last 6s = {tex}\frac {0 - 6m}{16s - 10s}{/tex} = - 1 ms-1

Q 22

An electron moving with a velocity of 5{tex}\times{/tex}104 ms-1 enters into a uniform electric field and acquires a uniform acceleration of 104 ms-2 in the direction of its initial motion.

  1. Calculate the time in which the electron would acquire a velocity double of its initial velocity.
  2. How much distance the electron would cover in this time?
Solution

Given, initial velocity of electron, u = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 ms-1 and acceleration, a = 104 ms-2

  1. Final velocity of electron = v = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} u = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 ms-1
    We know that, v= u +at or t = {tex}\frac{v-u}{a}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{10 \times 10^{4}-5 \times 10^{4}}{10^{4}}\right)=\frac{5 \times 10^{4}}{10^{4}}{/tex} = 5s
  2. Using relation; S = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex}at2 
    Distance covered by electron in the given time,S = (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104) {tex}\times{/tex} 5 + {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex}(104) {tex}\times{/tex} (5)2 = (25 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 + {tex}\frac{25}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 104 ) = 37.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 m
Q 23

Obtain a relation for the distance travelled by an object moving with a uniform acceleration in the interval between 4th and 5th second.

Solution

Using the equation of motion s = {tex}ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}{/tex}
Distance travelled in 5 seconds, s = {tex}u \times 5 + \frac{1}{2}a \times {5^2}{/tex}
or {tex}5u + \frac{{25}}{2}a{/tex}
Similarly, distance travelled in 4 seconds, s' = {tex}4u + \frac{{16}}{2}a{/tex}
Distance travelled in the interval between 4th and 5th seconds = {tex}(s - s') = (u + \frac{9}{2}a)m{/tex}

Q 24

Two stones are thrown vertically upwards simultaneously with their initial velocities u1 and u2 respectively. Prove that the heights reached by them would be in the ratio of {tex}u_1^2{/tex} : {tex}u_2^2{/tex} (Assume upward acceleration is -g and downward acceleration to be +g).

Solution

By using velocity- distance equation, v2 -u2 =2aS.
For upward motion, acceleration due to gravity, a= -g m/s2.
Let the height attained by object ='h'
Therefore, We have,  v2 = u2 - 2gh or h = {tex}\frac{u^{2}-v^{2}}{2 g}{/tex} ............ (1) 
But at highest point v = 0
Therefore, equation (1) become;   {tex}h=\frac{u^{2}}{2 g}{/tex}
Now, For first ball,Height attained, h1 = {tex}\frac{u_{1}^{2}}{2 g}{/tex}..............(2)
Similarly, For second ball, Height attained, h2 {tex}=\frac{u_{2}^{2}}{2 g}{/tex}............ (3)
Dividing equation, (2) by (3), we get.
 {tex}\frac{h_{1}}{h_{2}}=\frac{u_{1}^{2} / 2 g}{u_{2}^{2} / 2 g}={/tex}{tex}\frac{u_{1}^{2}}{u_{2}^{2}} \text { or } h_{1} : h_{2}=u_{1}^{2} : u _{2}^{2}{/tex}  ; Hence proved.

Q 5

A passenger in a moving train tosses a coin which falls behind him. It means that motion of the train is

Q 6

An object of mass 2 kg is sliding with a constant velocity of 4ms-1 on a frictionless horizontal table. The force required to keep the object moving with the same velocity is

Q 7

Rocket works on the principle of conservation of

Q 8

A water tanker filled up to {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}rd of its height is moving with a uniform speed. On sudden application of the brake, the water in the tank would

Q 9

There are three solids balls made up of aluminium, steel and wood, of the same shape and same volume. Which of them would have highest inertia?

Solution

Quantum of inertia depends on mass of the object. For same volume, the mass of steel would be highest; compared to aluminium or wood. Hence, the solid of steel would have the highest inertia.

Q 10

Two balls of the same size of different materials, rubber and iron are kept on the smooth floor of a moving train. The brakes are applied suddenly to stop the train. Will the balls start rolling? If so, in which direction? Will they move with the same speed? Give reasons for your answer.

Solution

Yes, the balls will start rolling in the direction in which the train was moving. Due to the application of the brakes, the train comes to rest but due to inertia the balls try to remain in motion.Therefore, they begin to roll. Since the masses of the balls are not the same, therefore, the inertial forces are not same on both the balls. Thus, the balls will move with different speeds.

Q 11

Two identical bullets are fired one by a light rifle and another by a heavy rifle with the same force. Which rifle will hurt the shoulder more and why?

Solution

According to law of conservation of momentum; the momentum of bullet forwad will be equal to the momentum of rifle backward . In case of light rifle; velocity will be more than the velocity of heavier rifle because of mass so that momentum (product of mass and velocity) for both shall  not be equal. Due to this, the lighter rifle will hurt the shoulder more.In both cases it has been called recoied.

Q 12

A horse continues to apply a force in order to move a cart with a constant speed. Explain why?

Solution

When a cart is moving on the road, it has to encounter friction. To maintain a constant speed, some force need to be applied continuously to overcome the friction. Hence, the horse needs to continuously apply a force in order move the cart with a constant speed.

Q 13

Suppose a ball of mass 'm' is thrown vertically upwards with an initial speed 'v', its speed decreases continuously till it becomes zero. Therefore, the ball begins to fall downward and attains the speed 'v' again before striking the ground. It implies that the magnitude of initial and final momenta of the ball are same. Yet, it is not an example of conservation of momentum. Explain why.

Solution

Law of conservation of momentum is applicable to isolated system (no external force is applied). In this case, the change in velocity is due to the gravitational force of earth.

Q 14

Velocity versus time graph of a ball of mass 50 g rolling on a concrete floor is shown in Fig. Calculate the acceleration and frictional force of the floor on the ball.

Solution

u = 80 m/s, v = 0 m/s and t = 8s
Acceleration can be calculated as follows:
v = u + at
Or {tex}a=\frac{v-u}{t}=-\frac{80}{8} m s^{-2}=-10 m s^{-2}{/tex}
Frictional force can be calculated as follows:
F = m {tex}\times{/tex} a = {tex}\frac {50}{1000}{/tex} kg {tex}\times{/tex} 10 ms-2 = 0.5 N 

Q 15

A truck of mass M is moved under a force F. If the truck is then loaded with an object equal to the mass of the truck and the driving force is halved, then how does the acceleration change?

Solution

Acceleration can be given as follow:
F = ma or a = {tex}\frac {f}{m}{/tex} or a1 = {tex}\frac {F}{M}{/tex}
When mass is doubled and force is halved;
{tex}a_{2}=\frac{F}{4 M} \quad or \quad \frac{a_{2}}{a_{1}}=\frac{F}{4 M} \div \frac{F}{M}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
So, acceleration becomes one-fourth.

Q 16

Two friends on roller-skates are standing 5 m apart facing each other. One of them throws a ball of 2 kg towards the other, who catches it. How will this activity affect the position of the two? Explain your answer.

Solution

Separation between them will increase. Initially the momentum of both of them are zero as they are at rest. In order to conserve the momentum the one who throws the ball would move backward. The second will experience a net force after catching the ball and therefore will move backwards that is in the direction of the force.

Q 17

Water sprinkler used for grass lawns begins to rotate as soon as the water is supplied. Explain the principle on which it works.

Solution

The working of the rotation of sprinkler is based on third law of motion. As the water comes out of the nozzle of the sprinkler, an equal and opposite reaction force comes into play. So, the sprinkler starts rotating.

Q 18

Using second law of motion, derive the relation between force and acceleration. A bullet of mass 10 g strikes a sand bag with a velocity of 103 ms-1 and gets embedded after travelling 5 cm. Calculate

  1. the resistive force exerted by the sand bag on the bullet.
  2. the time taken by the bullet to come to rest.
Solution
  1. {tex}m = 10g = \frac{{10}}{{1000}}kg,\,u = {10^3}\,m/s,\,\upsilon = 0,s = \frac{5}{{100}}m{/tex}
    {tex}{\upsilon ^2} - {u^2} = 2as{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow 0 - {({10^3})^2} = 2.a.\frac{5}{{100}}{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow a = \frac{{ - 1000 \times 1000}}{{2 \times 5}} \times 100{/tex}
    = -107 ms-2
    {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} F = m.a = -105 N
  2. {tex}\upsilon = u + at{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 0=(103) - 107t
    {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 107t = 103
    {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}t = \frac{{{{10}^3}}}{{{{10}^7}}} = {10^{ - 4}}\,s{/tex}
Q 19

Derive the unit of force using the second law of motion. A force of 5 N produces an acceleration of 8 ms-2 on a mass 'm1' and an acceleration of 24 ms-2 on a mass' m2'. What acceleration would the same force provide if both the masses are tied together?

Solution

We know, F = ma = 5N or 5kg ms-2
{tex} \Rightarrow {m_1} = \frac{F}{{{a_1}}} = \frac{5}{8}kg{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{m_2} = \frac{F}{{{a_2}}} = \frac{5}{{24}}kg{/tex}
If the mass are tied together, then
{tex}\therefore M = \left( {\frac{5}{8} + \frac{5}{{24}}} \right)kg = \left( {\frac{5}{6}} \right)kg{/tex}
Acceleration produced in M,
{tex}a = \frac{F}{M} = \frac{5}{{\frac{5}{6}}} {/tex} =  6 ms-2
Therefore, if both the masses are tied together, the force would result in an acceleration of  6 ms-2.

Q 20

What is momentum? Write its SI unit. Interpret force in terms of momentum. Represent the following graphically:

  1. Momentum versus velocity when mass is fixed.
  2. Momentum versus mass when velocity is constant.
Solution

Momentum can be defined as "mass in motion". All objects have mass, so if an object is moving, then it has momentum, which means it has its mass in motion. It is the product of the mass and velocity of the body.
It is denoted by p.
Momentum, p = mv
Where
p = momentum, m = mass, v = velocity
The equation illustrates that momentum is directly proportional to the mass of an object and directly proportional to the velocity of that object.
Its SI unit is kg-m/s.
Force applied on an object of mass m moving with acceleration a,
{tex}F = ma = m \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} acceleration = rate of change of velocity = {tex}\frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}{/tex}]
= {tex}\frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Force applied on an object is equal to the rate of change of momentum of the object.

  1. Momentum versus velocity graph when mass is fixed.
    If m is fixed, then p {tex}\propto{/tex} v

    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Momentum versus velocity graph will be a straight line passing through the origin (if {tex}v = 0{/tex}, then {tex}p = 0{/tex}).
  2. Momentum versus mass graph when velocity is constant.

    If velocity is constant, then p {tex}\propto{/tex} m.
    So, the momentum versus mass graph will be a straight line passing through the origin (if {tex}m = 0{/tex}, then {tex} p = 0{/tex}).

Q 15

An apple falls from a tree because of gravitational attraction between the earth and apple. If F1 is the magnitude of force exerted by the earth on the apple and F2 is the magnitude of force exerted by apple on earth, then

Q 16

What is the source of the centripetal force that a planet requires revolving around the Sun? On what factors does that force depend?

Solution

The gravitational force is the source of centripetal force that a planet requires to revolve around the sun. This force depends on the masses of the Sun and the planet and on distance between them.

Q 17

On the earth, a stone is thrown from a height in a direction parallel to the earth’s surface while another stone is simultaneously dropped from the same height. Which stone would reach the ground first and why?

Solution

For both the stones, Their initial velocity, u = 0
Acceleration in downward direction = g

Using equation, S =ut + {tex}\frac12{/tex}at2
Now, h = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}gt2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 0 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}gt2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}gt2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}}{/tex}
Since time period depends upon the height and acceleration due to gravity. Here, both stones fall from same height and planet. Therefore, Both stones will take the same time to reach the ground.

Q 18

Suppose gravity of the earth suddenly becomes zero, then which direction will the moon begin to move, if no other celestial body affects it?

Solution

The moon will begin to move in a straight line in the direction in which it was moving at that instant because the circular motion of the moon is due to centripetal force provided by the gravitational force of the earth.

Q 19

Identical packets are dropped from two airplanes, one above the equator and the other above the north pole, both at height h. Assuming all conditions are identical, will those packets take the same time to reach the surface of the earth. Justify your answer.

Solution

Identical packets are dropped from two aeroplanes, one above the equator and the other above the north pole, both at height h. Assuming all conditions are identical, will those packets take same time to reach the surface of earth.

Q 20

The weight of any person on the moon is about 1/6 times that on the earth. He can lift a mass of 15 kg on the earth. What will be the maximum mass, which can be lifted by the same force applied by the person on the moon?

Solution

Since the weight of any person on the moon is about 1/6 times that on the earth, hence acceleration due to gravity at the moon is 1/6 of that on earth. This means that by applying the same force a person can lift six times heavier objects on the moon than what he could lift on the earth. So, the maximum mass which can be lifted by the same force applied by the person on the moon is 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 kg = 90 kg.

Q 21

Calculate the average density of the earth in terms of g, G and R.

Solution

We know that, Acceleration due to gravity, g = {tex}\frac{GM}{R^2}{/tex} or M = {tex}\frac{gR^2}{G}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} We know that, Average density of the earth, D = {tex}\frac{Mass}{Volume}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{gR^2}{G \times V_e}{/tex} (where Ve is the volume of the Earth)
or D = {tex}\frac{g R^{2}}{G \frac{4}{3} \pi R^{s}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3g}{4\pi GR}{/tex}

Q 22

The earth is acted upon by gravitation of the sun, even though it does not fall into it. Why?

Solution

The earth does not fall into the sun because the earth remains in its circular orbit due to the gravitational force acting on it.

Q 23

How does the weight of an object vary with respect to the mass and radius of the earth? In a hypothetical case, if the diameter of the earth becomes half of its present value and its mass becomes four times of its present value, then how would the weight of any object on the surface of the earth be affected?

Solution

A weight of an object is directly proportional to the mass of the earth (M) and inversely proportional to the square of the radius of the earth (R), i.e.,
A weight of object {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\frac{M}{R^2}{/tex}
Original weight, Wo = mg = mG{tex}\frac{M}{R^2}{/tex}..........(1)
According to question, hypothetically,  Mass of earth,M' = 4 M and Radius of earth, R' = {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex}.

Now, New weight an object =Wn =mg' = mG{tex}\frac{M'}{R'^2}{/tex}..........(2)

Now, put the values of M' and R' in equation (2), we get.
Weight of an object, Wn = mG{tex}\frac{4 M}{\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}= (16 mG){tex}\frac{M}{R^2}{/tex} = 16 {tex}\times{/tex}Wo 
The weight will become 16 times.

Q 24

How does the force of attraction between the two bodies depend upon their masses and distance between them? A student thought that two bricks tied together would fall faster than a single one under the action of gravity. Do you agree with his hypothesis or not? Comment.

Solution

We know that, Acceleration due to gravity, {tex}g=\frac{GM}{R^2} {/tex}, M= Mass of earth, R= radius of earth and G= universal gravitational constant.

Since, Acceleration due to gravity is independent of the mass of the falling body. Therefore, This hypothesis is not correct. The two bricks like a single body, fall with the same speed to reach the ground at the same time in case of free fall.

Q 25

Two objects of masses m1 and m2 having the same size are dropped simultaneously from heights h1 and h2 respectively. Find out the ratio of time they would take in reaching the ground. Will this ratio remain the same if

  1. one of the objects is hollow and the other one is solid; and
  2. both of them are hollow, size remaining the same in each case? Give reasons.
Solution

As u = 0, using equation, S= ut + {tex}\frac12{/tex}at2 , We have, 

h1 =  {tex}\frac{1}{2} g t_{1}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} g t_{2}^{2}{/tex},
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{t_1}{t_2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{h_1}{h_2}}{/tex}

Since, time period does not depends upon the mass and size. Therefore,  Ratio will not change in either case because acceleration remains the same.

Q 26
  1. A cube of side 5 cm is immersed in water and then in saturated salt solution. In which case, will it experience a greater buoyant force? If each side of the cube is reduced to 4 cm and then immersed in water, what will be the effect on the buoyant force experienced by the cube as compared to the first case for water. Give the reason for each case.
  2. A ball weight 4 kg of density 4000 kg m-3is completely immersed in water of density 103kg m-3. Find the force of buoyancy on it. (Given g = 10 ms-2.)
Solution
  1. The cube will experience a greater buoyant force in saturated salt solution than in water because density of saturated salt solution is more than the density of water. If each side of the cube is reduced to 4 cm, it will result in reduction in volume of the cube. Hence, the buoyant force experienced by it will reduce in water.
  2. Buoyant force = weight of displaced water
    = density of water {tex}\times{/tex} volume of displaced water {tex}\times{/tex} g
    = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {4}{4000}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 [{tex}\because{/tex} volume = {tex}\frac {weight}{density}{/tex}]
    = 10 N

Q 10

A rocket is moving up with a velocity v. If the velocity of this rocket is suddenly tripled, what will be the ratio of two kinetic energies?

Solution

Initial velocity = {tex}\upsilon{/tex}, then final velocity, {tex}\upsilon{/tex}= 3v
Initial kinetic energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2}m{\upsilon ^2}{/tex}
Final kinetic energy (KE) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}m\upsilon {'^2} = \frac{1}{2}m{(3\upsilon )^2} = 9\left( {\frac{1}{2}m{\upsilon ^2}} \right){/tex}
(KE)initial : (KE )final =1:9.

Q 11

Avinash can run at a speed of 8 ms-1 against the frictional force of 10 N, and Kapil can move at a speed of 3 ms-1 against the frictional force of 25 N. Who is more powerful and why?

Solution

Power of Avinash PA = FA . vA = 10 {tex} \times{/tex} 8 = 80 W
Power of Kapil PK = FK. vK = 25 {tex} \times{/tex} 3 = 75 W
So, Avinash is more powerful than Kapil.

Q 12

A boy is moving on a straight road against a frictional force of 5 N. After travelling a distance of 1.5 km he forgot the correct path at a round about (Fig.) of radius 100 m. However, he moves on the circular path for one and half cycle and then he moves forward upto 2.0 km. Calculate the work done by him.

Solution

Displacement = 1500 m + 200 m + 2000 m = 3700 m
Work done = Force {tex}\times{/tex} displacement = 5 N {tex}\times{/tex} 3700 m = 18500 J
(Note: We do not need to calculate the circumference because we need to take displacement and not distancebecause displacement is change of position not distance covered by the object)

Q 13

Can any object have mechanical energy even if its momentum is zero? Explain.

Solution

Yes, mechanical energy comprises of both potential energy and kinetic energy. Zero momentum means that velocity is zero. Hence, there is no kinetic energy but the object may possess potential energy.

Q 14

Can any object have momentum even if its mechanical energy is zero? Explain.

Solution

No, Zero mechanical energy means that there is no potential energy and no kinetic energy. Therefore, if kinetic energy is zero, velocity becomes zero and hence, there will be no momentum.

Q 15

The power of a motor pump is 2 kW. How much water per minute the pump can raise to a height of 10 m? (Given g = 10 m s–2)

Solution

We have P = 2 kW = 2000 W, g = 10 m/s2, time = 2 minute = 60 s and height h = 10 m
We know; {tex}P=\frac{W}{\Delta t}{/tex} Or P = {tex}\frac {mgh}{\Delta t}{/tex}
Or 2000W = {tex}\frac{m \times 10 \times 10}{60}{/tex} Or m = {tex}\frac {2000 \times 60}{10 \times 10}{/tex} = 1200 kg
So, the motor will pump 1200 kg water per minute.

Q 16

The weight of a person on a planet A is about half of that on the Earth. He can jump up to 0.4 m high on the Earth. How high can he jump on planet A?

Solution

Let, Potential energy of a person on Earth = mg1h1

Potential energy of same person on planet 'A' = mg2h2

Since, The potential energy of the person will remain the same on the Earth and on planet A.

Therefore, mg1h1 = mg2h2

Since mass remains same, So,  if g1 = g, then g2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}g

Here, h1 = 0.4 ; Now h2 = {tex}\frac{g_1h_1}{g_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{g \times 0.4}{g_2}{/tex}
or h2 = 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 0.8 m.

Therefore, he can jump on plant A= 0.8m 

Q 17

The velocity of a body moving in a straight line is increased by applying a constant force F, for some distance in the direction of the motion. Prove that the increase in the kinetic energy of the body is equal to the work done by the force on the body.

Solution

If a body of mass changes its velocity from "u" to "v"  with an acce{tex}{/tex}leration, "a"due to the application of a constant force, "F"  in the direction of motion,  then the  displacement of the body, " S" is given by
v2 = (u2) + 2as
{tex}s=\frac{v^2-u^2}{2a}{/tex}
but the work done in changing the velocity of the body from u to v is
W = F.s
{tex}W=\frac{ma(v^2-u^2)}{2a}{/tex}
⇒ {tex}W=\frac{m(v^2-u^2)}2{/tex}
{tex}W={\textstyle\frac{mv^2}2}-{\textstyle\frac{mu^2}2}{/tex}
But it is change in kinetic energy
Hence verified.

Q 18

It is possible that an object is in the state nof accelerated motion due to external force acting on it, but no work is being done by the force? Explain with example.

Solution

Yes, it is possible, if an object is moving in a circular path because force is always acting perpendicular to the direction of displacement.

Q 19

A ball is dropped from a height of 10 m. If the energy of the ball reduces by 40 per cent after striking the ground, how high can the ball bounce back? (g = 10 ms-2)

Solution

Let, the mass of a ball ='m'

The energy possessed by a ball at height (h) = mgh = m {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 100 mJ.   (g= 10 ms-2) 
If energy is reduced by 40%, then the remaining energy = 100mJ - 40mJ= 60 mJ.

Let  h' be the height attained by a ball after bouncing back. 

Now, Remaining energy = mgh'
Therefore, 60 m = m {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} h' or h' = 6 m.

Q 20

If an electric iron of 1200 W is used for 30 minutes every day, find the electric energy consumed in the month of April.

Solution

Given , Power of electric iron, P =1200 W=  {tex}\frac{1200}{1000}{/tex} = 1.2 kW
Time, t = {tex}\frac{30}{60}{/tex} = 0.5 h 

Electric energy consumed in a day = Power  {tex}\times{/tex} time =(1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5) kWh
Electric energy consumed in month of April (i.e. 30 days) , E = (1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5) {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = 18kWh.

Q 21

A light and a heavy object have the same momentum, find out the ratio of their kinetic energies. Which one has a larger kinetic energy?

Solution

Linear momentum of first object, {tex}{p_1} = {m_1}{\upsilon _1}{/tex} and of second object, {tex}{p_2} = {m_2}{\upsilon _2}{/tex}
But, p1 = p2
or, m1{tex}{\upsilon _2}{/tex} = m2{tex}{\upsilon _2}{/tex}
If m12then {tex}{\upsilon _1} > {\upsilon _2}{/tex}
{tex}{(K.E.)_1} = \frac{1}{2}({m_1}{\upsilon _1}){\upsilon _1} = \frac{1}{2}{p_1}{\upsilon _1}{/tex}
and {tex}{(K.E.)_2} = \frac{1}{2}({m_2}{\upsilon _2}){\upsilon _2} = \frac{1}{2}{p_2}{\upsilon _2}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{{{{(K.E.)}_1}}}{{{{(K.E.)}_2}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}({p_1}{\upsilon _1}){\upsilon _1}}}{{\frac{1}{2}({p_2}{\upsilon _2}){\upsilon _2}}} = \frac{{{\upsilon _1}}}{{{\upsilon _2}}}{/tex}
But, {tex}{\upsilon _1} > {\upsilon _2}{/tex}
Therefore, (K.E.)1 > (K.E.)2

Q 22

An automobile engine propels a 1,000 kg car A along a levelled road at a speed of 36 km h-1. Find the power if the opposing frictional force is 100 N. Now, suppose after travelling a distance of 200 m, this car collides with another stationary car B of same mass and comes to rest. Let its engine also stop at the same time. Now, car B starts moving on the same level road without getting its engine started. Find the speed of the car B just after the collision.

Solution

mA = mB = 1000 kg. v = 36 km/h= 10 m/s
Frictional force = 100 N
Since, the car A moves with a uniform speed, it means that the engine of car applies a force equal to the frictional force.
{tex}\frac{{{\rm{Force}} \times {\rm{distance}}}}{{{\rm{time}}}}{/tex}Power =
= F.v
= 100 N {tex}\times{/tex} 10 m/s = 1000 W
after collision,
mAuA + mBuE = mAυA + mB
1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 + 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} υB
vB = 10 ms-1.

Q 23

A girl having a mass of 35 kg sits on a trolley of mass 5 kg. The trolley is given an initial velocity of 4 ms-1 by applying a force. The trolley comes to rest after traversing a distance of 16 m.

  1. How much work is done on the trolley?
  2. How much work is done by the girl?
Solution

Mass of system,m = mass of girl + mass of trolley = 35+5 =40 kg.

Initial velocity, u = 4ms-1, Final velocity, v = 0,  Displacement,S = 16m
Using v2 - u2 = 2aS,
a  = {tex}\frac{v^2 - u^2}{2S}{/tex} = -{tex}\frac{16}{2 \times 16}{/tex} = -{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ms-2 
Force = ma = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} (-{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}) = -20 N

  1. Work done on the trolley = Force{tex}\times{/tex} Displacement = 20 N {tex}\times{/tex} 16 m = 320 J.
  2. Since, A girl is sitting on a trolley, Therefore, Work done by the girl = 0 J.
Q 24

Work is done whenever a force moves something over a distance. You can calculate the energy transferred, or work done, by multiplying the force by the distance moved in the direction of the force. Energy transferred = work done = force x distance moved in the direction of the force.

Four men lift a 250 kg box to a height of 1 m and hold it without raising or lowering it.

  1. How much work is done by the men in lifting the box?
  2. How much work do they do in just holding it?
  3. Why do they get tired while holding it? (g = 10 ms-2)
Solution

(a) F = 250 kg {tex}\times{/tex} 10 ms-2 (g= 10 ms-2) = 2500 N s = 1 m W = F.s = 2500 N {tex}\times{/tex} 1 m = 2500 Nm = 2500 J (b) Zero, as the box does not move at all while holding it. (c) In or do hold the box, men are applying a force which is opposite and equal to the gravitational force acting on the box. While applying the force, muscular effort is involved. So, they feel tired.

Q 25

What is power? How do you differentiate kilowatt from kilowatt-hour? The Jog Falls in Karnataka state are nearly 20 m high. 2000 tonnes of waterfalls from it in a minute. Calculate the equivalent power if all this energy can be utilized? (g=10 ms-2)

Solution

The rate of doing work is called power. Kilowatt is the unit of power and kilowatt-hour is the unit of energy. Calculation for power: Height= 20 m, mass = 2000 tonne = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 kg and time = 1 min = 60s
Power = {tex}\frac {W}{t} = \frac {mgh}{t} = \frac {2 \times 10^6 kg 10 ms^-2 \times 20 m}{60s}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {2}{3} \times 10^7 W{/tex}

Q 26

How is the power related to the speed at which a body can be lifted? How many kilograms will a man working with the power of 100 W, be able to lift at constant speed of 1 ms-1 vertically? (g = 10 ms-2)

Solution

We know that, Power = {tex}\frac{Work \ done \ or \ energy}{time}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{mgh}{t}{/tex} = m.g.({tex}\frac{h}{t}{/tex})

Since, speed ={tex}\frac{Distance }{time}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{t}{/tex}

Therefore, mass, m={tex}\frac{Power}{g \times Speed}{/tex}
Hence,  mass of a body that can be lifted = {tex}\frac{Power}{g \times Speed}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{100}{10 \times 1}{/tex} = 10 kg

Q 27

Define watt. Express kilowatt in terms of joule per second. A 150-kg car engine develops 500 W for each kg. What force does it exert in moving the car at a speed of 20 ms–1?

Solution

Power of an agent which does work at the rate of 1 J per second is called 1 watt.
1 W = 1 J/s
So, 1 kW = 1000 J/s
Mass of car = 150 kg, power/kg = 500 W
Hence, total power = 150 {tex}\times{/tex} 500 = 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 W
We know,
P = {tex}\frac {W}{t} = \frac {F \times s}{t}{/tex} Or 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 = F {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {20m}{1s}{/tex}
Or F = {tex}\frac{7.5 \times 10^{4}}{20}{/tex} = 3.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 N = 3750 N

Q 28

Compare the power at which each of the following is moving upwards against the force of gravity? (given g = 10 ms-2)

  1. a butterfly of mass 1.0 g that flies upward at a rate of 0.5 ms–1.
  2. a 250-g squirrel climbing up on a tree at a rate of 0.5 ms–1.
Solution
  1. Mass = 1g = 0.001 kg, speed = 0.5 m/s
    P = {tex}\frac {W}{t} = \frac {mgh}{t} = \frac {0.001 \times 10 \times 0.5}{1}{/tex} = 0.005 W
  2. Mass = 250 g = 0.25 kg, velocity = 0.5 m/s
    P = {tex}\frac {W}{t} = \frac {mgh}{t} = \frac {0.25 \times 10 \times 0.5}{1}{/tex} = 1.25 W
    Power of squirrel is much higher than power of butterfly.

Q 9

Before playing the orchestra in a musical concert, a sitarist tries to adjust the tension and pluck the string suitably. By doing so, he is adjusting

Q 10

The given graph shows the displacement versus time travelling with velocity of 1500 ms-1. Calculate the wavelength of the disturbance.

Solution

From the graph, It is clear that
Time period, T = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 s.
Frequency, v = {tex}\frac{1}{T}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-6}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 Hz.

Velocity = 1500 m/s
Wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \times 10^5}{1500}{/tex} = 333.3 m.

Q 11

Which of the two graphs (a) and (b) (Fig.) representing the human voice is likely to be the male voice? Give reason for your answer.

Solution

Graph represented by 'a' is of male voice. We know that male voice has lower frequency than female voice, and graph shown by 'a' shows lower frequency.

Q 12

A girl is sitting in the middle of a park of dimension 12 m {tex}\times{/tex} 12 m. On the left side of it there is a building adjoining the park and on the right side of the park, there is a road adjoining the park. A sound is produced on the road by a cracker. Is it possible for the girl to hear the echo of this sound? Explain your answer.

Solution

If the time gap between the original sound and reflected sound received by the listener is around 0.1s, only then the echo can be heard. 

Velocity of sound in air = 344 m/s.

Time interval = 0.1 s

Therefore, The minimum distance travelled by the reflected sound wave for the distinctly listening to the echo = Velocity of the sound {tex}\times{/tex} Time interval = 344 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 34.5 m

Since, the girl is sitting in middle of a park of dimension 12 m ×12 m, Therefore; The distance travelled by the sound reflected from the building and then reaching to the girl = (6 + 6) = 12 m, which is much smaller than the required distance. Therefore, Girl will not hear any echo of sound.

Q 13

Why do we hear the sound produced by the humming bees while the sound of vibrations of pendulum is not heard?

Solution

The sound produced by the humming bees is within the audible range of humans, i.e. between 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. But the sound produced by vibrations of pendulum is not within our hearing range. Due to this, we are able to hear the sound of a humming bee but unable to hear the sound of a pendulum.

Q 14

If any explosion takes place at the bottom of a lake, what type of shock waves in water will take place?

Solution

Longitudinal waves; because sound waves are longitudinal waves.

Q 15

The sound produced by a thunderstorm is heard 10 s after the lightning is seen. Calculate the approximate distance of the thunder cloud. (Given the speed of sound = 340 ms–1)

Solution

Speed of sound = 340 m/s and time = 10 s
Distance = speed {tex}\times{/tex} time = 340 × 10 = 3400 m

Q 16

For hearing the loudest ticking sound heard by the ear, find the angle x in the Fig.

Solution

Incident line is making an angle of 50o with reflecting surface.
So, angle of incidence = 90o - 50o = 40o
Angle of reflection = angle of incidence
Hence, {tex}\angle x{/tex} = 40o

Q 17

Why is the ceiling and wall behind the stage of good conference halls or concert halls made curved?

Solution

Curved walls and celling of a concert hall work like concave reflecting surfaces. We know that a concave surface converges the reflected waves. This helps in ensuring that sound clearly reaches the audience.

Q 18

Represent graphically by two separate diagrams in each case

  1. Two sound waves having the same amplitude but different frequencies?
  2. Two sound waves having the same frequency but different amplitudes.
  3. Two sound waves having different amplitudes and also different wavelengths.
Solution



Q 19

Establish the relationship between the speed of sound, its wavelength, and frequency. If the velocity of sound in air is 340 ms-1. Calculate

  1. wavelength when the frequency is 256 Hz.
  2. frequency when the wavelength is 0.85 m.
Solution

The relationship between the velocity of the sound, its wavelength, and frequency is given by the formula v = λ × T
Using the formula
Given velocity, {tex}\upsilon{/tex} = 340 ms-1

  1. 340 = 256 {tex}\lambda{/tex}
    frequency, {tex}\lambda = \frac{{340}}{{256}}{/tex}
    = 1.33 m
  2. 340 = v (0.85)
    wavelength, v = {tex}\frac{{340}}{{0.85}}{/tex}
    = 400 Hz.
Q 20

Draw a curve showing density or pressure variations with respect to distance for a disturbance produced by sound. Mark the position of compression and rarefaction on this curve. Also, define wavelengths and time period using this curve.

Solution

Wavelength is the distance between two consecutive compressions or two consecutive rarefactions of a wave sound. Wavelength is represented by the Greek letter {tex}\lambda {/tex} (lambda). The SI unit of wavelength is metre (m).
Time period is the time taken to travel the distance between any two consecutive compression or rarefaction from a fixed point. The time period of a sound wave is represented by letter ‘T’. The SI unit of time period is second (s).
