Q 19

Define and give two examples for each of the following.

  1. Acute diseases
  2. Chronic diseases
  3. Infectious diseases
  4. Non-infectious diseases
Solution
  1.  Acute disease cast for a short period of time and do not cause long term effect, e.g., cold and cough.
  2. Chronic disease is a disease in which it takes years and years to cure but sometimes it can be life-long
  3. Infectious diseases are caused by the attack of pathogens and can pass from one person to another.
    e.g., malaria and chickenpox.
  4. Non-infectious diseases are caused by factors other than living pathogens and are not transmitted.
    e.g., diabetes and goitre.
Q 20

Name two diseases caused by Protozoans. What are their causal organisms?

Solution

The diseased caused by the protozoan are as follows:

  1. Sleeping sickness caused by the protozoa which belongs to the species, Trypanosoma brucei.
  2. Kala-azar caused by Leishmania caused by the protozoa of the Leishmania genus.
Q 21

Which bacterium causes peptic ulcers? Who discovered the pathogen for the first time?

Solution

Peptic ulcers are caused by Helicobacter pylori, previously named Campylobacter pylori is a Gram-negative bacteria found in the stomach. It was identified in 1982 by Australian scientists Barry Marshall and Reborn Warren.

Q 22

What is an antibiotic? Give two examples.

Solution

Antibiotic is a chemical substance secreted by microorganisms which can kill bacteria. For example, penicillin and streptomycin.

Q 23

Pneumonia is an example of ________ disease.

Q 24

Many skin diseases are caused by ________.

Q 25

Antibiotics commonly block biochemical pathways important for the growth of ________.

Q 26

Living organisms carrying the infecting agents from one person to another are called ________.

Q 27

Name the target organs for the following diseases:

  1. Hepatitis targets ______________
  2. Fits or unconsciousness targets __________________
  3. Pneumonia targets _________________
  4. Fungal disease targets ______________
Solution
  1. liver
  2. Brain
  3. lungs
  4. skin
Q 28

Who discovered 'vaccine' for the first time? Name two diseases which can be prevented by using vaccine.

Solution

Vaccines were first developed by Edward Jenner for the treatment of smallpox. Polio and Tuberculosis can be prevented by using vaccine.

Q 29

________ disease continues for many days and causes ________ on body.

Q 30

________ disease continues for a few days and causes no longer term effect on body.

Q 31

________ is defined as physical, mental, and social well-being and comfort.

Q 32

Common cold is ________ disease.

Q 33

Classify the following diseases as infectious or non-infectious.

  1. AIDS
  2. Tuberculosis
  3. Cholera
  4. High blood pressure
  5. Heart disease
  6. Pneumonia
  7. Cancer
Solution

Infectious diseases are  - AIDS, tuberculosis, cholera, and pneumonia.
Non-infectious diseases are- High blood pressure, heart disease, and cancer.

Q 34

Name any two groups of micro-organisms from which antibiotics could be extracted.

Solution

Bacteria and fungi are the two groups of micro-organisms from which antibiotics could be extracted.

Q 35

Name any three diseases transmitted through vectors.

Solution

Malaria, dengue and chikungunya are the three diseases transmitted through vectors.

Q 36

Explain by giving reasons:

  1. Balanced diet is necessary for maintaining a healthy body.
  2. The health of an organism depends upon the surrounding environmental conditions.
  3. Our surroundings should be free from stagnant water.
  4. Social harmony and good economic conditions are necessary for good health.
Solution
  1. A balanced diet is a first and foremost condition necessary for good health. A balanced diet provides all the nutrients, e.g. proteins, carbohydrates, fats, vitamins and minerals required by the body in correct proportions. When our diet lacks one or more of these nutrients, we get deficiency diseases or nutritional disorders, e.g. if our diet lacks the mineral iron, we may get a disease called anemia.
    Excessive or inadequate intake of food and nutrients leads to conditions such as obesity, kwashiorkor, and rickets, e.g. vitamin-A causes hypervitaminosis-A and calcium causes rickets. A balanced diet prevents deficiency diseases. It also increases our ability to fight against infections in general.
  2. Our health depends on the cleanliness of our surroundings. Flies and mosquitoes carry germs that cause diseases. Flies breed in rotting garbage and mosquitoes breed in pools of stagnant water. So, people fall ill quite often if the area in which they live or work has garbage, stagnant water, or open drains. Clean air is also a part of our surroundings and a basic requirement for good health.
  3. This is so because many waterborne diseases causing insect vectors flourish in stagnant water, which cause diseases such as malaria, dengue, etc., in human beings.
  4. Social harmony and good economic conditions are necessary for good health. Social harmony involves each other’s participation in joys and sorrows, helping others, etc. If we mistreat each other, we cannot be happy or healthy. Proper earning is necessary to provide adequate and nutritious food and a clean environment for living. Thus, good economic condition is also a necessary factor for good health.
Q 37

What is a disease? How many types of diseases have you studied? Give examples.

Solution

Any deviation from the normal functioning or state of incomplete physical or mental well being is called disease. The disease is a condition that impairs the proper functioning of the body or one of its parts
The disease may be a response to

  1. Environmental factors (as malnutrition, industrial hazards or climate).
  2. Specific infective agents (as worms, protozoans, fungi, bacteria or viruses).
  3. Inherent defects of the organism (as genetic anomalies).
  4. Combination of these factors. 
Different Types of Disease Causes of Diseases Examples
Physical Temporary or permanent damage to any body part Bone fracture, leprosy, strain or sprain.
Infectious Invasion of the body by microorganisms (viruses, bacteria, fungi, protozoans, nematodes, insects, etc) Typhoid, diarrhoea, malaria, hepatitis, AIDS, TB. polio, influenza, cholera
Deficiency An inadequate diet or lack of a balanced diet Kwashiorkor, marasmus, scurvy, rickets, anaemia, goitre, beriberi.
Inherited Defective genes are passed from parents to their offspring.  Haemophilia, sickle cell anaemia, diabetes.
Degenerative Organs and tissues wear away and do not work well with the age Arthritis, hearing and sight defects, gout.
Mental Brain damage or psychological illness Depression, paranoia.
Social Social interaction with family, friends and strangers. Drug addiction, alcoholism, smoking unhealthy life-styles, social isolation.
Q 38

What do you mean by disease symptoms? Explain giving two examples?

Solution

Symptoms are the evidence that point to the presence of a disease they are visible in the form of either structural or functional changes in our body or any of its parts. When the functioning or the appearance of one or more systems of the body will change for the worse, it gives certain abnormal signs of the disease. On the basis of these symptoms, doctors search for definite clues or sign for a particular disease.
Examples:

  1. Lesions on the skin are the symptoms of chickenpox.
  2. Cough is the symptom of lung infection.
  3. The symptoms for malaria and typhoid are same like fever, weakness, headache etc. But signs for the disease are different as malaria fever manifests through chills and that of typhoid fever are rashes, stomach pain etc.
Q 39

Why is immune system essential for our health?

Solution

The immune system of our body is a defense mechanism to fight against pathogenic microbes. It has cells that are specialised to kill infecting microbes and keep our body healthy. If the body’s immune system is strong, it can easily fight pathogens, keeping us healthy.

Q 40

What precautions will you take to justify "prevention is better than cure".

Solution

When someone gets a disease:

  1. His/her body function gets damaged and may never recover completely.
  2. He/she may become bedridden for some time.
  3. He/she can serve as a medium for further spread of disease.

Therefore, prevention is better than cure. For the prevention of diseases, the following general practices are adopted:

  1. Avoid exposure to airborne microbes.
  2. Adopt living conditions that are not overcrowded.
  3. Prevent exposure to waterborne microbes.
  4. Safe drinking water should be provided.
  5. Avoid vector-borne infection. Establish a clean environment as it would not allow the breeding of disease spreading vectors.
Q 41

Why do some children fall ill more frequently than others living in the same locality?

Solution

Frequency of falling ill depends on the strength of the body's immune system to fight common pathogens. Due to poor immune system, some children fall ill frequently. Balanced diet and proper nutrition for healthy body is required to have a strong immune system.

Q 42

Why are antibiotics not effective for viral disease.

Solution

Antibiotics block the bacterial pathways without affecting the pathways of humans. In the case of viruses, they have very few biochemical pathways of their own. They use the metabolic machinery of host and grow and reproduce. Since these biochemical processes are the targets of antibiotics, antibiotics do not work on viruses.

Q 43

Becoming exposed to or infected with an infectious microbe does not necessarily mean developing noticeable disease. Explain.

Solution

Because of strong immune system, our body is normally fighting off microbes. We have cells which are specialised to kill the pathogenic microbes. These cells are active when infecting microbes enter the body and if they are successful in removing the pathogen, we remain disease-free. So even if we are exposed to infectious microbes, it is not necessary that we suffer from diseases.

Q 44

Give any four factors necessary for a healthy person.

Solution

For a healthy person it is necessary that

  1. the surrounding environment should be clean. Air and water-borne diseases should not spread.
  2. personal hygiene is maintained to prevent infectious diseases.
  3. proper, sufficient nourishment and food is available for good immune system of our body.
  4. body is immunised against severe diseases.
Q 45

Why is AIDS considered to be a 'syndrome' and not a disease?

Solution

AIDS causing virus—HIV that comes into the body via the sexual organs or blood transfusion will spread all over the body through lymph nodes. The virus damages the immune system of the body and due to this the body can no longer fight off many minor infections. Instead, every small disease like cold can become severe pneumonia or minor gut infection can become severe diarrhoea with blood loss. The effect of disease becomes very severe and complex, at times killing the person suffering from AIDS. Hence, there is no specific disease symptom for AIDS but it results in a complex disease. Therefore, it is known as a syndrome.

Q 31
River from land, add minerals to sea water. Discuss how?
Solution

As rivers  pass through land it takes with the flowing water the soluble minerals present in the rocks , soil and adds it into the sea water.

Q 32

How can we prevent the loss of topsoil?

Solution

Loss of topsoil can be prevented by:

  1. increasing the vegetation cover
  2. checking the felling of trees or deforestation
  3. preventing excessive grazing by animals to ensure a constant vegetation cover on the Earth’s surface.
Q 33

How is the life of organisms living in water affected when water gets polluted?

Solution

Addition of undesirable chemicals like pesticides, fertilisers, industrial wastes and domestic wastes not only kill the aquatic organisms, they also cause water¬borne diseases. With the addition of pollutants, the phytoplankton and other organisms require more oxygen for their degradation, thus decreasing the amount of oxygen available in water. Due to this reduction in the dissolved oxygen in water, there are adverse effects on the aquatic organisms leading to their deaths.Many aquatic species are on the brink of extinction.Polluted water also negatively impacts the breeding power of aquatic life. It makes fish and plants deficient in their ability to regenerate and reproduce.

Q 34

During summer, if you go near the lake, you feel relief from the heat, why?

Solution

During summer, due to the high temperature there is evaporation of the water. This results in a comparative lower temperature on water surface as heat is used from surroundings and thus the air near the water bodies is cooler.

Q 35

In coastal area, wind current moves from the sea towards the land during but during night it moves from land to the sea. Discuss the reason.

Solution

Air moves from a region of high pressure to a region of low pressure. Air above the land gets heated quickly during day and starts rising. This creates a region of low pressure as a result of which air above the sea rushes into this area of low pressure. This movement of air from one region to the other creates winds. During night, as water cools down slowly, the air above water is warmer than the air on land. So, air moves from land to sea creating winds.

Q 36

Following are a few organisms

  1. lichen
  2. mosses
  3. mango tree
  4. cactus

Which among the above can grow on stones and also help in formation of soil? Write the mode of their action for making soil.

Solution

Lichens and mosses grow on rocks. They release chemical substances which break down the stones resulting in the formation of soil. Lichens grow on the rock surface, extract minerals from them. This creates small crevices at places where a thin layer of soil builds up. Mosses grow over these crevices and cause deepening of the crevices and result in build sup of more soil inside them. Deeper crevices form cracks and cracks become wider and deeper when the roots of short lived herbs pass into them. With the passage of time, the roots of bigger plants, (e.g., peepal, banyan tree) pass into the cracks. Cracks gradually widen and cause slow fragmentation and eventually pulverization of rocks.

Q 37

Soil formation is done by both abiotic and biotic factors. List the names of these factors by classifying them as abiotic and biotic.

Solution

Soil formation can be both by biotic and biotic factors.

Biotic factors includes biological i.e. living factors like plants, animals. When plants grow their roots penetrate deep inside the ground and result in breaking of soil particles and formation of soil. Certain lichens and microbes secrete substances which also leads to weathering (process of formation of soil) of rock and formation of soil. Human activities such as mining, digging also result in weathering and formation of soil. 

Abiotic factors means physical factors such as temperature, wind and water. When water flow with high speed it results in breaking of rocks. Wind also result in weathering when blow with high speed. The difference in the temperature of the day and night time results in expansion and contraction of the rocks leading to weathering and formation of soil.

Q 38
All the living organisms are basically made up of C, N, S, P, H and O. How do they enter the living forms? Discuss.
Solution

Plants take up C and H by the process of photosynthesis and the rest of the minerals are absorbed from the soil. Consumers take in O  by the process of respiration and the rest of the minerals are taken via food.

Q 39
Why does the percentage of gases like oxygen, nitrogen and carbon dioxide remain almost the same in the atmosphere?
Solution

Cycling of these gases maintains consistency of these gases. These are taken up and released by almost every living organism and thus their concentration in the atmosphere is maintained.

Q 40

Why does moon have very cold and very hot temperature variations, e.g., from -190°C to 110°C, even though it is at the same distance from the Sun as the Earth is?

Solution

Because moon has no atmosphere. Earth atmosphere protects life on the earth by absorbing ultraviolet radiation coming from the sun and maintaining the average temperature on the surface (not too hot and not too cold) through green house effect.

Q 41

Why do people love to fly kites near the seashore?

Solution

Due to the uneven heating of land and sea, there is movement of air leading to wind formation near seashore. Because of the wind created during day time people fly kites near seashore.

Q 42

Why does Mathura refinery pose problems to the Taj Mahal?

Solution

Mathura refinery releases toxic gases (like oxides of sulphur and nitrogen) which results in acid rain. Acid rain is corrosive in nature and causes discoloration and damage to the white marble of Taj Mahal.

Q 43

Why do lichens not occur in Delhi whereas they commonly grow in Manali or Darjeeling?

Solution

Lichen is a bio-indicator and sensitive to SO2 pollution from automobiles. Delhi has maximum automobile pollution, hence has a highly polluted environment. Due to the polluted environment, lichens do not survive in Delhi whereas they are present in the pollution-free environment of Darjeeling or Manali.

Q 44

Why does water need conservation even though large oceans surround the land masses?

Solution

Water needs conservation –

  1. Due to increasing population, use of water has increased in large quantities for domestic as well as industrial and agricultural purposes
  2. Due to increased water pollution.there is shortage of availablity of  pure water.
  3. Due to uncertain  and reduction in rainfall ground water level has decreased 
  4. Inceased population increases urbanization , thus consumption of water for urban development has increased
Q 45

There is mass mortality of fishes in a pond. What may be the reasons?

Solution
  1. Addition of hot water.
  2. Addition of poisonous compounds like mercury and cadmium etc in water. (due to disposal of chemical cells)
  3. Blockage of gills of fishes with any pollutant.
Q 46

Lichens are called pioneer colonisers of bare rock. How can they help in formation of soil?

Solution

Lichens are the first organisms to grow on bare rocks and hence are called pioneer colonizers of bare rock. They release certain enzymes which break the rocks. Thus, lichens help in formation of soil.

Q 47

“Soil is formed by water.” If you agree to this statement then give reasons for your answer.

Solution

Water helps in the formation of soil in the following ways:

  1. Water enters rocks through the cracks on them.
  2. The water freezes at low temperature, thereby expanding the rocks.
  3. This repeated expansion and contraction of rocks over time leads to their breaking down into smaller pieces .
  4. Water also wears down rocks by flowing over them for a long period of time.
  5. The flow of water creates friction between the water and the rocks.
  6. This friction breaks down the rocks, ultimately, soil is formed.The resultant soil particles may flow along with the water and get deposited.
Q 48

Fertile soil has lots of humus. Why?

Solution

Fertile soil has lots of humus because it contains microorganisms that decompose dead organic matter present in soil and help in the formation of humus. Humus is a source of the mineral, absorbs water, and makes the soil porous for easy passage of plant roots.

Q 49

Why step farming is common in hills?

Solution

Step farming is practised in hills to check soil erosion through water currents on the slopes.

Q 50

Why are root nodules useful for the plants?

Solution

Root nodules of leguminous plants harbor nitrogen-fixing bacteria. These bacteria fix atmospheric nitrogen into nitrogen compounds which can be absorbed by plants. Thus, root nodules are of great help not only to leguminous plants but also to other plants.

Q 51

How do fossil fuels causes air pollution?

Solution

Burning of fossil fuels has following effects –

  1. Burning of fossil fuels releases oxides of nitrogen and sulphur. It causes respiratory problems as well as acid rain.
  2. Burning of fossil fuels increase amount of suspended particles in air, which forms smog during winter. It reduces visibility and causes accidents on roads.
  3. On burning it also releases CO2 which traps Solar energy and increases the temperature of the earth.
Q 52

What are the causes of water pollution? Discuss how you can contribute to reducing water pollution.

Solution

Water pollution is caused due to the following reasons are:-

  1. Water dissolves fertilizers and pesticides from agricultural fields with surface runoff rainwater. It takes them to the nearby water bodies, where they cause harmful effects on aquatic life.
  2. Dumping of sewage and other wastes into water bodies adversely affects the water quality.
  3. Industries use water for cooling in various operations. Later this hot water is released into water bodies. This causes sudden changes in the temperature of the waterbody that affects the breeding of aquatic organisms.

The following activities help to control water pollution:

  1. Sewage and garbage generated by homes and industries should be treated properly before discharging into water sources. It will reduce water pollution, causing less harm to aquatic life.
  2. If hot water generated by the industries is collected at a commonplace and then cooled before it is discharged into the water bodies, it will not affect the breeding capacity of aquatic organisms.
  3. If commercial areas, factories, and industries are shifted to isolated areas (i.e. far away from residential areas), it can reduce the effect of water pollution on people.
Q 53

A motor car, with its glass totally closed, is parked directly under the Sun. The inside temperature of the car rises very high. Explain why?

Solution

Infrared radiations in sunlight pass through the glass and heat the interior of the car. The radiation emitted by upholstery and other inner parts of the car cannot pass out of the glass, so the heat trapped inside raises the temperature of the interior. This is because glass is transparent to infrared radiation from the Sun having smaller wavelengths than that emitted by the interior of the car which are of longer wavelengths to which the glass is opaque.

Q 54

Justify "Dust is a pollutant".

Solution

Dust is present in air as suspended particles. It can cause allergy and other respiratory diseases. It also affects plant growth by covering stomata on leaf surface and blocking them. It acts as the carrier of toxic compounds like heavy metals. Thus, dust acts as a pollutant.

Q 55
Explain the role of the Sun in the formation of soil.
Solution

During the daytime, the rocks are heated up by the Sun and thus they expand. Whereas, at night they cool down and thus contract. Since all parts of the rock do not expand and contract at the same rate, it results in cracking of rocks, breaking them into smaller pieces.

Q 56

Carbon dioxide is necessary for plants. Why do we consider it as a pollutant?

Solution

Plants require CO2 in an optimum amount for the process of photosynthesis. But high concentration of (more than normal) CO2 is harmful and considered as a pollutant .
It is considered as a pollutant because carbon dioxide is a green house gas and its excessive amount in the atmosphere results in global warming. It increases the overall temperature of the earth, resulting in changes in the earth’s climate. During the last century, the temperature of earth has increased by 0.6ºC. This increase in temperature is ultimately believed to cause the  melting of polar ice caps, rise in the sea level, and submerging of the coastal areas.
Besides that, higher concentration of carbon dioxide may also cause suffocation, hard breathing and choking problems in humans. 
Also it leads to many environmental problems due to the absorption of Infra red radiations

Q 24
What is a GM crop? Name any one such crop which is grown in India.
Solution

Crop which has been developed by introducing a new gene from a different source, to obtain the desired character, is called genetically modified (GM) crop. For example, Bt cotton which is made insect-resistant by introducing a new gene from a bacteria.

Q 25

List out some useful traits in improved crops?

Solution

Improved crops show the following useful traits:

  1. High yielding varieties
  2. Improved nutritional quality
  3. Better agronomic qualities
  4. Resistance to biotic and abiotic stress
  5. Better adaptability for different conditions
  6. Change in maturity
Q 26

Why is organic matter important for crop production?

Solution

Organic matter is important for crop production because:

  1. It helps in improving soil structure by improving the soil fertility.
  2. It helps in increasing water holding capacity of sandy soil.
  3. In clayey soil large quantities of organic matter helps in drainage and in avoiding waterlogging.
Q 27
Why is excess use of fertilisers detrimental for the environment?
Solution

Fertilisers are inorganic chemicals which are not easily degraded. Excess use of fertilisers causes environmental pollution as their residual and unused amounts will become pollutants for air, water and soil.

Q 28

Farming without the use of chemicals as fertilizers, herbicides, and pesticides is known as ________.

Solution

Organic farming

Q 29

Farming without the use of chemicals as fertilizers, herbicides, and pesticides is known as ________.

Q 30

Growing of wheat and groundnut on the same field is called as ________.

Q 31

Planting soyabean and maize in alternate rows in the same field is called as ________.

Q 32

Growing different crops on a piece of land in pre-planned succession is known as ________.

Q 33

Xanthium and Parthenium are commonly known as ________.

Q 34

The causal organism of any disease is called as ________.

Q 35

Match the column A with column B

(A) (B)
(a) Cattle used for tilling and carting (i) Milk producing female
(b) Indian breed of chicken (ii) Broiler
(c) Sahiwal, Red Sindhi (iii) Drought animals
(d) Milch (iv) Local breed of cattle
(e) Chicken better fed for obtaining meat (v) Aseel
Solution

The correct order of match is given as (a) - (iii), (b) - (v), (c) - (iv), (d) - (i), (e) - (ii).

Q 36

If there is low rainfall in a village throughout the  year, what measures will you suggest to the farmers for better cropping?

Solution

For better cropping in low rainfall areas, farmers can be suggested to:

  1. Practice farming with drought-resistant and early maturing varieties of crops.
  2. To enrich the soil with more humus content as it increases the water-holding capacity and retains water for longer duration.
Q 37

Group the following and tabulate them as energy yielding, protein yielding, oil yielding and fodder crop.
Wheat, rice, berseem, maize, gram, oat, pigeon gram, sudan grass, lentill, soyabean, groundnut, castor and mustard.

Solution

1. Energy yielding — wheat, rice, maize
2. Protein yielding — gram, pigeon gram, lentil, soyabean
3. Oil yielding — groundnut, castor, mustard, soyabean
4. Fodder crops — berseem, oat, sudan grass

Q 38

Define the term hybridization and photoperiod.

Solution

Hybridization - Hybridization refers to crossing between genetically dissimilar organisms to produce offsprings with a desirable combination of characters.
Photoperiod - Duration of sunlight available to the plant is called as photoperiod. It affects the growth, flowering, and maturation of crops.

Q 39

Photoperiod affect the ________.

Q 40

Kharif crops are cultivated from ________ to ________.

Q 41

Rabi crops are cultivated from ________ to ________.

Q 42

Paddy, maize, green gram, and black gram are ________ crops.

Q 43

Wheat, gram, pea, mustard are ________ crops.

Q 44

Cultivation practices and crop yield are related to environmental conditions. Explain.

Solution

Cultivation practices and crop yield are related to environmental conditions. All crops do not grow under similar conditions. Environmental factors like temperature, climate conditions, etc. play a vital role in the growth of all the plants. Some require high temperature, some low temperature, longer duration of sunlight, shorter duration of sunlight, more humidity, low humidity, moderate humidity, loam soil, sandy soil, etc.
For example, in the case of Apple, it cannot be grown in plains because it requires several days of low temperature. In plains, there are two major seasons of crop plants, kharif (rainy season) and rabi (winter season) crops in India can be classified into three main groups:

  1. Rabi crop: It is a winter season crop grown from November to April.
  2. Kharif crop: It is a rainy season crop grown from June to October.
  3. Zaid crop: It is a summer season crop grown from April to June.
Q 45

A total of ________ nutrients are essential to plants.

Q 46

________ and ________ are supplied by air to plants.

Q 47

________ is supplied by water to plants.

Q 48

Soil supply ________  mineral nutrients to plants.

Q 49

Six nutrients are required in large quantity and called as ________.

Q 50

Seven nutrients are needed in small quantity for plants and are called ________.

Q 51
Differentiate between compost and vermicompost?
Solution

Compost: It is prepared by the process in which farm waste materials like livestock excreta, vegetable wastes, animal refuse, domestic waste, straw, eradicated weeds are decomposed and used as manure.
Vermicompost: It is the compost prepared from organic matter using earthworms which hasten the process of decomposition.

Q 52

Arrange these statements in correct sequence of preparation of green manure.

  1. Green plants are decomposed in soil.
  2. Green plants are cultivated for preparing manure or crop plant parts are used.
  3. Plants are ploughed and mixed into the soil.
  4. After decomposition it becomes green manure.
Solution

(b) {tex} \to {/tex} (c) {tex} \to {/tex} (a) {tex} \to {/tex} (d)

Q 53

An Italian bee variety A. mellifera has been introduced in India for honey production. Write about its merits over other varieties.

Solution

Merits of Italian bee variety A. mellifera are:

  1. It stings less.
  2. It has high honey collection capacity.
  3. It stays in the given beehive for longer periods and breeds very well.
Q 54

In agricultural practices, higher input gives higher yield. Discuss how?

Solution

A farmer needs to put higher inputs if he wants to get higher yield from his farm. Inputs can be in the form of better seeds, latest farm equipments, better irrigation, use of fertilizers and manures, etc. But all these inputs need a higher input of money. Higher yield thus can be obtained only by pumping a higher amount of money if farming.

Q 55

Discuss the role of hybridisation in crop improvement.

Solution

The success of the Green Revolution was based on hybridization; apart from providing better facilities for irrigation and the use of chemical fertilizers.
High Yielding Varieties (HYV) seeds were created through hybridization. HYV seeds provided better yield because plants grown from HYV seeds were having many desirable characteristics. These plants were able to giver higher yield; in terms of per acre output. These plants could be grown in a variety of conditions; which was essential for a large country like India. HYV seeds ensured the improved output of rice, wheat, and maize.
New varieties of fruits could be made because of hybridization. Many new varieties of mango now begin to bear fruit in a shorter duration; and also give large fruits. Many new varieties of papaya are much bigger and seedless, and this could be possible because of hybridization.
Thus, it can be said that hybridization helped in improving food security in India; by improving crop production.

Q 56

Define Vermicompost.

Solution

Vermicompost - Compost is a kind of manure rich in organic matter and nutrients. The compost prepared by using earthworms to hasten the process of decomposition of plant and animal refuse is called as vermicompost.

Q 57

Define green manure?

Solution

Green manure: Some plants like sun hemp or guar are grown in the field prior to the sowing of the crop seeds. These are then mulched by ploughing them into the soil. These green plants gradually decompose and turn into green manure which helps in enriching the soil in nitrogen and phosphorus.

Q 58

Define Bio fertilizer.

Solution

Bio fertilizer - Living organisms which are used as fertilizer to supply nutrients to the  plants, are called as biofertilizers. For example, blue green algae, which fix nitrogen in soil, rice fields etc is a biofertilizer.

Q 59

Discuss various methods for weed control.

Solution

Various modes of weed control are:

  1. Mechanical removal.
  2. Prevention method: Proper seed bed preparation to avoid weed growth.
  3. Timely sowing of crop to avoid the growth of weed.
  4. Inter-cropping and crop rotation also help in weed control.
Q 60

Differentiate between the Capture fishery and Culture fishery.

Solution

Capture fishery is the method of obtaining fish from natural resources while culture fishery is the method of obtaining fish by fish farming.

Q 61

Differentiate between the mixed cropping and Inter-cropping.

Solution
Mixed Cropping

Inter Cropping

.

(a) Aim is to minimize risk of crop failure.

(a) Aim is to increase productivity per unit area

(b) Seeds of component crops are mixed before sowing.

(b) Seeds of component crops are not mixed.

(c) Sowing is not done in rows.

(c) Sowing is done in rows in a  precise pattern.
Q 62

Differentiate between the Bee keeping and Poultry farming.

Solution

Bee keeping is the practice of rearing  honey bees for obtaining honey while poultry farming is the practice of raising the domestic fowl for egg and meat production.

Q 63
Give the merits and demerits of fish culture.
Solution

Demerits: (i) Threat to biodiversity, (ii) only economically important and valued fishes will be cultured.
Merits: (i) Large amount of desired fishes can be obtained in small area,
(ii) improvement can be done.

Q 64

What do you understand by composite fish culture?

Solution

Composite fish culture is the method to culture five or six species, both indigenous and exotic, together in a single fish pond. These species are selected so that they do not compete for food among themselves having different types of food habits. As a result food available in all the parts of the pond is used.
For example— Catlas are surface feeders, Rohu is middle zone feeder and Mrigals and common carps are bottom feeders.
Fishes are selected on the basis of the following characteristics.

  1. The selected species are fast-growing. For example, when common Indian carps
    (rohu, catla, and mrigal) and exotic carps (silver carp, grass carp, and common carp) are selected for culturing, they give 8-9 times more yield.
  2. The fishes selected should not compete with each other for space and nutrition. They should live in distinct zones inside the pond. For example, catla and silver carp live mostly in the surface zone, rohu and grass carp live in the middle zone and mrigal and common carp live at the bottom.
  3. The feeding habits of species selected should be distinct. For example, silver carp feeds on phytoplankton, Catla feeds on zooplanktons, rohu and mrigal feed on decaying plants and detritus, grass carp feeds on aquatic plants and weeds. Thus, they can be grown together. Composite fish farming is advantageous, economical, and profitable from a business point of view.
Q 65

Why should beekeeping be done in good pasturage?

Solution

Beekeeping is done in good pasturage because good pasturage provides more quantity and better quality of nectar for honey to the honeybees.

Q 66
Write the modes by which insects affect the crop yield.
Solution

Insects have a damaging effect on the crop yield. Some insects cut the plant parts inhibiting their growth while others suck the cell sap so bees cannot help in pollination. Some insects are even seen as the bore which damage the entire crop yield.

Q 67

Discuss why pesticides are used in very accurate concentration and in very appropriate manner?

Solution

Pesticides are used in very accurate concentration and in a very appropriate manner because if used in excess it does the following things:

  1. harms the soil and causes loss of fertility,
  2. cause poisonous to many plant and animal.
  3. kills the microorganism of soil,
  4. causes air, water, and soil pollution.
Q 68

Name two types of animal feed and write their functions.

Solution

The two types of animal feed are:

  1. Roughage: These are rich in fibre; e.g., cowpea, berseem, etc.
  2. Concentrates: These are nutrient-rich and low on fibres; e.g., oats, maize, etc.
Q 69

What would happen if poultry birds are larger in size and have summer adaptation capacity? In order to get small-sized poultry birds having summer adaptability, what method will be employed?

Solution

Maintenance of temperature is needed for better egg production by poultry birds. Therefore, larger size (increase in surface area of body) and adaptability of summer may cause decline in egg production. To obtain smaller size and higher summer adaptability, cross-breeding of poultry birds are done. Smaller size is also needed for better housing and low feed.

Q 70

Suggest some preventive measures for the diseases of poultry birds.

Solution

Following methods should be applied for prevention of diseases in poultry birds:

  1. Maintenance of hygiene in bird enclosure.
  2. Use of disinfectants at regular intervals.
  3. Isolation of any infected bird.
  4. Periodic vaccination of birds.
Q 71

Figure shows the two crop Heads [plots A and B] have been treated by manures and chemical fertilisers respectively, keeping the environmental factors same.
Observe the graph and answer the following questions.

  1. Why does plot B show sudden increase and then gradual decrease in yield?
  2. Why is the highest peak in plot A graph slightly delayed?
  3. What is the reason for the different pattern of the two graphs?
Solution
  1. With the addition of chemical fertilisers there is sudden increase in yield due to release of nutrients N,P,K, etc. in high quantity. The gradual decline in the graph may be due to continuous use and high quantity of chemicals which kills microbes useful for replenishing the organic matter in the soil. This decreases the soil fertility.
  2. Manures supply small quantities of nutrients to the soil slowly as it contains large amounts of organic matter [Hint: Importance of organic matter can be included]. It enriches soil with nutrients; thereby increasing soil fertility continuously.
  3. The differences in the two graphs indicate that use of manure is beneficial for long duration in cropping as the yield tends to remain high when the quantity of manure increases.

In case of plot B the chemical fertilisers may cause various problems when used continuously for long time. Loss of microbial activity reduces decomposition of organic matter and as a result, soil fertility is lost that affects the yield.

Q 72

Complete the crossword puzzle in a given figure.

Across
1. Oil yielding plant (9)
3. Crop grown in winter season (4)
5. Fixed by Rhizobium (8)
9. Common honey bee (4)
Downward
2. Animal feed (6)
4. A micronutrient (5)
6. Unwanted plant in crop fields (4)
7. An exotic breed of chicken(7)
8. Bottom feeders in the fish pond(7)
10. A marine fish (4)

Solution

After observing the puzzle we have
Across
1.Sun Flower
3. Rabi
5. Nitrogen
9. Apis
Downward
2. Fodder
4. Boron
6. Weed
7. Leghorn
8. Mrigala
10. Tuna

sections.label: In Text
sections.html:
Q 1

Which of the following are matter? Chair, air, love, smell, hate, almonds, thought, cold, lemon water, smell of perfume

Solution

Chair, air, almonds, lemon water

Q 2

Give reasons for the following observation:
The smell of hot sizzling food reaches you several metres away, but to get the smell from cold food you have to go close.

Solution

Since hot sizzling food has temperature higher than the cold food and at higher temperature diffusion rate (movement) of particles is very fast due to this the smell of hot sizzling food reaches us from several metres away but to get the smell from cold food you have to go close.

Q 3

A diver is able to cut through water in a swimming pool. Which property of matter does this observation show?

Solution
This shows that in water which represents the liquid state of matter, there are sufficient inter particle spaces. A diver is able to cut through water. He might not do the same had these spaces been very small (e.g. in solid state).
Q 4

What are the characteristics of the particles of matter.

Solution

The important characteristics of the particles of matter are as :
1. Every matter is made up of particles.
2. The particles constituting a matter are very small in size.
3. The particles have empty or vacant spaces in them known as interparticular spaces.
4. Particles are not stationary and are in a state of motion.
5. Attractive forces are present in the particles of a substance. These are called interparticular forces.
6. The motion of particles increases with the rise in temperature due to increased kinetic energy.

Q 5

The mass per unit volume of a substance is called density. (density = mass/volume).
Arrange the following in order of increasing density - air, exhaust from chimneys, honey, water, chalk, cotton and iron.

Solution

Arranging substances in their increasing order of densities: Air< exhaust from chimneys< cotton< water< honey< chalk< iron.  

Q 6

Tabulate the differences in the characteristics of states of matter.

Solution

Differences between solid, liquid and gaseous states are as follows 

Property Solid Liquid Gas
Shape They have definite shape. They do not have a definite shape. They do not have a definite shape.
Volume They have fixed volume. They do not have fixed volume. They do not have fixed volume.
Density They have high-density. Liquids have less density than solids. Gases have the least density.
The kinetic energy of particle Least More than solid. Maximum
Compressibility Negligible Low High
Q 7

Comment upon the following:
Rigidity, compressibility, fluidity, filling a gas container, shape, kinetic energy, and density.

Solution

Rigidity: The property due to which an object retains its shape and size is known as rigidity. Solids are rigid while liquids and gases are not.
Compressibility: The property due to which a substance reduced to its lower volume when force is applied is called compressibility. Gases are the most compressible while solids and liquids are not.
Fluidity: The property due to which a substance tends to flow is known as fluidity. Gases and liquids can flow, hence they are known as fluids.
Filling gas container: Particles of a gas move freely in all the directions and occupy all the space available to them. Hence, gas fills the container completely.
Shape: Solids have fixed shapes and boundaries. Liquid and gases have no fixed shape and definite boundaries.
Kinetic energy: The energy of particles of matter due to their movement is called their kinetic energy. Gases have maximum kinetic energy among the three states of matter. Kinetic energy increases with the rise in temperature and vice-versa.
Density: The mass per unit volume of a substance is called its density.
Density = {tex}\frac{Mass}{Volume}{/tex} or D = {tex}\frac{m}{V}{/tex}
Generally, a substance has a maximum density in its solid-state as compared to a liquid or gaseous state. The units of density are kgm-3 or gm-3.

Q 8

A gas fills completely the vessel in which it is kept. Give reason.

Solution

A gas fills completely the vessel in which it is kept because of the fast diffusion of the particles in a gas. The number of vacant spaces or voids in the gaseous state is very large. This means that the particles of a gas move at a very fast speed. They readily fill completely the vessel in which the gas is kept. Thus the volume of the gas is the same as that of the vessel.

Q 9

A gas exerts pressure on the walls of the container. Give reason.

Solution

The molecules in a gas have large kinetic energy. They collide with each other and also strike the walls of the container with certain force and impart momentum to them. This force per unit area or momentum is responsible for the pressure of the gas.

Q 10

A wooden table should be called a solid. Give reason.

Solution

A wooden table should be called a solid because it matches the characteristics of the solid-state. Such as,

  1. It is very hard and rigid.
  2. Its shape cannot be changed by altering temperature or pressure.
  3. It is quite heavy which means high density.
  4. There is no movement of the constituent particles present.
Q 11

We can easily move our hand in the air but to do the same through a solid block of wood we need a karate expert. Given reason

Solution

We can easily move our hand in the air but to do the same through a solid block of wood we need a karate expert. It is because the molecules of the air have less force of attraction between them and a very small external force can separate them and pass through them.

Q 12

Liquids generally have lower density as compared to solids. But you must have observed that ice floats on water. Find out why.

Solution

This is due to a peculiar property of water.The density of water increases by decreasing temperature as all liquids upto 4 degree celcius and attains the maximum density.On further cooling water molecules expand and its density ( mass per unit volume) starts decreasing. Thus ice  has a lower density than water and it floats on water.

Q 13

Convert the temperature to Celsius scale: 300 K

Solution

K= 273+ oC
Therefore, to convert 300k into oC = K - 273 = 300 - 273 = 27oC

Q 14

Convert the temperature to Celsius scale: 573 K

Solution

Temperature in °C = 573 K – 273.15 K = 299.85°C.

Q 15

What is the physical state of water at:

  1. 250oC
  2. 100oC
Solution

250oC - The boiling point of water is 100oC. Above this temperature water is converted into steam. Therefore, water will be in gaseous state.
100oC - Water boils at 100∘C. Water can exists in both liquid as well as gaseous state.

Q 16

For any substance, why does the temperature remain constant during the change of state?

Solution

Once the change of state of a substance begins or starts, the energy which is now supplied is being used up as latent heat. It means that it does not increase the kinetic energy of the particles and is used up only to bring about a change in state. Therefore the temperature becomes constant. Once the state is changed, the kinetic energy starts increasing.

Q 17

Suggest a method to liquefy atmospheric gases.

Solution

The atmospheric gases can be liquefied either by increasing pressure or by decreasing temperature.

Q 18

Why does a desert cooler cool better on a hot dry day?

Solution

The humidity level in the atmosphere is quite low and the evaporation rate of water expected to be high. Evaporation is inversely proportional to humidity. Since cooling is caused during evaporation, a cooler is quite effective on a hot and dry day.

Q 19

How does the water kept in an earthen pot (matka) become cool during summer?

Solution

During summers the water present on the surface of the earthen pot evaporates which causes the cooling effect. Besides earthen pot bears pores on it hence water continuously oozes out from it and thus evaporation occurs continuously and so does the cooling.

Q 20

Why does our palm feel cold when we put some acetone or petrol or perfume on it?

Solution

Some liquids get quickly vapourised and they are called volatile liquids. Acetone, petrol and perfume are also volatile liquids therefore they get heat from our palm and cause cooling.

Q 21

Why are we able to sip hot tea or milk faster from a saucer rather than a cup?

Solution

Saucer has a bigger surface area as compared to cup. Since evaporation is a surface phenomenon, by using a saucer instead of cup we are increasing the surface area for evaporation to occur. Faster evaporation of particles of tea or milk allows cooling and taking a sip becomes easier.

Q 22
What type of clothes should we wear in summer?
Solution

Cotton is a good absorbant of water, hence it absorbs sweat quite well. The pores in the fabric expose that sweat to easy evaporation, hence we should prefer wearing cotton clothes in summer.

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Convert the given temperature to the celsius scale: 293 K

Solution

Temperature in °C = 293K - 273K =  20oC

Q 2

Convert the given temperature to the celsius scale: 470 K

Solution

Temperature in °C = 470K - 273K  = 197oC.

Q 3

Convert the given temperature to the Kelvin scale: 25oC

Solution

Kelvin temperature (K) = 273 + 25 = 298 K.

Q 4

Convert the given temperature to the Kelvin scale: 373oC

Solution

As we know that,
K = C + 273
Temperature on Kelvin scale = 373oC + 273 = 646 K

Q 5

Naphthalene balls disappear with time without leaving any solid. Give reason.

Solution

Naphthalene has a tendency to sublime i.e. it changes directly to the gaseous state. Therefore, the size of the naphthalene balls slowly decreases and ultimately they disappear. No solid residue is left.

Q 6

We can get the smell of perfume sitting several metres away. Give reason.

Solution

Perfume is actually a mixture of a number of pleasant-smelling vapours. Their particle diffuses quite fast and can reach a person who may be even at several metres away from a person who has used perfume.

Q 7

Arrange the following substances in increasing order of forces of attraction between the particles— water, sugar, oxygen.

Solution

Oxygen< water< sugar.

Q 8

What is the physical state of water at 25oC?

Solution

At 25oC - water is liquid, 

Q 9

What is the physical state of water at - 0oC

Solution

The physical state of water at - 0oC  is solid (ice).

Q 10

What is the physical state of water at 100oC.

Solution

The physical state of water at 100oC is gas (water vapour).

Q 11

Give two reasons to justify Water at room temperature is a liquid   

Solution

Water is a liquid at room temperature (25oC) due to the following reasons:
(i) When placed in a beaker, its level cannot be changed on pressing.
(ii) It can take the shape of any container in which it is placed.

Q 12

Give two reasons to justify an iron almirah is a solid at room temperature.

Solution

An iron almirah is a solid due to following reasons:
(i) It has definite volume and shape. This means that it is hard and rigid.
(ii) It has high melting point and it's density is very high.

Q 13

Why is ice at 273 K more effective in cooling than water at the same temperature?

Solution

While melting ice absorbs latent heat of melting from the surroundings and gets changed into water that makes the cooling effect more intense as compared to water at same temperature.

Q 14

What produces more severe burns, boiling water or steam?

Solution

As compared to boiling water it is observed that steam produces more severe burns since as the steam changes into boiling water it releases heat of condensation which is equivalent to latent heat of water that's why the result is more severe burning.

Q 15

Name A, B, C, D, E and F in the following diagram showing change in its state.

Solution

A – Fusion (Heating – Melting)
B – Vapourisation
C – Cooling – Condensation (Liquefaction)
D – Cooling – Freezing (Solidification)
E – Sublimation
F – Solidification or sublimation.

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1

A solution contains 40 g of common salt in 320 g of water. Calculate the concentration in terms of mass by mass percentage of the solution.

Solution

Mass of solution (salt) = 40 g
Mass of solvent (water) = 320 g
We know,
Mass of solution = Mass of solute + Mass of solvent
= 40 g + 320 g
= 360 g
Mass percentage of solution
{tex}=\frac{\text { Mass of solute }}{\text { Mass of solution }} \times 100{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{40}{360} \times 100=11.1 \%{/tex}

sections.label: In Text
sections.html:
Q 1

What is meant by a substance?

Solution

A substance is a matter which has a specific composition and specific properties.Every pure element or pure compound is a substance. A substance can not be separated by any physical method. The properties/composition of a pure substance is fixed.

Q 2

List the points of difference between homogeneous and heterogeneous mixtures.

Solution
 

Homogeneous mixture

Heterogeneous mixture
(i) Homogeneous mixtures have a uniform composition throughout the mixture. Heterogeneous mixtures do not have a uniform composition throughout the mixture.
(ii) Their components cannot be separated by filtration. Separation of constituents requiresmethods like distillation. Their components can be separated by filtration.
(iii) E.g. Salt & water solution, Sugar & water solution. E.g. Amixture of sulphur powder and iron fillings, A mixture of kerosene oil and water.
Q 3

Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous mixtures with examples.

Solution
Homogeneous mixture Heterogeneous mixture
These are called as solutions. These are called as suspensions/colloids.
Substances are Uniformly distributed. These substances are Unevenly distributed.
These are not visible to the naked eye, but visible through the microscope. These are easily visible to the naked eye and also through microscope.
The particles appear smaller in size. The particles are either smaller or larger in size.
These are pure substances. These are not pure substances.
They represent same physical properties. They do not possess same physical properties.
Examples include milk, gasoline, sugar solution, corn oil, fog etc Examples are mixture of mud & water, beach sand, vinegar, air cloud  etc
Q 4

How are a sol, a solution and a suspension different from each other?

Solution
 

Sol

Solution

Suspension
(i) A sol is a type of a colloidal solution. A solution is a kind of homogeneous mixture where the particles are in a fine state. A suspension is a kind of heterogeneous mixture where insoluble particles are spread throughout a solvent.
(ii) It has a dispersed phase (solid) and a dispersion phase (liquid).  A solution has a soluble solute and a solvent phase. The particles of solute do not settle down It has insoluble solute suspended in the solvent medium. The particles of a suspension settle down when left undisturbed for some time.
(iii) It appears homogeneous but is heterogeneous. It is not transparent. It is homogeneous, clear and transparent. It is heterogeneous in nature. It is not transparent.
(iv) Particles are visible with the help of an electron microscope. Particles are not visible to the naked eye. Particles are visible to the naked eyes.
(v) Particle size is between 10-7 cm and 10-5 cm. Particle size is of the order of 10-9 cm. Particle size is more than 10-5 cm.
(vi) E.g. Gold sol, Milk of magnesia E.g. Sugar solution, Salt solution E.g. Muddy river water, Dust storm
Q 5

To make a saturated solution, 36 g of sodium chloride is dissolved in 100 g of water at 293 K. Find its concentration at this temperature.

Solution

Mass of solute (Sodium chloride) = 36g
Mass of solvent (water) = 100g
Mass of solution = Mass of Solute + Mass of solvent
= 36g + 100g = 136g
Concentration = {tex}(\frac {\text{ mass of solute}}{\text{mass of solution}}){/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100
= {tex}(\frac {36g}{136g}) \times{/tex} 100 = 26.47%

Q 6

Classify the following as chemical or physical changes:-

  1. Cutting of trees
  2. Melting of butter in a pan
  3. Rusting of almirah
  4. Boiling of water to form steam
  5. Passing of electric current through water and the water breaking down into hydrogen and oxygen gases
  6. Dissolving common salt in water
  7. Making a fruit salad with raw fruits
  8. Burning of paper and wood
Solution

If the chemical composition is changing then it is a chemical change.

i. Cutting of trees Physical change
ii. Melting of butter in a pan Physical change
iii. Rusting of almirah Chemical change
iv. Boiling of water to form steam Physical change
v. Passing of electric current through water and the water breaking down into hydrogen and oxygen gases Chemical change
vi. Dissolving common salt in water Physical change
vii. Making a fruit salad with raw fruits Physical change
viii. Burning of paper and wood Chemical change
Q 7

Try segregating the things around you as pure substances or mixtures.

Solution

Pure substances- Water, bread, sugar, and gold.
Mixtures- Steel, plastic, paper, talc, milk, and air.

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Which separation techniques will you apply for the separation of the following?

  1. Sodium chloride from its solution in water.
  2. Ammonium chloride from a mixture containing sodium chloride and ammonium chloride.
  3. Small pieces of metal in the engine oil of a car.
  4. Different pigments from an extract of flower petals.
  5. Butter from curd.
  6. Oil from water.
  7. Tea leaves from tea.
  8. Iron pins from sand.
  9. Wheat grains from husk.
  10. Fine mud particles suspended in water.
Solution
  Mixture Separation technique
(a) Sodium chloride from its solution in water Evaporation of water
(b) Ammonium chloride from a mixture containing sodium chloride and ammonium chloride Sublimation of ammonium chloride
(c) Small pieces of metal in the engine oil of a car Filtration
(d)

Different pigments from an extract of flower petals

 Chromatography
(e) Butter from curd Centrifugation of curd
(f) Oil from water Separating funnel
(g) Tea leaves from tea Filtration by using a strainer
(h) Iron pins from sand With the help of a magnet
(i)

Wheat grains from husk

 Winnowing
(j) Fine mud particles suspended in water Centrifugation
Q 2

Write the steps you would use for making tea. Use the words solution, solvent, solute, dissolve, soluble, insoluble, filtrate and residue.

Solution

Steps that would be used for making tea are as follows:-

  1. The solvent used for making tea is water. Take some amount of solvent in a pan and boil it for few minutes.
  2. After the solvent is sufficiently warm, add a little amount of sugar to the solvent. Sugar is used as a solute to provide a sweet taste to the solvent.
  3. The solute will dissolve completely in the solvent and form a true solution.
  4. Add some tea leaves to the true solution. The chemical substances present in the tea leaves are soluble and will dissolve in the solution.
  5. Pour some milk solute into the pan. The milk will dissolve in the solution.
  6. Bring the solution to a boil and switch off the burner. The tea leaves will remain insoluble in the solution.
  7. Filter the solution with a strainer to separate the insoluble tea leaves.
  8. After filtration, the tea solution will be obtained as a filtrate. the insoluble tea leaves that remain as residue can be thrown away.
Q 3

Pragya tested the solubility of three different substances at different temperatures and collected the data as given below (results are given in the following table, as grams of substance dissolved in 100 grams of water to form a saturated solution).

Temperature in K
Substance dissolved 283 293 313 333 353
Solubility
Potassium Nitrate 21 32 62 106 167
Sodium Chloride 36 36 36 37 37
Potassium Chloride 35 35 40 46 54
Ammonium Chloride 24 37 41 55 66
  1. What mass of potassium nitrate would be needed to produce a saturated solution of potassium nitrate in 50 grams of water at 313 K?
  2. Pragya makes a saturated solution of potassium chloride in water at 353 K and leaves the solution to cool at room temperature. What would she observe as the solution cools? Explain.
  3. Find the solubility of each salt at 293 K. Which salt has the highest solubility at this temperature?
  4. What is the effect of change of temperature on the solubility of a salt?
Solution
  1. The amount of potassium nitrate required to produce a saturated solution at 313 K in 100 g of water = 62 g
    The amount of potassium nitrate that would be required to produce a saturated solution at 313 K in 50 g of water = (62 x 50) / 100 g
    Therefore, 31 g of potassium nitrate would be required to produce a saturated solution at 313 K in 50 g of water.
  2. At 373 K, preparation of a saturated solution will need 54 g of potassium nitrate. At a room temperature of 293 K, a saturated solution of potassium nitrate requires just 35 g potassium nitrate. As the solution cools, excess potassium nitrate (54 g – 35 g = 19 g) will precipitate out as insoluble salt.
  3. Solubility of potassium nitrate, sodium chloride, potassium chloride and ammonium chloride in 100 g of water at 293 K are 32 g, 36 g, 35 g and 37 g respectively.
    Ammonium chloride has the highest solubility (37 g) at this temperature.
  4. Effect of change of temperature on the solubility of a salt: As a general rule, the solubility of the salts is directly proportional to the temperature. If the temperature is increased, the solubility of the salt generally increases.
Q 4

Explain the Saturated solution.

Solution

A solution that has dissolved as much solute as it is capable of dissolving at a particular temperature is called a saturated solution. A saturated solution cannot dissolve more of solute particles at a particular temperature.

Q 5

Explain the Pure substance.

Solution

A pure substance is a substance which contains particles of only one kind and has a definite set of properties.
e.g. Sugar, salt, water and nitrogen are pure substances.

Q 6

Explain the Colloid.

Solution

A colloid is a kind of heterogeneous mixture/solution in which the particle size is between 10-7cm and 10-5cm such that the solute particles neither dissolve nor settle down. Colloids have a dispersion medium and a dispersed phase. E.g. Smoke, milk, shaving cream, jelly, cheese, etc.

Q 7

Explain the Suspension.

Solution

Suspension is a kind of heterogeneous mixture in which insoluble solid particles remain suspended in the medium and dispersed particles are visible to unaided eyes. The particles have a tendency to settle down and can be filtered out. E.g. Muddy river water, chalk powder in water, dust storm, sand in water, etc.

Q 8

Classify each of the following as a homogeneous or a heterogeneous mixture.
soda water, wood, air, soil, vinegar, filtered tea.

Solution

Homogeneous mixtures: Soda water, air, vinegar, filtered tea
Heterogeneous mixtures: Wood, soil

Q 9

How would you confirm that a colourless liquid given to you is pure water?

Solution

This can be confirmed by the following experiments.

  1. Filter the colourless liquid through a very fine filter paper. If no residue is left on the filter paper, this means that the liquid is pure water and has no suspended impurities present in it.
  2. Evaporate the colourless liquid in a china dish or beaker. In case no residue is left, this means that it is pure water and has no dissolved impurities present in it.
  3. Determine the boiling point of pure liquid. If it comes out to be nearly 373 K (100oC), this means that the pure liquid is water.
Q 10

Which of the following materials fall in the category of a pure substance?

  1. Ice
  2. Milk
  3. Iron
  4. Hydrochloric acid
  5. Calcium oxide
  6. Mercury
  7. Brick
  8. Wood
  9. Air
Solution

Ice, iron, hydrochloric acid, calcium oxide, and mercury are pure substances. 
Pure substances are homogeneous material that contains particles of one kind of atom or molecules and have a definite set of properties.

Q 11

Identify the solutions among the following mixtures:-

  1. Soil
  2. Sea water
  3. Air
  4. Coal
  5. Soda water
Solution

The solution is a homogeneous mixture of two or more substances. Among the given mixtures solution are as follows:-
(b) Sea water: Seawater can be considered as a solution since it has dissolved salts (solid solute) in water (liquid solvent).
(c) Air: Air is a gas-in-gas solution.
(e) Soda water: Aerated drinks like soda water are gas-in-liquid solutions. Soda water contains carbon dioxide as a gaseous solute and water as a liquid solvent.

Q 12

Which of the following will show Tyndall effect?

  1. Salt solution
  2. Milk
  3. Copper sulphate solution
  4. Starch solution.
Solution

Tyndall effect is shown by colloidal solutions. It is not shown by true solutions or suspensions. Milk and starch solution has larger particles since they are not true solutions, so they will show Tyndall effect. 
Salt solution and copper sulphate solution are true solutions and the size of their particles is very small so as to scatter a beam of light.

Q 13

Classify the following into elements, compounds and mixtures:

  1. Sodium
  2. Soil
  3. Sugar solution
  4. Silver
  5. Calcium carbonate
  6. Tin
  7. Silicon
  8. Coal
  9. Air
  10. Soap
  11. Methane
  12. Carbon dioxide
  13. Blood
Solution

Classification of the given substances into elements compounds and mixtures:

Elements Compounds Mixtures
Sodium Calcium Sugar solution
Silver Methane Soil
Tin Carbon dioxide Coal
Silicon Soap Air, Blood
Q 14

Which of the following are chemical changes?

  1. Growth of a plant
  2. Rusting of iron
  3. Mixing of iron filings and sand
  4. Cooking of food
  5. Digestion of food
  6. Freezing of water
  7. Burning of a candle
Solution

A chemical change is a change that brings a change in the chemical properties of matter and a new substance is formed.
Rusting of iron, cooking of food, digestion of food, burning of a candle are chemical changes.

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1
  1. Calculate the relative molecular mass of water (H2O).
  2. Calculate the molecular mass of HNO3.
Solution
  1. Atomic mass of hydrogen = 1u, oxygen = 16 u
    So the molecular mass of water, which contains two atoms of hydrogen and one atom of oxygen is
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 16
    = 18 u
    The molecular mass of water = 18 u
  2. The molecular mass of HNO3 = the atomic mass of H + the atomic mass of N + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} the atomic mass of O
    = 1 + 14 + 48 = 63 u
    The molecular mass of HNO3  = 63 u
Q 2

Calculate the formula unit mass of CaCl2.

Solution

Atomic mass of Ca + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} atomic mass of Cl) = 40 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 35.5 = 40 + 71 = 111 u

sections.label: In Text
sections.html:
Q 1

In a reaction, 5.3 g of sodium carbonate reacted with 6 g of acetic acid. The products were 2.2 g of carbon dioxide, 0.9 g water and 8.2 g of sodium acetic. Show that these observations are in agreement with the law of conservation of mass.
sodium carbonate + acetic acid {tex} \to{/tex} sodium acetic + carbon dioxides + water

Solution

According to the law of conservation of mass:
mass of reactants = mass of products
Let's calculate and find out both results –

  1. mass of reactants = mass of sodium carbonate + mass of acetic acid
    = 5.3 g + 6 g
    = 11.3 g
  2. mass of products = mass of sodium ethanoate + mass of carbon dioxide + mass of water
    = 8.2 g +2.2 g + 0.9 g = 11.3 g
    We find that the mass of reactants is 11.3 g and the mass of product is also 11.3 g. Hence it is proved that these observations are in agreement with the law of conservation of mass.
Q 2

Hydrogen and oxygen combine in the ratio of 1:8 by mass to form water. What mass of oxygen gas would be required to react completely with 3 g of hydrogen gas?

Solution

Here we have been given that hydrogen and oxygen always combine in the fixed ratio of 1:8 by mass. This means that:

1 g of hydrogen gas requires = 8 g of oxygen gas.

so, 3 g of hydrogen gas requires = {tex}3 \times 8 {/tex} g of oxygen gas

                                               = 24 g of oxygen gas

Therefore, the mass of oxygen gas required to react completely with 3 g of hydrogen gas will be.{tex}24g{/tex}

Q 3

Which postulate of Dalton’s atomic theory is the result of the law of conservation of mass?

Solution

The postulate of Dalton’s atomic theory which is the result of the law of conservation of mass is mentioned as below:

Atoms are indivisible particles, which cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction.

Q 4

Which postulate of Dalton’s atomic theory can explain the law of definite proportions?

Solution

The postulate of Dalton's atomic theory which explains the law of definite proportions is “Atoms combine in the ratio of small whole numbers to form compounds and the relative number and kinds of atoms are constant in a given compound.”

Q 5

Define the atomic mass unit.

Solution

According to the latest recommendations of the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), the atomic mass unit (amu) is abbreviated as u or unified mass.

 For chemical calculations, the atomic masses of elements are expressed by taking the atomic mass of one atom of an element as the standard mass.  One atomic mass unit (1 u) is defined as exactly one-twelfth the mass of an atom of Carbon-12 atom. 1 amu = 1/12 of atomic masses of {tex}_6{C^{12}}{/tex}

Q 6

Why is it not possible to see an atom with naked eyes?

Solution

An atom is an extremely minute particle and as such actual mass of an atom of hydrogen is considered to be {tex}1.6 \times {10^{ - 24}}g{/tex}. That is why it is not possible to see an atom with naked eyes.

Q 7

Write down the formulae of:

  1. sodium oxide
  2. Aluminium chloride
  3. sodium Sulphide
  4. magnesium hydroxide
Solution
  1. Sodium oxide
    Symbols = Na, Valency = 1
    Symbols = O, Valency = 2
    cross over valencies
    Na2O1 or Na2O
    Thus, the formula of sodium oxide compound is  Na2O.
  2. Aluminium chloride
    Symbols = Al, Valency = 3
    Symbols = Cl, Valency = 1
    cross over valencies
    Al1Cl3 or AlCl3
    Thus, the formula of  Aluminium chloride compound is AlCl3
  3. Sodium Sulphide
    Symbols = Na, Valency = 1
    Symbols = S, Valency = 2
    cross over valencies
    Na2S1 or Na2S
    Thus, the formula of Sodium Sulphide compound is Na2S.
  4. Magnesium hydroxide
    Symbols = Mg, Valency = 2
    Symbols = OH, Valency = 1
    cross over valencies
    Mg1(OH)2 or Mg(OH)2  
    Thus, the formula of magnesium hydroxide compound is Mg(OH)2.
Q 8

Write down the names of compounds represented by following formulae:

  1. Al2(SO4)3
  2. CaCl2
  3.  K2SO4
  4. KNO3
  5. CaCO3
Solution
  1. Al2(SO4)3 is aluminium sulphate. Ions present are Al3+ and Cl-
  2. CaCl2 is calcium chloride. Ions present are Ca+ and {tex}SO_4^{2 - }{/tex}
  3. K2SO4 is potassium sulphate. Ions present are K+ and {tex}SO_4^{2 - }{/tex}
  4. KNO3 is potassium nitrate. Ions present are K+ and {tex}NO_3^ - {/tex}
  5. CaCO3  is calcium carbonate. Ions present are Ca2+ and {tex}CO_3^{2 - }{/tex}
Q 9

What is meant by the term chemical formula?

Solution

A chemical formula is the representation of elements present in a compound with the help of symbols and also the number of atoms of each element with those numbers only. for eg: A molecule of water (compound) contains 2 atoms of hydrogen and one atom of oxygen hence its chemical formula is H2O

Q 10

How many atoms are present in a:

  1. H2S molecule and
  2. {tex}PO_4^{3 - }{/tex} ion?
Solution
  1. 2 atoms of hydrogen + 1 atom of sulphur = 3 atoms. Hence 3 atoms are present in an  H2S molecule.
  2. 1 atom of phosphorus + 4 atoms of oxygen = 5 atoms. Hence 5 atoms are present in an ion {tex}PO_4^{3 - }{/tex}.
Q 11

Calculate the molecular masses of H2, O2, Cl2, CO2, CH4, C2H6, C2H4, NH3, CH3OH.

Solution
  1. Molecular mass of H2= atomic mass of {tex}H \times 2{/tex} ={tex}1 \times 2{/tex} = 2u
  2. Molecular mass of O2= atomic mass of {tex}O \times 2{/tex} = {tex}16 \times 2{/tex} = 32u.
  3. Molecular mass of Cl2= atomic mass of {tex}Cl \times 2{/tex} = {tex}35.5 \times 2{/tex} = 71u.
  4. Molecular mass of CO2= atomic mass of C + (atomic mass of O {tex}\times{/tex} 2)
    = (12 + 32)
    = 44 u
  5. Molecular mass of CH4= 12 + atomic mass of hydrogen {tex}\times4{/tex}
    = 12 + (1{tex}\times{/tex}4)
    = 16 u
  6. Molecular mass of C2H6= {tex}\left( {12 \times 2} \right) + \left( {1 \times 6} \right){/tex}
    =24+6
    =30 u
  7. Molecular mass of C2H4= {tex}\left( {12 \times 2} \right) + \left( {1 \times 4} \right){/tex}
    =24 + 4
    =28 u
  8. Molecular mass of NH3= {tex}14 + \left( {1 \times 3} \right){/tex}=14 + 3= 17 u
  9. Molecular mass of CH3OH= {tex}12 + \left( {1 \times 3} \right){/tex} +16+1 =12+3+16+1 = 32 u
Q 12

Calculate the formula unit masses of ZnO, Na2O, K2CO3, given atomic masses of Zn = 65 u, Na = 23 u, K = 39 u, C = 12 u, and O = 16 u.

Solution

Given atomic masses of Zn = 65 u, Na = 23 u, K = 39 u, C = 12 u, and O = 16 u.

Formula unit mass of:
i) ZnO = Atomic mass of Zn + atomic mass of O

         = (65 + 16) u = 81 u

ii) Na2O =Atomic mass of Na + atomic mass of O

            = {tex}\left( {23 \times 2} \right){/tex}+16= 46 + 16 = 62 u

iii) K2CO3= Atomic mass of K2 + Atomic mass of C + Atomic mass of O

              = {tex}\left( {39 \times 2} \right){/tex} + 12 + {tex}\left( {16 \times 3} \right){/tex}= 78 + 12 + 48 = 138 u

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

A 0.24 g sample of compound of oxygen and boron was found by analysis to contain 0.096 g of boron and 0.144 g of oxygen. Calculate the percentage composition of the compound by weight.

Solution

Mass of the given sample compound = 0.24 g
Mass of boron in the given sample compound = 0.096 g
Mass of oxygen in the given sample compound = 0.144 g
{tex}\%{/tex} composition of compound = {tex}\%{/tex} of boron and {tex}\%{/tex} of oxygen
Therefore {tex}\%{/tex} of boron = mass of boron 100/mass of the sample compound {tex}0.096 \times \frac{{100}}{{0.24}}{/tex}
= 40 percent
Therefore {tex}\%{/tex} of oxygen = mass of oxygen  100/mass of the sample compound
{tex}0.144 \times \frac{{100}}{{0.24}}{/tex}
= 60 percent

Q 2

When 3.0 g of carbon is burnt in 8.00 g oxygen, 11.00 g of carbon dioxide is produced. What mass of carbon dioxide will be formed when 3.00 g of carbon is burnt in 50.00 g of oxygen? Which law of chemical combination will govern your answer?

Solution

Our answer will be based on the law of constant proportion. Since carbon and oxygen combine in the fixed proportion of 3:8 by mass to produce 11 g of carbon dioxide, therefore, the same mass of carbon dioxide (11g) will be obtained even if we burn 3g of carbon in 50 g of oxygen. The extra oxygen (50 - 8 = 42 g oxygen ) will remain unreacted. 

Q 3

What are polyatomic ions? Give examples .

Solution

Polyatomic ions are the group of atoms which carry either positive charge (cations) or negative charge (anions). For example,
(i) Carbonate ion (CO3)2-   (ii) Nitrate ion (NO3)-   (iii) Ammonium ion (NH4)+   (iv) Phosphate ion (PO4)3-.

Q 4

Write the chemical formulae of the following:

  1. Magnesium chloride
  2. Calcium oxide
  3. Copper nitrate
  4. Aluminium chloride
  5. Calcium carbonate.
Solution

Chemical formulae of 

  1. Magnesium chloride
    Symbols = Mg, Valency = 2
    Symbols = Cl, Valency = 1
    cross-over valencies
    Mg1Cl2 or MgCl2
    Thus, the formula of magnesium chloride is MgCl2.
  2. Calcium oxide
    Symbols = Ca, Valency = 2
    Symbols = O, Valency = 2
    cross-over valencies
    Ca2O2 or CaO
    Thus, the formula of Calcium oxide is  CaO.
  3. Copper(II) nitrate
    Symbols = Cu, Valency = 2
    Symbols = NO3, Valency = 1
    cross-over valencies
    Cu1(NO3)2 or Cu(NO3)2
    Thus, the formula of copper nitrate is  Cu(NO3)2.
  4. Aluminium chloride
    Symbols = Al, Valency = 3
    Symbols = Cl, Valency = 1
    cross-over valencies
    Al1Cl3 or AlCl3
    Thus, the formula of  Aluminium chloride is AlCl3
  5. Calcium carbonate.
    Symbols = Ca, Valency = 2
    Symbols = CO3, Valency = 2
    cross-over valencies
    Ca2(CO3)2 or CaCO3
    Thus, the formula of  Calcium carbonate is CaCO3.
Q 5

Give the names of the elements present in the following compounds.

  1. Quick lime
  2. Hydrogen bromide
  3. Baking powder
  4. Potassium sulphate
Solution

Compound

formula

Element present

Quick lime

CaO

Calcium and oxygen

Hydrogen bromide

HBr

Hydrogen and bromine

Baking powder

NaHCO3

Sodium, hydrogen, carbon and oxygen

Potassium sulphate

K2SO4

Potassium, sulphur and oxygen
Q 6

Calculate the molar mass of the following substances.

  1. Ethyne, C2H2 
  2. Sulphur molecule, S8
  3. Phosphorus molecule, P4 (Atomic mass of phosphorus= 31)
  4. Hydrochloric acid, HCl
  5. Nitric acid, HNO3
Solution
  1. Ethyene = C2H2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} mass of carbon atom + 2× mass of hydrogen atom|
    ={tex}12 \times 2 + 1 \times 2{/tex} = 24 + 2
    = 26 u =26 g
  2. Sulphur molecular = S8
    =8×mass of sulphur atom
    ={tex}8 \times 32{/tex}
    = 256 u= 256 g
  3. Phosphorus molecule = P4
    =4 {tex}\times{/tex} phosphorus atom
    ={tex}4 \times 31{/tex}
    = 124 u= 124 g
  4. Hydrochloric acid = HCl
    = mass of hydrogen atom + mass of chlorin atom
    = 1 + 35.5
    = 36.5 u = 36.5 g
  5. Nitric acid = HNO3
    = mass of hydrogen atom + mass of nitrogen atom + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} mass of oxygen atom
    = 1 + 14 + {tex}16 \times 3{/tex}= 15 + 48
    = 63 u =63 g

sections.label: In Text
sections.html:
Q 1

What are canal rays?

Solution

Canal rays also called anode rays, are seen moving from the anode towards the cathode in the specially designed discharge tube. However, they do not originate from the anode. They were discovered by Goldstein in 1886.

Q 2

If an atom contains one electron and one proton, will it carry any charge or not?

Solution

An electron is a negatively charged particle, whereas a proton is a positively charged particle. The magnitude of their charges is equal. Therefore, an atom containing one electron and one proton will not carry any charge. Thus, it will be a neutral atom.

Q 3

On the basis of Thomson's model of an atom, explain how the atom is neutral as a whole.

Solution

As per Thomson’s model of an atom, the number of electrons (negatively-charged particles) inside an atom is equal to the number of protons (positively-charged particles). The amount of charge on an electron and that on a proton is equal in magnitude. Hence, the positive and negative charges are balanced by each other. The balancing of equal and opposite charges makes the atom neutral as a whole.

Q 4

On the basis of Rutherford's model of an atom, which sub-atomic particle is present in the nucleus of an atom?

Solution

The scientific idea is that an atom has electrons surrounding a nucleus that contains protons and neutrons. The nucleus of atom is positively charged according to Rutherford's model of an atom. All the proton's in an atom are therefore, present in the nucleus.

Q 5

Draw a sketch of Bohr's model of an atom with three shells.

Solution

Bohr's model of an atom with three shells:
The three stationary orbits are designated as K-shell (nearest to the nucleus), M-shell and N-shell.
The atom with three shells can accommodate a maximum of 2, 8 and 18 electrons respectively.

Q 6

What do you think would be the observation if the {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle scattering experiment is carried out using a foil of a metal other than gold?

Solution

The aim of Rutherford’s experiment was to test the Thomson’s plum-pudding model. Rutherford used a very thin sheet or foil of gold (0.00006 cm in thickness) because gold is the most malleable metal. The {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles are much smaller than the gold atoms in the foil. If any other metal other than gold had been used in the experiment, there would not have been any difference between the size of the alpha-particles and the metal atoms and therefore there would not have been any penetration of alpha-particles through the foil.

Q 7
